blogs.blogTitle: First Impressions: 5 Factors That Recruiters Should Know
blogs.blogSummary: You can't judge a book by its cover, as we all know. And yet, we persist in doing it. The first time you see a potential candidate, you may feel the need to form an opinion about them immediately. Within a few of seconds, you have already made a number of snap decisions, both consciously and unconsciously.
blogs.blogDescp:
You can't judge a book by its cover, as we all know. And yet, we persist in doing it.


The first time you see a potential candidate, you may feel the need to form an opinion about them immediately. Within a few of seconds, you have already made a number of snap decisions, both consciously and unconsciously. You can see she is certain of herself. The guy has clearly put in a long day. It's clear that she's been under stress. It appears like he's quite bright. Your first impression of the interview is quite accurate.


The underlying issue is whether or not you should believe that first reaction. Fortunately, there is now a plethora of studies in the field of psychology that examine the impact of first impressions, or "thin-slicing" as it is more well called. What's more, it implies that the impressions you make on others (and the impressions others make of you) are very consistent with each other. However, it also advises that we moderate our judgements, which are frequently manifestations of our unconscious bias, in order to arrive at conclusions that are more equitable and fruitful.


1. One's first impression may be eerily similar to the impression they leave with others


It was discovered that the adage was incorrect. First impressions are usually quite spot on. The end of the article.


Humour intended. Let's get into the science behind it a bit more.


"Research has found that first impressions are surprisingly valid," said Nobel winner and superstar psychologist Daniel Kahneman. As one individual put it, "You can predict very quickly whether you like a person and if others will."

According to research, a potential employer can tell if an applicant will be offered the job within the first 15 seconds of the interview. In agreement with this finding, another research found that "a short excerpt of a job interview can be predictive of hireability." Another research showed that employment judgements made "after viewing 12 seconds of silent interviewee behavior" were consistent with those made following the whole interview.


How did you make a good impression on potential employers? Candidates who "appeared attentive (rather than anxious), competent, confident, dominant, optimistic, and professional" "were more likely to receive positive hiring recommendations."


Another research, this one concentrating on professors, discovered that students' evaluations of their instructors after just 30 seconds of silent teaching were very comparable to those of students who had taken a full semester of classes with their instructors.


This means that initial impressions are often accurate depictions of how others feel about you. However, it isn't necessarily a good thing, as we'll discover.  


2. Prejudice may color first judgements


The above data shows that our first perceptions of a person are very indicative of how we'll ultimately feel about them. But let's be clear: liking someone has nothing to do with their potential to do a good job. Likeability is essential in several fields, such as sales. However, the applicant you think is stupid might really be the most brilliant programmer in the world.


There is room for prejudice here. A person's appearance may have a significant and, often, unjustifiable impact on how you perceive them. Studies have shown that candidates who are even slightly overweight have a far lower chance of being hired, and this is particularly true for women.


Another research indicated that recruiters value taller applicants more, with an extra $800 added to their yearly income for every inch of height. This means that someone who is 5 feet and 5 inches tall would make $166,000 less than someone who is 6 feet tall over the course of their 30-year career. Despite being equally or less qualified than their less beautiful counterparts, several studies have shown that handsome persons are more likely to be employed and awarded greater beginning salary.


Recognizing your own hidden biases might help you find fantastic underappreciated prospects, much like Moneyball.


3. A bad initial impression might set off a chain of unfortunate events


As we've seen, there are times when a negative first impression is warranted. To make matters worse, getting out on the wrong foot may lead to a downward spiral.


According to research conducted at Cornell University, people tend to confirm their first perceptions through their actions and body language. The applicant will sense this and react appropriately. Even more concerning is the fact that destructive emotions feed off of themselves. If you get off to a horrible start, the prospect will likely mirror your attitude and be distant, uncomfortable, and disinterested.

If you worry that you could be giving out a negative first impression, suppress such feelings as much as possible. Consider the candidate's intentions with an open mind and behave as if they're succeeding - this may help bring out the best in them and lead to a reevaluation of your first opinion.


4. If you're a recruiter, giving in to early impressions might damage your effectiveness


First impressions are important, but they may backfire on you if you give the wrong impression. In fact, your performance suffers when the applicant creates a favorable impression. Recruiters' behavior changes when they have a positive impression of an applicant, much as the self-fulfilling prophesy described in the preceding research. One research found that when interviewers "followed up positive first impressions... by showing positive regard towards the applicant," they were more likely to "sell" the organization and the position to the candidate while also asking fewer questions.


Because of the bright lights, the recruiters start acting more like salespeople and asking less questions. Recruiters serve as both detectives and cheerleaders; although selling the firm won't affect their effectiveness, limiting their access to information would. If initial impressions accurately predicted performance, then prioritizing sales above investigation may be understandable.


Don't be fooled. Pick up your jaw from the floor, ask additional questions, and examine the prospect critically if you believe they're amazing. Put your emotions aside and look at the situation objectively.


5. Your introduction is crucial


You're aware that the applicant is anxious to create a good impression; yet, first impressions work both ways. A recent survey found that 80% of job seekers made decisions based on the relationships they had with interviewers.


So, what can we conclude about the power of initial impressions? Keep your faith, but check it twice.

While first impressions are often accurate predictors of how you'll feel about someone over time, you should keep your professional judgment separate from your personal ones. Recognize your weaknesses, make it a priority to improve in those areas, and don't overlook the importance of a strong personal presentation.
blogs.pageTitle: First Impressions: 5 Factors That Recruiters Should Know
blogs.pageKeywords: First Impressions: 5 Factors That Recruiters Should Know
blogs.pageDescp: First Impressions: 5 Factors That Recruiters Should Know

blogs.blogTitle: A Case for Dropping 'Bachelor's Degree' from Job Requirements
blogs.blogSummary: Even if the position doesn't strictly need tertiary education, it's more unusual to see "bachelor's degree" listed as a necessity.
blogs.blogDescp:
Even if the position doesn't strictly need tertiary education, it's more unusual to see "bachelor's degree" listed as a necessity.


In fact, a survey from Harvard Business School found that administrative assistant and production supervisor positions were particularly affected by degree inflation in the United States. Despite the fact that the great majority of individuals now working in such fields do not have a bachelor's degree, the vast majority of job listings for these occupations now specify that candidates must have one. For instance, whereas 67% of available production supervisor roles need a bachelor's degree, just 16% of already employed supervisors have earned one.


This tendency, however, may soon be reversed due to the increasing cost of higher education and the mistrust it is receiving from members of Generation Z. Companies including IBM, EY, and Penguin Random House have recently altered their recruiting practices by no longer prioritizing applicants with a four-year degree.


Why? Business elites are beginning to question the conventional wisdom that holding a bachelor's degree is a prerequisite for professional "success." Consider that many very successful individuals (Steve Jobs included) did not go to college. Given the rapid evolution of job duties, the capacity to adapt and learn quickly may prove to be of paramount importance. In addition, you may benefit from recruiting people who didn't go to university (more on this later).


Here are four justifications for giving applicants who lack a bachelor's degree a second look:


1. You may expand your pool of potential hires and improve retention by placing more emphasis on corporate training than on academic qualifications


A degree from a four-year university may not always translate into success in the workplace. You may teach your new workers all they need to know to be successful in your professional setting with a thorough corporate training program, but spending time on campus can help students gain certain soft skills and strengthen their critical thinking.


Considering that training programs have been demonstrated to enhance engagement and staff retention, this is extremely crucial. Putting new employees through a structured training program not only helps them learn the ropes but also boosts the likelihood that they will stay with the firm for the long haul.


2. Putting less weight on a candidate's level of schooling will make it easier to locate people from traditionally underrepresented groups


For good reason, diversity and inclusion programs have become one of the most pervasive recruitment trends of the last several years. Companies with a wide range of employees' races and ethnicities have been shown to outperform those with a narrower range of employees' backgrounds.


It may be possible to increase team diversity by hiring people who do not have a four-year degree. You will not only increase the diversity of your workforce by hiring people from a variety of various walks of life, but you will also increase the number of applications from groups who are underrepresented in the workforce and are less likely to have college degrees, such as blacks and Hispanics.


3. Expanding your pool of candidates by paying attention to potential and soft skills


One of the biggest arguments for looking outside the traditional college student pool is that doing so increases your chances of discovering untapped potential.


In order to locate candidates with the proper potential and the necessary soft skills for the job, recruiters are increasingly looking beyond pedigree, which includes work experience, achievements, and credentials like college degrees. People with attributes like perseverance and optimism, or those with the proper character for a team leadership job, may be desirable hires.


Interview questions and neuroscience-based tests from companies like HireVue are just two examples of the many reliable methods available for gauging a candidate's soft skills and job appropriateness. In today's competitive job market, however, a bachelor's degree alone isn't enough to guarantee success.


4. Finding applicants who are willing to learn and have technical ability is key to filling skill shortages


Recruiters have run into difficulty in recent years due to a lack of qualified candidates, particularly for dynamic technology positions. However, in a competitive job market, recruiters and their employers might gain an advantage by being open to applicants who lack a four-year degree but have the abilities and ability to succeed in new areas.


In reality, rapid changes in firms' skill needs contribute to skills gaps by producing recent graduates who lack expertise in in-demand areas.


Companies like IBM, Github, and Intel are among those that are going beyond formal education to identify individuals with technological talent. People who have completed intensive training programs in a short amount of time, such coding boot camps, are examples of the types of employees included in this category.


In conclusion, it may seem contradictory, but not having a degree may really be an advantage


One solid reason to consider recruiting someone without a four-year degree is to have a more diverse workforce, one with distinct viewpoints and different backgrounds. Getting rid of degree requirements is a competitive advantage in today's job market and may help your organization effectively address persistent issues, such as talent shortages.
blogs.pageTitle: A Case for Dropping 'Bachelor's Degree' from Job Requirements
blogs.pageKeywords: A Case for Dropping 'Bachelor's Degree' from Job Requirements
blogs.pageDescp: A Case for Dropping 'Bachelor's Degree' from Job Requirements

blogs.blogTitle: A Look Back to the Oldies for Some Useful Advice on Recruiting
blogs.blogSummary: These facts were all true when I first began working as a recruiter: You would only need to offer two or three exceptional applicants if you were familiar with the specific job requirements and the hiring manager. In most cases, a conversation with...
blogs.blogDescp:
These facts were all true when I first began working as a recruiter:


  1. You would only need to offer two or three exceptional applicants if you were familiar with the specific job requirements and the hiring manager.
  2. In most cases, a conversation with 8-10 solid prospects will provide the names of two or three promising possibilities. In most cases, recommendations from the prospect pool accounted for half of these exceptionally qualified individuals.
  3. The key resource for locating these first prospects was an out-of-date business directory organized by sector that identified goods, primary phone numbers, and the names of top executives.
  4. After just four or five days of calling, we had two or three excellent prospects. Once you've established a substantial network of promising leads, this time frame may be reduced to two or three days.
  5. There was a cost associated with each employment in the form of the search charge, but if the new employee was very productive, that cost would be minimal.


The high-touch, "quality is #1" strategy was falling out of favor in the early 2000s due to the rise of ATS and online job boards in the 1990s. Some businesses falsely believed they could "win the war for talent" by employing tools to cut down on hiring expenses.


During the last quarter-century, we have seen a rapid rise in the use of "high-tech" to replace "high-touch" methods. The number of large job boards offering to provide suitable individuals within days increased, and new applicant tracking systems (ATS) and artificial intelligence (AI) suppliers entered the market.


This massive and ever-increasing investment in technology has yielded disappointing results. Very little data suggests progress in any of the following areas: hire quality; cost per hire; time to fill; employee retention; job satisfaction; or productivity. It's not hard to see why: Since any interested person might now apply for a job, additional technological defenses were required to keep things running smoothly. As a consequence, any gains in productivity were nullified by an increase in fixed expenses, and the quality of new hires suffered as a result.


Methods to integrate "newfangled" recruitment tools with more conventional approaches


  1. Technology may be used to recruit for performance and fit rather than skills and efficiency by incorporating old-fashioned, high-touch principles early in the process. The following are strategies our past customers have employed throughout this change.
  2. Instead of writing dull job descriptions, use engaging multimedia storytelling. The job description reads like a tale, but picture it accompanied with a video from the recruiting manager.
  3. Make the application procedure two-staged. Make a change to your application procedure such that individuals who want to be considered "official" candidates are required to provide a brief writing sample outlining an achievement that is most relevant to the demands of the position. See below for the justification in law. With only two steps, you can automatically remove 90% of applicants, saving time and money in the process.
  4. Communicate with fewer high-quality leads. You should focus your recruitment efforts on persons who meet your performance requirements, who exhibit the Achiever Pattern, and for whom your position represents a logical progression in their careers. This boosts the possibility that a recipient will reply to an email and improves the likelihood that recruiters will notice the candidates and make them an offer they can't refuse.
  5. Grow and make connections. Make a pitch for a preliminary chat rather than a job. By relaxing your timeline, making additional contacts, and doing things slowly, you may accomplish more and get better recommendations.
  6. People are screened by phone instead of video. Validate your phone interview with the candidate to see whether they have the skills you're looking for, will mesh with your company's culture, will push your talent bar higher, and will allow you to change the focus from salary to advancement opportunities. This is the crux of the high-touch process, and when done well, it boosts hiring quality while reducing noise, red tape, and costs.
  7. Evaluate not only current abilities but also future possibilities. Ask this one inquiry to find out whether they can back up their claims with action or simply speak the talk.


Focus on hiring for potential rather than immediate pay and convenience. Wait to provide a position until you know the candidate can respond "Forget the money, why do you want this job?" with confidence. The correct response must demonstrate an in-depth familiarity with the job's performance standards and the reasons why taking it would be a professional step forward.


Although it may seem counterintuitive, the finest individuals don't usually switch careers in the ways that technological advancements would have them do so. That's what sets them apart as the elite of society. You will find that this idea is just as relevant now as it was when hiring standards were more stringent.
blogs.pageTitle: A Look Back to the Oldies for Some Useful Advice on Recruiting
blogs.pageKeywords: A Look Back to the Oldies for Some Useful Advice on Recruiting
blogs.pageDescp: A Look Back to the Oldies for Some Useful Advice on Recruiting

blogs.blogTitle: What every recruiter needs to know about salary negotiations
blogs.blogSummary: Recruiters should never break this guideline while negotiating offers in order to increase acceptance rates and decrease the likelihood of counteroffers...
blogs.blogDescp:
Recruiters should never break this guideline while negotiating offers in order to increase acceptance rates and decrease the likelihood of counteroffers:


Make sure the applicant orally accepts your offer and all terms and conditions before putting pen to paper.


Recruiters and recruiting managers need to have strong offer negotiation skills in order to assemble high-quality teams. Everything you've done up to this point will be for nothing if you can't seal the sale. I believe that the ability to negotiate offers is just as crucial as the ability to source candidates, conduct interviews, and create a job analysis outlining the most significant KPOs for a position. In light of this, I'll share with you some of the most important strategies I've learned along the route to becoming a skilled negotiator.


1. Don't use salary as a deciding factor but as a negotiation chip

Don't bring up payment until the very last screen of the call. Top talent is always prepared to take a pay cut if it means taking the next step in their career. In an effort to save time, inexperienced recruiters often inquire about salary prior to determining whether or not the candidate is a good match for the position. The phone's screen is a great place to spy on potential development areas, such as new responsibilities or a shift in focus.


2. Rate yourself on a scale from 1 to 10

Ask the prospects you're interested in after the initial interview with the recruiting manager, "How does our job rank on a 1-10 scale compared to everything else you're considering including a possible counter-offer?" The next step is to figure out why you aren't first and what it would take to go up just one spot to the top spot on a scale from 1 to 10. This is how issues with the position are uncovered, and how they might be addressed in further interviews.


3. Investigate issues to find their root cause

It's crucial to figure out why a prospect has any reservations about working with you or decides against applying at all. Most of the time, the issue will just be a lack of comprehension or a reasonable worry that can be addressed. When you've located the source of the problem, just ask the affected party whether they are prepared to go to the next phase of the procedure if the issue is resolved to their satisfaction. If the candidate shows any reluctance, it's likely that the issue addressed is only a pretext for something else. If the issue is preventing the individual from progressing, it must be resolved.


4. Withdraw funds to gauge genuine enthusiasm

Ask the candidate whether they would still be interested in the position if the salary were to be eliminated a few days before extending an offer. The vast majority will agree. The next question to be asked is why. Don't make the offer if the candidate can't explain how taking the job would further their career or if they focus too much on the immediate perks of the position. This individual hasn't done their homework, and they won't be successful (in the sense of being happy and motivated) if they take your offer.


5. Take your time if an offer is turned down

If your offer is declined, you should inquire as soon as possible as to the reason(s) why. It's usually for a higher salary or some other immediate gain. If so, you might remark something like, "It seems you're making a strategic decision using tactical information." Then, you should clarify that the other individual has undervalued the better long-term advantages of your introduction. Faster development, a larger and more influential role, and a more varied and gratifying workload all fall into this category. You'll have to work extra hard to convince them that your position offers a better career path than the alternative option if these points weren't covered during the interview.


When initiating a requisition or conducting an in-depth interview to identify gaps that indicate a fair career step, negotiating the closure is just as vital as developing a performance profile. Long ago, I realized that no amount of financial resources could ever be sufficient to entice the most talented individuals. Still, you'll have greater success closing deals since you're providing a better chance of development. To make sure every prospect is aware of the distinctions, recruiters employ methods like offer testing and worry alleviation.
blogs.pageTitle: What every recruiter needs to know about salary negotiations

blogs.blogTitle: Pay Attention to These 3 Traits When Candidates Discuss Their Weaknesses and Mistakes
blogs.blogSummary: We've all had to deal with applicants who were unable, to be honest about their shortcomings. It's never a good indication, and it almost always kills the sale. This is particularly true if the problem persists and is severe.
blogs.blogDescp:
We've all had to deal with applicants who were unable, to be honest about their shortcomings. It's never a good indication, and it almost always kills the sale. This is particularly true if the problem persists and is severe.


However, sometimes we simply see glimpses of mild alarm. Take the case of a highly qualified applicant for a senior job on your team that you just completed interviewing. During the interview, the applicant mentioned a little disagreement he had with a classmate, but he was not particularly honest in taking responsibility for his (quite clear) role in escalating the situation.


Maybe this one of the candidates is acceptable near enough.


One of your coworkers answers, "No," since it's evidence that he kept something from you. "Honesty is one of our core values." Another team member had a very different view on the situation: "I don't think he was being deceptive, I just think he tends to focus on the positives and downplay the mistakes — it's not a great thing, but it's not deceptiveness." A third member of the group had an entirely different theory: "I don't think he forgot the mistake — he never realized he made one in the first place!" A lack of knowledge of one's own nature, in my opinion.


Which viewpoint is more accurate? How can we utilize our interview data to determine which of these three hypotheses is most likely to be correct? Let's begin by defining what we mean by "root cause."


  1. Insight into our own persona and presentation is compromised by a lack of self-awareness. It boils down to an incapacity to self-reflect in particular ways. When severe, it may prevent an individual from developing to their full potential and cause ongoing performance challenges, particularly in professions that need extensive collaboration and communication.
  2. The inability to criticize oneself constructively is a complex but significant problem. When this person's sense of self is threatened, they are able to see the warning indications that they are off the mark in the present, but they tend to ignore or suppress this knowledge in the future. It's not a problem of information collection, but of information retention, the tendency to suppress or ignore data that runs counter to one's self-conception. The performance of such people may be OK within their established boundaries, but their learning curve will flatten (and their relationships will become strained) if they allow their mistakes to be forgotten and "constructive criticism" to be disregarded.
  3. The interviewee's purposeful effort to conceal facts about his or her background demonstrates dishonesty, which is a serious character flaw. The candidate is basically aware of the reality but isn't ready to talk about it. Fair enough, it may be acceptable (it is an interview, after all) if a candidate subtly displays a predisposition to "spin" bad facts, especially at the beginning of the session. However, blatant or persistent efforts to avoid discussing one's flaws are a highly worrying symptom.


How can we identify the causal element? Of course, there is no perfect science, but based on my evaluations of hundreds of C-suite executives, I have found the following to be generally applicable guidelines:


  • Those who lack self-awareness may find it difficult to discuss both their strengths and weaknesses during an interview. You can get them to speak about an achievement or a mistake (the "facts"), but when you press them to pinpoint the underlying attributes they possess or lack, you get blank stares or a diagnosis that doesn't seem to match the data. There may be red flags in their in-room behavior, such as strange statements, bizarre behavior, or an unwillingness to react to the interviewer's obvious indications in the worst circumstances. However, poor self-awareness does not necessitate the presence of such behaviors; many people with little insight into their own character have learned to pass for average by subconsciously mimicking the actions of others around them.
  • Self-criticism-averse candidates will exhibit some of the following characteristics. To begin, people have an easier time remembering their shortcomings and prior errors when they occurred more recently in their lives. However, this is not the case when it comes to their strengths and achievements, which can be relied on regardless of the time span. Second, when they do admit to failings, they may show underlying signals of pain in their speech and body language. Their defensiveness, blaming, or retreat is a result of their tendency to internalize criticism. Third, they seldom accept full responsibility for the input they get, instead playing down the comments or moving on to a new position or company where they can start again with their faulty self-concept intact.
  • When confronted with questions concerning failures and vulnerabilities in previous employment, a dishonest person constantly deflects or twists the questions. Subtle indicators of discomfort are evident throughout the interview, just as they would be for someone battling with self-criticism. The deceiver's pain does not arise from the substance of addressing their faults, as it does in individuals with self-critique concerns; rather, it derives from their attempts to redirect you away from the matter completely. The dishonest candidate will usually have a ready explanation for each error or flaw you point out. They are getting ready to set up a guise in the hopes that you would fall for it. These might be "strengths disguised as weaknesses" ("I work too hard"), "irrelevant weaknesses" ("I'm bad at golf"), or "poor results that always appear to be the product of others' actions or "politics." And in the end, their tale doesn't seem to line up; they shift their goals and objectives, their job changes make no sense, and their judgments of their own performance don't seem to square up with reality.


Of fact, none of these three characteristics is very appealing. The fact is that we all have seeds of these qualities inside us, even if they are dormant or undeveloped at the moment. If you want to make better recruiting judgments, it's crucial that you recognize the differences between them, even if the scale of the differences is tiny.


After analyzing the situation in light of these criteria, the issue of whether or not the applicant should be disqualified arises naturally. There is no universal formula for making recruiting decisions; rather, it is dependent on (i) the scope of the problem and (ii) the nature of the position being filled. You could, for instance, be OK with a somewhat sub-optimal level of self-awareness if your position does not require regular and/or potentially stressful interactions with significant stakeholders. Similarly, if the position does not require a steep learning curve, you may feel confident hiring a candidate who sometimes suffers from self-criticism but is otherwise very bright and driven. The cost of a terrible hiring will always outweigh the expense of prolonging your search, thus I advise leaning toward a negative bias if there is any uncertainty about any of these factors.


Once you've narrowed the problem down to dishonesty, though, it's something you shouldn't skimp on. Warning signs include (i) asking a candidate for flaws or weaknesses out of genuine interest, (ii) redirecting the conversation respectfully when the applicant tries to deflect, and (iii) experiencing a second or third deflection. You shouldn't "call the candidate out" or end the interview if you have a problem with anything they said or did, but you should take note of it and be ready to address it with the other interviewers later. And take comfort; you have prevented an expensive hiring error.

blogs.pageKeywords: Pay Attention to These 3 Traits When Candidates Discuss Their Weaknesses and Mistakes

blogs.blogTitle: Before You Make an Offer Applicants Must Answer These 10 Questions
blogs.blogSummary: Getting "yes" responses to the 10 questions below is a more formal method of testing. It's vital to remember that being told "no" isn't always a terrible thing. Turning a potential recruit's "no" into a "yes" is an example of recruitment.
blogs.blogDescp:
It's always best to assume that someone who is offered a job will either take it, decline it, or change their mind later. As long as you use standard procedures while hiring, you won't have to worry about any surprises.


Never make an offer until you are certain it will be accepted.


This principle stems from the requirement to gauge the candidate's interest in an offer on its individual parts before making a formal written offer.


One easy test is to see whether the applicant would be willing to accept a reasonable offer and start work by a given date. Any hesitation in responding is a red flag that the offer will be declined.


Getting "yes" responses to the 10 questions below is a more formal method of testing. It's vital to remember that being told "no" isn't always a terrible thing. Turning a potential recruit's "no" into a "yes" is an example of recruitment.


1. Would you be willing to try something new if there was evidence that it would be a significant improvement over your current situation?


This serves as an excellent foundation. Now that you have a yes, don't try to sell them on the position, but on having a conversation about their future with the company. This is a brief examination of the candidate's LinkedIn profile and an explanation of the potential benefits of accepting the position. The 30% Solution is a non-monetary boost that includes all of the following: a larger role, quicker promotion, a bigger influence, and work that the applicant finds personally rewarding.


2. Would you be open to an initial phone interview with the hiring manager if the position offered a somewhat lower starting salary but a far more promising future?


This is a great follow-up question following a thorough phone interview with the recruiter. This eliminates financial considerations and replaces them with a focus on professional development as the driving force for promotions. It also involves the hiring manager from the beginning of the search.


3. Given what you now know, would you want to be considered a strong candidate for this position?


Be selective in who you ask to come in for in-person interviews. To finalize this deal, please restate the growth-over-compensation stipulation.


4. Is this position superior to the alternatives you've been thinking about?


Check out how the candidate's other options stack up against your position before you even consider them a finalist. If your job is not a top priority, figure out why.


5. We're considering ____ as the beginning date. Is it feasible for you to accept an offer this week?


Serious applicants start planning their start date shortly after the first round of onsite interviews. The absence of information on a potential start date is indicative of a lack of enthusiasm.


6. don't worry about the cash. How much do you need for this job?


Get the candidate's reasoning for accepting the position regardless of pay a few days before extending the final offer. Don't make the offer if they can't provide you with a convincing explanation of the 30% Solution.


7. Have you given careful consideration to the steps you will take to resign?


An insincere resignation is one for which the candidate cannot provide a detailed explanation. Inquire about this while arranging the delivery of the offer letter.


8. Is a verbal confirmation of your agreement to the conditions we've discussed acceptable to you?


A verbal promise and written acceptance within 24 hours is reasonable, provided that all parameters have been addressed and agreed upon. If they are evasive or trying to put you off, it should raise red flags. Before moving further, you should identify and address any issues.


9. If our amended offer satisfies your needs, may you give us your unqualified acceptance?


It's not uncommon for job seekers to want more pay before they'll officially accept an offer. Make it clear that you won't make any attempts to increase the compensation package until the individual promises to accept the revised offer if it is accepted. Allow them some time to think about it since it's a big deal.


10. Do you feel confident telling your coworkers that you have made your choice and will not entertain any counteroffers?


Include this stipulation in your discussion of a raise. Don't even try to negotiate a raise in your compensation without reading this first.

When deciding between employment offers, it's important to weigh more than just the salary and benefits package. Using the "go slow" testing technique outlined here, you may be confident that the applicant is making their decision to accept or decline your offer based on the merits of the position itself, rather than on factors such as salary, title, or geographic proximity.

blogs.blogTitle: Here Are 3 Things to Do When Your Best Employee Quits
blogs.blogSummary: Your most valuable employee raises the bar for the rest of the team. They are equally capable of illuminating a space and resolving a complicated issue with simplicity. But, sadly, they have just informed you that they will be departing.
blogs.blogDescp:
Your most valuable employee raises the bar for the rest of the team. They are equally capable of illuminating a space and resolving a complicated issue with simplicity. But, sadly, they have just informed you that they will be departing.


You bite down on the want to shout "Nooooo!" and instead respond with "Congratulations!" You let them know that they will be missed, and then, once you have taken a few deep breaths, you begin to plan out the next actions that you will take.


If the star player on your team ever lets you know that they are leaving for a better opportunity elsewhere, there are three things you should do in order to stop the bleeding and maybe even turn this loss into a gain in the long run. Here are those three things:


1. Conduct a departure interview, and then put what you've learned into practice


The vast majority of businesses consider departure interviews mainly as an exercise in problem-solving: Let's find out what pushed our top performer out of the company. (This disregards the notion that, as a top worker at your company, the person who is leaving may simply have gotten an offer they couldn't refuse.) An exit interview, on the other hand, may give more than just a list of issues that need to be addressed if the aims are more expansive.


According to the findings of one research, a well-executed departure interview may provide insight into what does and does not work at your organization, boost engagement among employees, enhance retention rates, and even create some competitive information. Finally, it has the potential to transform workers who are leaving the organization into brand advocates for the business.


Be careful to arrange the interview before the leaving employee has mentally checked out — don't wait until their last week in the office — and have second-line managers (the manager of the outgoing employee's supervisor) conduct the interview in a type of skip-level meeting. Make sure to plan the interview before the employee has mentally checked out. Record the chat, take notes, and most importantly, put what you learn into practice as soon as possible.


During the interview, it is important to discuss both the good and the bad parts of the employee's experience. Questions like "Why are you leaving?" are important to ask. What was it that you enjoyed the best about working here? In what ways may the situation have been better before you left? And if you were the company's CEO, what three things would you alter about the way it does business?


Once you have your star's opinion on their boss, find out what they think of their coworkers, the culture of the organization, and the circumstances under which they work. Make use of the opportunity presented by the interview to get some competitive intelligence: How does the level of pay, perks, and chances for promotion compare to what is offered at comparable firms in your industry? Last but not least, you need to make certain that you find out the specifics of what their function is at your organization. The majority of the time, it will be quite different from the previous job description that you provided on that position's website.


Invoke no hostility or condemnation in your interrogation of the other person. You will be able to gather the employee's replies that are the most thorough and insightful this way, and you will also increase the likelihood that the departing worker will have positive things to say about your business in the future. According to Gallup, a favorable departure experience makes a leaving employee almost three times more likely to recommend the organization compared to a neutral or bad experience during their time there.


2. Have a conversation with your staff as soon as you possibly can put an end to rumors and fear and remind them that transitions bring them new chances


Meet with the group that your employees are a part of as soon as you reasonably can. You should put an end to any rumors that are going about, and you should also be there when they find out the news. You have an obligation to put a stop to any rumors that have been circulating concerning "Who's next?"


If possible, your leaving star should break the news to the team first. Then you will need to communicate with them. Let them know that although the leave is a setback, you are delighted that your colleague has a new opportunity, and that you have a plan in place for what will happen in that employee's final days. Inform your staff how the job that their colleague was doing will be covered until a replacement can be located.


Insist on the fact that this is not the end of the world and that change is always both a problem and an opportunity. It's possible that this implies that work and responsibility may be transferred in a manner that more effectively plays to the skills and goals of the members of the team who are still there. To create room for work that will be both more interesting and highly appreciated, it's possible that certain jobs or projects may need to be abandoned.


Make it clear to your team that they can count on your assistance.

After that, pay attention.

Find out how the team, as a whole and on an individual level, is currently feeling. And find out what aspects of their work team members like and what aspects of their jobs they dread, and then attempt to discover methods to encourage them to focus more on the aspects of their jobs that they enjoy and less on the aspects of their jobs that they hate.


3. Make appointments for a series of stay interviews with your other important workers


One other option to get information and insights that help strengthen employee retention, team morale, and productivity is to do a round of stay interviews. These interviews can both offer you critical knowledge and reassure your greatest performers that you care about them.


It is not necessary to wait until a key member of your team who is essential to your success is leaving before conducting stay interviews. However, once someone does, they are always crucial to have.


In addition, they provide you with the chance to learn more about the employees you manage, while also ensuring that your top employees are occupied with work that both challenges and interests them. Your interview with them for a stay should include questions on what elements of their work make them thrilled, what aspects of their job they dread, what their ideal career would look like, and what aspects of their present role and duties they find the most bothersome.


When you spend time in employee stay interviews, you reduce the likelihood that you will need to devote time to hiring replacements for your best performers.


In closing, remember to muster up as much good manners and appreciation as you can so that your star may go out on a high note.


Be sure to have a going-away party for your star employee before you break the news that they are leaving your firm and honor the many accomplishments they have attained while working for you. Don't be afraid to speak about the aspects of their character that you'll miss in the future. This not only increases the likelihood that the individual in question will become a star ambassador, but it also sends a message to the other members of your team that you are not shaken by the news and that you actually feel that life will not only continue, but it will continue to bloom despite the change.


And when your organization has completed its exit interviews as well as its stay interviews, you will be able to deliver on your promise to the team that the departure would result in positive changes and new chances.


Last but not least, there is still another compelling incentive to ensure that your star employee leaves your firm with a positive outlook on their tenure there: According to the findings of research that was published in the journal Personnel Psychology, boomerang workers may account for anywhere between 10 and 20 percent of a company's "new" recruits. Therefore, it is possible that the employee you consider to be your "star" may really turn out to be a "comet," continually circling back to your firm in order to once again provide their light to the team.

blogs.blogTitle: 5 Ways to Regain Control of an Off-Topic Interview
blogs.blogSummary: Applicants that speak too much during interviews are a common source of frustration for hiring managers. While this may seem like a minor issue, it really affects a lot of
blogs.blogDescp:
Applicants that speak too much during interviews are a common source of frustration for hiring managers. While this may seem like a minor issue, it really affects a lot of people and may have a big influence on how well you evaluate applicants.


Picture yourself at a job interview with a candidate who, on paper, seems like a great fit. A former coworker of theirs has given them wonderful reviews, and their resume matches their claims perfectly. You've got an in-depth, well-researched interview guide and you're anxious to find out more about them. The conversation begins once you ask the first question. In addition to that, we spoke. In addition to that, we spoke. You want them to elaborate on an intriguing point, but they’ve already moved on to another digression. You've wasted 15 minutes so far, and you haven't even asked a second question. What went wrong, if anything? Where do we go from here?


It's tempting to rule out a candidate who can't provide pithy answers, especially if the position calls on polished public speaking and interpersonal abilities. It's possible that this is the best course of action, particularly if the behavior is severe and you've already tried your best to correct it. 


However, it's not always so cut and dried; I've seen more than a few instances when an extremely chatty applicant went on to perform excellently in the new post, the problem being nothing more than nervousness. Many interviewers I've seen really encouraged this kind of thing by failing to establish rapport or show interest in the interviewees as people. The interviewer's lack of confidence allowed the applicant to step in and take control of the conversation.


There are five methods I use to prevent the discussion from drifting and to prevent me from talking too much.


1. Establish baselines

Tell the applicant right at the beginning of the interview that you have a lot of ground to cover and that you realize your time is limited. If you want to make sure you have plenty of time for questions at the end, you may even ask for permission to nudge or interrupt them sometimes. If you can get the applicant to see the value in being concise before you have to call them out (i.e., before they have done anything wrong), you will have achieved your goal. This strategy is particularly helpful if your recruiters or other interview team members have warned you that this individual might provide some difficulties.


2. Interrupt regularly, but with enthusiasm

A CEO candidate may be interrupted ten, twenty, or more times by me during an evaluation. And I don't mean the apologetic "if you don't mind me jumping in" kind; I mean the type of interruptions where you just speak over the other person. Most of the time, the candidates have no idea that they are being interrupted; I do it out of genuine interest and energy. I can't help but show my curiosity in your narrative via my expression and words. This strategy calls for getting to know the applicant on a deep level, which can only happen if you're really curious about them as a person. It's not a forgery! When trust is high, though, interruptions are simple.


3. Make a "W" on your face

I've discovered that the best approach to encourage a candidate to let you step in during an interview, whether it's in person or through video chat, is to pucker your lips into a "W" shape as if you were ready to ask a "What" question. We're hardwired to recognize this particular mouth shape as the precursor to a question being posed by the other person. Test it out! Just purse your lips the next time you're chatting with a friend, and if you really want to wow them, raise your finger a few inches over your head as you do it. In the English-speaking world, this sequence of gestures is a near-universal indicator that the other person wants to ask a question, and it can be understood with no further explanation.


4. Make the "Sss" sound

Using the W face (and any accompanying head or hand motions) during a phone interview is probably not the best idea. You need a quiet noise that conveys the same message of wanting to speak out. I've conducted hundreds of phone interviews and discovered that the "Sss" sound is the most successful. This is because the higher-frequency "Sss" sound is typically more heard and clear across most phone lines. When I want to say "So," I often employ the "Sss" sound as a prelude, as in "Sss...so what did you..." Again, give this a go the next time you're talking to a chatty individual on the phone. This sound should be audible to the other person even while they are having a conversation, and they should understand it as a signal to allow you to intervene.  


5. Cut them off and say the time ran out if everything else fails

Because it entails a little (though momentary) disruption of interpersonal connection, this is not a strategy to deploy carelessly. If time is running out and you still have important questions to answer, you might indicate a "time out" by raising an open palm. It's a more direct approach to silence that could take the other person by surprise at first. Therefore, an apology should be made immediately after. 


You may try something like this:

I apologize for having to interrupt you. The clock shows that we have just [X] minutes remaining. I'm sorry. I hope we still have time for me to ask you a few more questions and for you to ask some of your own. Let me go through the answers to the following set of questions; if it seems appropriate, we may begin with the headline and go on from there. Does it make sense?

The applicant has now gotten a strong yet friendly indication from you, and they should act upon it.


The quantity of material you can cover in an interview and the frequency with which you find yourself talking too much will drastically improve if you get comfortable with these tactics. Naturally, there may still be problems sometimes. But if you've utilized these methods consistently, you can be certain that the issue is with the applicant, not your ability to moderate the conversation.

blogs.blogTitle: How to Find the Perfect Employee: Questions for the Hiring Manager
blogs.blogSummary: When a recruiter has to submit more than three or four applicants for a single outstanding hire, there is clearly something wrong with the process.
blogs.blogDescp:
When a recruiter has to submit more than three or four applicants for a single outstanding hire, there is clearly something wrong with the process. If two of the remaining three aren't adequate replacements, then something far more serious is wrong.


Now that I've concluded a series of meetings with a group of hiring managers and their recruiters, I'm more certain than ever that the primary reason there aren't enough eligible applicants is that recruiters and HR professionals don't fully grasp the true nature of the positions they're trying to fill. 


Gather all the information you need to find and hire top candidates at the intake meeting


As a new recruiter, I was able to pick and choose which search assignments I would take on thanks to my prior experience in engineering and manufacturing. However, my efficiency as a recruiter declined as I began to be tasked with responsibilities I had little experience with. I overcome my inexperience in the field by asking the recruiting manager a crucial question to kick off our first meeting:


In order to have a successful year in this position, what specific tasks must this individual complete?


In addition to having the recruiting manager identify a few main goals, it was also vital to outline specific tasks for the new worker to do in the first few months to gauge progress toward those goals. A performance-based job description is essentially a list of 6–8 key performance goals (KPOs) broken down by timeframe. By creating a job description like this, recruiters and hiring managers may work together towards a common goal of finding and recruiting the best candidates with the least amount of time and effort spent.


The most noticeable upside to this change was the opportunity to broaden the applicant pool to include high-potential, passive individuals who were qualified for the position but whose skill sets didn't match those typically required for it. Candidates that saw the position as a prospective career progression, rather than a general and undefined lateral transfer, responded 2-3 times better to our outreach communications.


We learned that SMARTe (specific, measurable, attainable, relevant, timely, and inclusive of environmental factors such as pace, culture, hiring manager, and unusual challenges) objectives for the most crucial KPOs were essential for gaining a holistic understanding of the position. The remaining key performance indicators should detail the work to be done, the necessary steps or adjustments, and the expected outcomes in as few words as possible.


Some examples of translating knowledge, experience, and abilities into such outcome measures are as follows:


  • Rather than requiring candidates to have "three to five years of experience selling SaaS software to the Fortune 500," a more effective requirement would be "maximize the territory plan to achieve a quarterly run rate of $250K within 12 months."
  • An overly ambitious key performance indicator (KPI) for a developer with 1-2 years' expertise in C# and.NET Core Angular may read, "Within six weeks, establish best practices for how the team will handle application data on the front-end, using Angular with NGRX."
  • The condition that the candidate has five years of foreign accounting expertise and a CPA from a Big Four firm is less informative than the demand that they complete the implementation of the new FCPA international reporting standards by the end of the second quarter.
  • The question "How is the competency used on the job?" may shed light on any quality that needs it. Competencies may be translated into concrete tasks, such as "Present quarterly status reports to the executive team" for the competence "Excellent communication skills."


Knowing this about the role made determining skill levels easy. Candidates were asked to provide specific instances of work that exemplified the KPOs outlined.


When the recruiting team officially presented their data utilizing this interviewing strategy, they were able to make considerably more accurate predictions of the quality of hire and on-the-job performance than they had been able to make using typical behavioral interviewing procedures.


This method assures that recruiters are screening on criteria that best predict performance and fit, in addition to explaining expectations up front with a performance-based job description (which, incidentally, has been found to be the #1 attribute of all successful managers). Recruiters can't do a good job of screening prospects or selling the position to top talent if they don't have a firm grasp on what the position really entails.


The greatest advantage, though, was that, once on the job, both productivity and contentment shot through the roof. The explanation was simple: businesses only recruited, evaluated, and employed people for jobs that they themselves found rewarding. The crucial distinction was the intake meeting's clarity of expectations. Problems with achievement and contentment arise because they are seldom defined until after an employee has begun working.


Making the switch to a performance-qualified strategy does not need lowering standards for aptitude, fit, performance, or potential. It's hardly rocket science to figure out that the standardization of job requirements down to a list of abilities, past experiences, and general competencies limits the caliber of applicants.

blogs.blogTitle: 8 Time-Saving and Stress-Reducing Productivity Tips for Recruiters
blogs.blogSummary: Here are eight easy-to-implement suggestions that may help you become more productive, spend less time, and even enjoy your work more.
blogs.blogDescp:
You need to impress your applicants and reach your goals. It's easy to feel like you can't accomplish both when your list of things to do seems infinite. After all, a 24-hour day has its limits. 


Fortunately, there are a lot of options for making the most of your time. Making even little adjustments to the way you go about your business may have a huge impact, freeing up hours and minutes each week. 


Here are eight easy-to-implement suggestions that may help you become more productive, spend less time, and even enjoy your work more.


1. should keep a log of their time spent on each task in order to determine which ones are the most time-consuming


Many in the recruitment industry feel they could do a better job, but they don't know how. That's because it's tough to make meaningful adjustments if you don't know which activities are sabotaging your productivity the most. 

Keeping time records might be useful. Apps like RescueTime and Toggl are available for this purpose. Toggl allows you to set timers for specific activities with just one click, while RescueTime operates in the background and generates a report based on your daily online activity. There are no cost options for either. However, if you want to take things back to the stone age, a pen and paper would do the trick. 


Use whatever approach you choose to keep track of your time for at least a week or 10 days before evaluating its efficacy. It's conceivable that certain activities are eating up too much of your time and may need some simplification. 


Identifying your peak and trough periods of productivity might help you plan your day accordingly. If you find that you get the most done first thing in the morning, you may prioritize things like sourcing and drafting job descriptions for when you have the greatest energy and attention. 


2. Use a browser add-on to minimize tabs when researching


Let me know if you've heard this before: It's been 10 minutes since you started your intensive sourcing session, and you've already amassed almost a thousand tabs. This is how it seems, at least. It's annoying and time-consuming to try to find the tab you need when you have so many open that they're all too small and you have to linger over them to see what the page contains. The browser's performance degrades when more tabs are opened. 


Reduce distractions with a lightweight (free) browser add-on. If you're using Google Chrome, you can keep track of all your active tabs in a unified list with the help of a plugin called OneTab. Tree Tabs for Firefox does a similar thing, allowing you to organize your tabs into categories and establish a hierarchy inside your list. These are only a few examples; the internet is full of more. 


In addition to improving your productivity while switching between tabs, a decluttering browser plugin may help you save memory and speed up your browsing experience. Everything benefits. 


3. disable alerts and only check email at certain times


When you begin to monitor your time, you may notice a pattern of frequent email checks. You could think that losing a minute here or there isn't a big deal, but eventually it will pile up. Even the simplest work ends up taking much longer than it needs to since it might take up to 23 minutes to establish your attention again after an interruption, according to some study.


When working on a subject that needs your undivided attention, it's a good idea to disable alerts on all of your devices. Try not to check your email at any other time of the day or night than the allotted time for doing so (maybe 15 minutes when you first come to the workplace and again after lunch). If you're having trouble avoiding temptation while checking email, closing the tab could help. 


If you're afraid about missing anything crucial, you can always include a quick message in your email signature to establish ground rules and tell people how to get in touch with you immediately if they need something. A candidate who is running late for an interview will have your contact information in case they need to reach you by phone or pigeon. 


4. consolidate your candidate sourcing, applicant management, and hiring procedures into a single digital hub


Even if you've mastered each method individually, switching back and forth between them all day, every day will cause you to waste time and energy. 


Gather your resources in one convenient location and save the additional hassle. Jobcart may facilitate this process. You can now save time and streamline your hiring processes by integrating Jobcart Recruiter with your existing applicant tracking system. 


This autumn, Jobcart will also launch Talent Hub to further streamline the application process. You may streamline your processes thanks to Talent Hub, where you can do anything from posting positions to sourcing applicants to making offers, all from one interface. In addition, the recruiting process will be transparent to the whole team, allowing for less duplication of effort and improved teamwork. (We will update you on the release date later this year.)


5. implement a chatbot on your job board to assist with applicant inquiries 24/7


Assisting potential applicants in locating relevant resources is crucial. However, time spent answering simple inquiries is time taken away from more valuable activities, such as getting to know the other person. 


Chatbots may be a huge time-saver by doing mundane tasks like delivering progress updates and answering frequently asked questions from applicants. You may choose from many ready-made solutions, like Mya and Olivia. Sutherland and Johnson & Johnson are only two examples of firms that have developed their own chatbots or interactive platforms. 


Plus, robots can work nonstop without tiring, while you have to take breaks. You may save yourself 30 minutes in the morning by not responding to candidate questions provided to you the night before. 


6. Adopt the Pomodoro Technique and give yourself a five-minute break after every 25 minutes of work


It's easy to neglect resting when you have an endless list of things to accomplish. However, you should reconsider your priorities if you've ever tried to eat lunch while completing phone screenings. 


The few minutes you spend getting a coffee or going for a fast power walk are well worth it since studies have shown that doing so increases productivity, creativity, and motivation. 


Try the Pomodoro Technique to force yourself into the habit of taking short breaks. Using a timer, you may split up your work into 25-minute chunks and remind yourself to take a brief break, perhaps five minutes, in between. The method was created by Italian chef Francesco Cirillo, who utilized a kitchen timer in the form of a tomato (pomodoro in Italian). However, the timer on your smartphone works just as well. If this time doesn't work for you, feel free to change it. 


You don't have to quit if you're really in the zone when your timer goes off. This method, though, may help you be more conscious of when it's time to take a break, which will save you from burning out after eight straight hours of work. 


And if you're having one of those days when you just can't put the phone down, maybe try checking it outdoors instead. Getting up and out of your usual environment is shown to increase productivity. 


7. Make use of a centralized calendaring system to save time and effort when arranging screening calls


It's shocking how much time is needed to arrange phone interviews and other preliminary screenings. When trying to schedule a meeting with a candidate, it may take multiple texts back and forth until you both agree on a time that works for everyone. 


Use a computerized calendar to simplify scheduling. Jobcart Scheduler allows you to include a link to your availability calendar in your InMails from inside Jobcart Recruiter. The scheduler will automatically add the candidate's chosen time and date to your calendar after they click the link. 

Tools like Calendly and YouCanBook.me are also available for your perusal. Both have freemium models, with additional premium services available for a monthly cost. 


8. Keep candidates informed often and quickly


Candidates' impressions of your organization and their openness to accepting an offer are both influenced by how well you keep them informed after the interview. The time spent on worried emails and phone calls from excited prospects, though, may add up fast. 


Setting out time on your schedule every week to deliver a brief update can help you resist the need to reply to communications immediately without negatively impacting the candidate experience. Stacy Zapar, a specialist in the field of recruitment, often devotes two hours on Friday afternoons to what she calls "Friday Feedback Blitz." 


The success of this tactic relies on candidates' expectations being communicated upfront. Stacy does this by promising each applicant that she would provide them with an update before the weekend begins, and then never breaking this promise. That way, potential hires won't feel the need to constantly harass you for updates, reducing the number of messages you get throughout the work week. 


Since time is of the essence, avoiding burnout is essential


Don't sacrifice efficiency for poor quality. Improve your effectiveness and productivity at work with a few simple adjustments.  

Prioritize automating the most mundane parts of your job and eliminating or simplifying processes that are time-consuming but not necessary. Pay attention to your routines and make adjustments to your schedule as needed to take advantage of your peak performance hours. Finally, remember to pamper yourself every once in a while. Taking frequent pauses is actually more effective than trying to power through exhaustion.

blogs.blogTitle: 8 Easy Methods to Impress Job Seekers at Every Stage
blogs.blogSummary: Candidates will appreciate your efforts and be more comfortable going ahead with a position if they realize that you are putting in the time and energy. Therefore,
blogs.blogDescp:
A bad candidate experience may cost millions annually in lost revenue and quality applicants. Even if they don't win the job, if applicants have a good time during the interview process, they are more likely to spread the word about the firm and may even become customers. 


This is motivating to think about, but it's not always simple to put into practice no matter how hard you try. Keeping every applicant interested in the process and enthusiastic about your firm might seem like an enormous effort when you have a large number of prospects flowing through the pipeline and your workload is piling up. However, this is not necessary. There are several straightforward approaches you may take that won't drain your time or your account. 


Candidates will appreciate your efforts and be more comfortable going ahead with a position if they realize that you are putting in the time and energy. Therefore, in order to stand out from the crowd throughout the recruiting process, here are eight simple strategies to wow and please prospects. 


1. Study potential prospects and make detailed notes

The best candidates always do their homework before applying to or interviewing with a firm. Just by repaying the favor, you may quickly demonstrate your appreciation. 


Make particular mention of anything from their social media accounts in your first message if they are a prospect you have researched. It may have been anything in their profile, such as a hilarious anecdote or impressive accomplishment, that attracted your attention on Jobcart. This easy strategy may boost your response rate by 10% by showing potential hires that you're paying attention to what they have to say.


It's important to review each candidate's résumé in detail before each phone screening and interview and to make note of notable achievements. That way, instead of just checking off items on a generic checklist, you may ask the applicant detailed questions about how different initiatives came to completion and what problems they encountered. You'll get more information about the prospect, and they'll know you're a conscientious recruiter. 


2. Help potential employees experience the company's culture and principles, rather than merely hearing about them


According to Gallup, your ability to entice the top 20% of the workforce may rest on the strength of your company's culture. Candidates are interested in hearing about the company's culture and the people who work there, but you may not be able to convince them to join by just telling them.


Anyone may boast that their culture is exceptional. One way to wow applicants is to have them experience the wonder for themselves. 


It's a good idea to include photos and videos of actual workers doing genuine work on your employment site. A film showcasing workers out in the community making a difference, for instance via a volunteering program, may be very motivating. 


One simple strategy for giving candidates a genuine taste of the company culture is to include the team they'd eventually join in the hiring process. During an onsite, invite the team to give the applicant a tour of the workplace and/or invite them to lunch. It may be helpful for potential new hires to hear about the company culture from current employees; they may also enjoy some lighthearted interludes between more serious interviews and discussions. 


The interview is also a great chance to speak about the company's principles and the ways in which they are embodied by workers. Candidates may learn a lot about themselves in these kinds of interactions since everyone wants to feel appreciated at work. 


3. keep the lines of communication open while you're recruiting


Inadvertently ghosting a candidate is a rapid method to ruin their perception of your firm. 


However, this also implies that maintaining communication is crucial if you want to maintain their interest and earn their confidence. That holds true regardless of whether or not an applicant passes the first screening. 


Stacy Zapar, a seasoned recruiter, is known for the heartfelt emails she delivers to unsuccessful job candidates. To save time, she utilizes a standard format that contains her name, position, and request to keep in contact. Not only does she provide the individuals she interviewed closure, but she also expands her network of potential prospects for future positions. 


Send frequent status updates to applicants who have advanced to the next stages, even if the sole news is that there is no news. This is particularly important during the interview and offer phases and may be the deciding factor in retaining applicants even if competing offers are made.


Naturally, when you have a large number of applicants in your pipeline, it may be easy to lose track, and communication may get muddled. Using an ATS may keep you organized and ensure that qualified applicants don't fall through the cracks.


The autumn release of Jobcart Talent Hub, an applicant tracking system, will centralize all of your current recruitment processes in one place. Everyone on your team will be able to see the current status of applicants in your ATS, so there will be no need to contact them again or presume that someone else has done so.


4. let the applicant know ahead of time how many people will be attending the interview


A panel interview might be intimidating for candidates who were expecting to talk with a single interviewer. You can help them do well in their interviews if you provide them advance notice of who they will be meeting with and what the schedule will look like. 


Send interviewees an email outlining the day's events, who they'll be meeting, and what each panelist will be focusing on a few days before the interview. You may let them know that the list may shift significantly based on people's availability if the timetable is still fairly fluid. This shows that you care about them and their experience and that you intend to keep them updated. 


You may demonstrate your support for the interviewees and provide them with the resources to prepare by providing a link to their profiles on Jobcart. Candidates' self-assurance in the interview setting directly correlates to their self-assurance in the organization.


5. Discuss the job's drawbacks openly (without being too harsh)


Candidates recognize that, regardless of the position or field, there will always be ups and downs. What they don't count on is for you to point out the disadvantages. By showing concern for their needs, you may improve their perception of your firm. 


Don't sugarcoat the job's drawbacks in your interactions with the applicant. The applicant would want to know whether there is a possibility that they will have to deal with challenging clientele or adhere to stringent deadlines. 


You shouldn't dwell on a topic endlessly, but honesty goes a long way. Use openness to your advantage by showcasing the ways in which your company helps its workers overcome these obstacles. This strategy might also be used to uncover persons who flourish in the face of adversity. 


6. evaluate your surroundings thoroughly and think about leaving the meeting room


The interview setting may have as much of an effect on the candidate's experience as the questions and answers themselves. And if the space is boring or stuffy, they may form negative impressions of the business. 

If you would rather do the interview at your office or a conference room, put in the additional effort to make the space inviting. Before they enter, open a window to allow in fresh air, clear the clutter, and tell folks to leave you alone. 


The ambient temperature should be taken into account as well. As a result, some female job seekers may get uncomfortably cold in the workplace due to heating systems that were developed with males in mind. whether you're unclear if the temperature is appropriate, ask the applicant when they arrive whether they're comfortable.


Interviews might be held in more intriguing or informal settings, depending on the location of the candidate. Mercedes-Benz, for instance, conducts some of its job interviews in the vehicles themselves. A change of scenery might be refreshing for candidates who are anticipating an office. 


7. keep your word as much as you can


Keeping your word is a basic but often overlooked method to enhance the applicant experience. 


If you've promised the interview at a certain time, you should make every effort to keep that appointment. The applicant has probably rearranged their own schedule to meet with you, and they may be worried about things like childcare or other time-sensitive matters. Showing up on time for the interview shows both the applicant and the employer that they are valued.


After the interviews are complete, give them an estimated response time and do all in your ability to meet that deadline. As soon as you discover you won't be able to provide a response by the time you said you would inform the person concerned. If you show them that you understand their anxiety throughout this time and are committed to keeping them updated, they may be more likely to accept your offer when it finally comes. 


8. Give some attention to the way in which you provide negative feedback


It's never simple to convey disappointing news. However, turning down applicants in a cold, indifferent manner, or keeping them in the dark, is a certain way to ruin their impression of your company. It may have a significant effect on profits if they are already clients of your business and the incident makes them reconsider future purchases.


Sending a timely, thoughtful, and individual email of rejection may tell volumes about the firm. Setting aside time to talk on the phone is also useful. It demonstrates your interest in the individual's background and keeps the door open for future communication, which may be useful if the candidate turns out to be a better match for another position. 


94 percent of applicants want to hear specific criticism about their strengths and areas for growth. Even if it's against the rules of your organization, you may still learn from rejected applicants what you might have done better. You'll be in a stronger position to improve your method and avoid making the same errors again with that critique in hand.


It's all in the particulars 


The applicant's experience may be made or broken by the smallest of factors. If an applicant doesn't get the job but is impressed by your consideration and attention to detail, they will remember you and your firm favorably. 


Cliché though it may be, putting yourself in the applicant's shoes may help you identify any issues with the process and fix them before the candidate even notices them. The procedure is in excellent condition if it seems to have been made with people in mind.
