blogs.blogTitle: The Importance of the Job Description During the Hiring Process and Beyond
blogs.blogSummary: It does not make sense to dilute the candidate experience for the individuals you truly want to employ in order to make everyone else feel good, especially because 95% or more of the people who apply for vacant positions are unqualified.
blogs.blogDescp:
For the record, I am of the opinion that it is crucial to provide an exceptional candidate experience for serious and well-qualified individuals. After all, you're making an impact on these individuals' lives, thus it's crucial that they have all the facts before them when deciding on a future profession. However, in my view, it does not make sense to dilute the candidate experience for the individuals you truly want to employ in order to make everyone else feel good, especially because 95% or more of the people who apply for vacant positions are unqualified. 


Recruiting top-tier talent requires a more inclusive application process


If you don't care about how the top prospects feel throughout the recruiting process, you're missing out on a great opportunity to improve the applicant experience. The applicant experience is meaningless if the most qualified and diverse candidates aren't even exposed to your job advertisements in the first place. Even if the top candidates stumble into your advertisements, they may be dissuaded from applying on the spot if the language used is derogatory, prejudiced, or exclusive. 


In addition, recruiters need to ensure that offerees have a firm grasp of the role, the company's ethos, and the hiring manager's management style. If they are hired without this knowledge, it creates a dishonest and deceptive applicant experience.


Keep in mind that the applicant journey doesn't end when they accept your offer; it continues long after that. Gallup reports that just 30% of American workers are happy in their current positions. Since high turnover rates are a serious issue in the United States, it's important to consider a candidate's feelings about their employment well after they've been employed.


Here are seven suggestions I have for making the application process stand out from the rest:


1. instead of using a technique that aims to screen out as many candidates as possible, adopt a "scarcity of talent" approach. You may enhance and raise the skill bar without raising expenses or time to fill by spending more time with fewer and more brilliant individuals.


2. at the first meeting with new employees, be sure to explain what is expected of them. Consider the following question while defining the employee value proposition (EVP): "Why would a top person want this job over competing opportunities aside from the compensation?" Due to its importance in attracting top-tier candidates, this data should form the backbone of a remarkable application process.


3. stay away from a resume-like job description. Top talent isn't interested in vague lateral moves and doesn't have time to decipher flowery job descriptions. Check out this sample advertisement that succeeds on both counts. 


4. Cover up all "Apply Now" links with duct tape. Include a request for a half-page cover letter detailing how the applicant's experience relates to the requirements listed in the job description to weed out the casual applicants. This is the legal reason for doing what we're doing.


5. Demonstrate to potential applicants why they should consider applying to your vacancy. The greatest approach to show prospects you value them is to take their careers seriously, so don't stop at providing timely feedback and being on time for meetings. It's important to have an icebreaker chat and introduce yourself at the very beginning of the sales process.


6. Learn about the candidate's personal drive and professional goals. The top applicants will be driven by the work itself and the opportunities it presents for advancement in their careers, rather than by the pay or benefits package. Instead of being in a hurry to make a hiring, take the time to get to know your applicants and their motivations via a "go-slow" discovery approach.


7. Take the time to hear out potential employees and use their input. The standard of an organization's interviews is indicative of the overall quality of the applicant experience. If the applicant's experience isn't professional, impartial, and comprehensive, then it's not good enough. When unsuccessful job applicants express satisfaction with the process and think they were fairly evaluated, you will have achieved this goal.


Concluding Ideas

To attract and retain top talent, businesses must provide more than just competitive salaries and perks; they must also deliver an engaging applicant experience that prioritizes human interaction over automated systems. To attract and retain top talent, it's important to tailor the hiring process to how people search for positions, how they anticipate being interviewed, and what they need to know to make the best long-term career choice. If done well, employee happiness and productivity will increase for a long time after hiring. This is the ideal candidate experience, and it's the kind of thing any business should aim for.

blogs.blogTitle: Use These 6 Recruiting Strategies to Increase Efficiency by 100%
blogs.blogSummary: In order to find a solution, I frequently reflected on my experience from prior jobs unrelated to talent acquisition. My background is in finding and fixing issues with production and distribution processes.
blogs.blogDescp:
As a new recruiter, one of my biggest annoyances was hearing excellent candidates rejected by terrible reasons. Every time this occurred, the HR manager would ask, "Do you have any other candidates?" and I'd have to start the search process again. It's a waste of time for the applicant, the hiring manager, and the recruiter. When this occurs often, it indicates a problem with the recruiting procedure.


In order to find a solution, I frequently reflected on my experience from prior jobs unrelated to talent acquisition. My background is in finding and fixing issues with production and distribution processes. It turned out that many of the skills I gained in that field were directly applicable to improving the recruitment procedure. I wanted to speed up my placement rate by a factor of two. It took a long time, but I eventually achieved my goals. Specifically, the following must occur:


1. have experience in the field. You can't attract top candidates or conduct a thorough interview without a clear understanding of the role the new recruit will play. Too many applicants need to be examined since it is impossible to predict their performance, fit, contentment, or motivation. It is more productive to ask the recruiting manager to describe the position in terms of key performance goals than to inquire as to the specific talents needed. In addition to setting the standard for a performance-based interview, this sort of performance-qualified strategy broadens access to the job market for applicants who may not fit the usual mold.


2. Use the A/B testing method. Many managers refuse to even meet with candidates who don't have the requisite set of abilities, despite the fact that over two decades of study has shown that doing so improves job performance. I'd submit a few of applicants that met the skills requirements and a couple more who met the performance requirements to highlight the distinction. Seeing greater talent and enhancing assessment accuracy just required one exceptional performance-qualified individual for most managers to concur that this technique was a game changer.


3. swim with the sharks. You should only socialize with other candidates or those who can introduce you to other candidates. Candidates for the final round should be performance qualified (able to complete the task as described in point 1), have the Achiever Pattern (suggesting they are in the top third of their peer group), and see the position as an immediate opportunity for professional growth. The hiring manager will meet with these candidates, and they have a good chance of accepting an offer is made.


4. Accepting "no" as a response is not an option. If a recruiter can't get beyond the "I'm happy where I am" or "I'm not interested" excuse, even the most desirable applicants will rapidly back out. Aside from perseverance, a thorough familiarity with the position and the art of the career-focused phone interview is essential here. Discovery selling is a lot like recruiting in that it's more vital to sell the conversation than to promote the position with generic and over-the-top hyperbole in order to attract the best applicants.


5. have just the semi-finalists come in for an in-person interview. Interviewing unqualified prospects is a waste of time for both the recruiting manager and the candidate. Hiring managers may reduce this issue by using a scripted, two-way exploratory phone screen to accommodate the demands of even the most picky candidates. It is more efficient for the management to have some idea of the employee's performance before meeting them, and the manager is less likely to be influenced by the employee's personality or looks.


6. verify all information with the recruiting manager. After years of trying to persuade a very loud and strong-willed CFO of a big firm, I finally managed to convince him that my director of cost accounting was one of the finest in the nation, despite his reticent demeanor. My applicant proved his mettle by presenting an overview of the intricate costing system he set up for a large automaker. The CFO's initial impression of the applicant was too quick, but after seeing this evidence, he consented to a second meeting. Because of this, this corporation is now our most important customer. The takeaway: Instead of relying on vague assertions about your abilities, get concrete examples instead.


If recruiting managers at your organization need to view too many prospects before making a single exceptional hire, something is wrong with your hiring process. The evaluation criteria are perhaps overly broad, which is the root of the problem. My approach to this issue was to educate myself on the nature of the task at hand and to find out what set apart the greatest performers from the others. The next thing I did was train myself to interview better than any of my customers. You may increase your placement rate by 100% if you do this, and it's essential for any recruiter to avoid having excellent candidates rejected for irrelevant reasons.

blogs.blogTitle: Improve Teamwork in Recruiting by Following These 8 Steps
blogs.blogSummary: To rapidly and effectively hire the finest candidates, recruitment teams must be able to work together effectively both internally and with critical partners such as hiring managers. If your team is having trouble with this, try switching around some of the routines to see if it improves matters.
blogs.blogDescp:
Working together provides tangible benefits that can't be ignored. A Stanford research, for instance, discovered that when individuals work together, they are able to maintain focus for 64% longer, are less likely to get bored or disengaged, and produce better results overall. The researchers found that individuals were more willing to take on problems when they felt like they were part of a team. 


To rapidly and effectively hire the finest candidates, recruitment teams must be able to work together effectively both internally and with critical partners such as hiring managers. If your team is having trouble with this, try switching around some of the routines to see if it improves matters. 


Here are a few strategies to make your recruitment team happier and more productive: foster thoughtful cooperation; enhance working connections; feed motivation. 


1. Define the team's overall objectives and think of ways to increase motivation by gaming them


The team's performance may be improved by the implementation of individual objectives and quotas. However, when everyone on the team has the same end in mind, they are more likely to stay focused on their tasks and hold each other responsible. 


Organizational targets might include things like speeding up the hiring process by 50% or increasing the number of employee referrals by 100%. It's important to talk about your objectives with your team so that everyone knows what they're working toward and how they may contribute. When setting internal objectives, it's best to relate them to the larger purpose and values of the organization. This will make employees feel more invested in their efforts. 


Adding elements of game theory to your objectives may have a significant effect on team output and enthusiasm. Creating a team-wide scoreboard, rewarding points for a job well done (such as finding a referral), and giving out a token of appreciation at the end are all viable options. 


DocuSign, an electronic signature and digital transaction management tool, has an excellent example of this strategy in action with its recruiting leaderboard. DocuSign utilized its data to rate recruiters based on criteria like the number of recruits they made and the complexity of the posts they filled in order to increase team engagement. The weekly awards for outstanding performance boosted morale across the board. And the corporation could step in and provide further assistance to anybody who needed it.


The best part is that after 12 months of the program, participation levels have increased and nobody has quit.


By encouraging some friendly rivalry, your team will talk about their objectives more often, which will help them stay front of mind and provide you with more insight into how everyone is contributing. 


2. Using an application tracking system (ATS) is a great way to increase efficiency and transparency


One of the greatest obstacles to working together is having your work hidden from view. Inefficiencies and embarrassing blunders might occur when team members aren't aware of what others are doing on a certain project. For example, you can believe you've located the ideal candidate, only to find out that your colleague contacted them a week ago. 


The dispersed nature of your team's workflow across many channels increases the likelihood that critical details may be overlooked. To prevent this from happening, it's important to employ well-integrated tools or a comprehensive recruiting solution, such as Jobcart Talent Hub. 


With Talent Hub, you can find, manage, and recruit new employees all from the same place. After adding the appropriate team members and hiring managers to a project, everyone involved will have access to the same information on each applicant. This lessens the possibility of miscommunication and facilitates teamwork. In addition to DocuSign, Zoom, and HireRight, Talent Hub connects with many other popular HR software programs; a full list may be found here.


3. Focus on open lines of communication and strive to provide timely information


When working with a big or geographically dispersed team, effective communication is crucial. Team meetings are great, but maintaining an open channel of communication between members promotes more cooperation and helps identify concerns before they become crises.


You can assist your team stay informed and on the same page by using online communication and collaboration solutions like monday.com or Basecamp. Team members are able to openly debate tactics, which ultimately benefits the team as a whole.


Using a messaging app as a primary means of communication allows for more efficient dissemination of vital information, acquisition of necessary permissions, and maintenance of forward momentum. Slack's HR department uses internal software for this same reason. The tiny staff places a premium on open lines of communication and systematic structure in order to efficiently process the tens of thousands of applications they get each year. This is accomplished via a five-stage process that begins with the approval of job postings and continues through the coordination of interviews, discussion of feedback, and the finalization of offer choices, all of which take place inside private Slack channels. 


No matter what medium you use, it's important to schedule regular in-person meetings. Scheduling weekly video sessions with tools like Skype or Google Hangouts helps put faces to names and fosters better bonds between team members if you can't get everyone together in person.


4. Instruct team members on how to handle disagreements maturely and foster productive discussion


Every group has disagreements from time to time, but if your recruitment team isn't prepared to handle them, they may completely derail your efforts to work together. 


Having an open forum in team meetings may help you prevent conflict and educate your team members on constructive ways to resolve disagreements. The creation of a safe atmosphere where all views are acceptable and available for discussion depends on allowing everyone a chance to express their perspectives, explain why they agree or disagree with specific methods, and examine alternative choices. You can make better judgments and avoid making errors if you create an atmosphere where people feel safe speaking their opinions, even if they go against the majority's (in this case, the team's).


However, some disagreements may not be related to the job at all or may go unnoticed. It is your duty as a manager to keep an eye out for any tensions within the team and to refrain from playing the role of facilitator in any way. Get a feel for the morale of your staff by doing anonymous surveys or setting up discreet one-on-ones if you need more information. If you don't, you can be caught off guard by allegations that one of your team members is stirring up trouble or by the departure of a key recruiter. 


Team members will be more prepared to manage future problems on their own if they have received training on how to assess a situation, reduce tensions, and reach an agreement as a group. Easily teach a large group of people with the help of Jobcart Learning's dispute resolution courses. Team members may benefit from using this printable worksheet created by the United States Department of Agriculture to think strategically through disagreements and reach a more meaningful settlement.


5. establish trust and openness as cornerstones of your group's culture


Another strategy to enhance communication and prevent conflict is to make openness and honesty cornerstones of the culture of your recruitment team. 

By candidly addressing both wins and losses with the team, you may assist promote an atmosphere of openness. Mistakes are inevitable, and if they aren't addressed, they're more likely to be repeated. Mistakes shouldn't be seen as failures, but rather as stepping stones to improvement. Therefore, have team members begin conversations with "Can you help me?" rather than diving directly into a discussion of the problem, since this will put their teammates in the mindset to proactively consider solutions.


You have a significant role to play as a leader in ensuring that these ideals are maintained. Your team will follow your example if you demonstrate that you are not afraid to admit when you were wrong or think on how you might have improved. One way to foster openness and teamwork is to have a daily standup in which everyone reports on their progress for the day. 


6. Improve the efficiency of your meetings and determine how important choices will be made by


Team meetings have the potential to either foster cooperation or stymie its progress. Lack of organization in meetings leads to people talking over one another, which is neither productive nor collaborative. 


Before anything else, you should figure out who absolutely must be there and what they must know in advance. Put forward and adhere to a well-defined plan of action. If someone is taking up too much time talking and slowing things down, you may want to have a private conversation with them later. 


They probably don't even aware they're taking up too much time at meetings; reminding them of the value of everyone having a chance to speak will help them approach meetings with more consideration in the future. 


Decisions that need to be made and the process by which they will be made should be laid forth at the outset of the meeting. By doing so, your team will be less likely to get paralyzed by uncertainty or frustrated when choices are made for them. A manager may choose to inform his or her squad that choices would be put to a vote, but that in the event of a tie, the manager will make the final call. If you lay out the steps from the outset, disagreements are far less likely to arise. 


7. Set an example that will aid your staff in avoiding burnout and advancing in their professions


It's easy to take on an "I'll just handle it" mentality when your staff is swamped with work and challenges. However, this might impede teamwork by encouraging other members to adopt the same strategy, which in turn raises the danger of fatigue for everyone involved. 


Instead, actively consider ways to work together to get things done, such as reaching out to others for support or crowdsourcing ideas. This will guarantee that everyone in your team has a fair share of the workload, and it will also provide those with more experience the opportunity to take on more challenging assignments, furthering their careers. If you approach your job like an island, the rest of your team is likely to do the same. Instead, try to find methods to create bridges and rafts so that everyone on the team puts an emphasis on working together. 


8. Educate yourself with facts and with one another 


However well your team works together, you can always do better. Taking the time to regularly retrieve reports from your ATS and dive into the data may shed light on the team's successes and areas for improvement. This may start people talking about different approaches, which can boost morale and teamwork. 


Team members' engagement with your recruitment tools may be monitored using this data as well. The use of Jobcart's Talent Hub has advantages such as these. Understanding the strengths and shortcomings of each recruiter is made simpler with information about their specific contributions. 


This kind of information may also help other members of the team learn from one another. If you want everyone on your team to perform at their best, encourage the individuals who are already doing so to share their knowledge with others. 


Concluding Ideas

It's simpler and more pleasurable for everyone involved when recruitment teams function like a well-oiled machine. Participation rises, errors decrease, and morale among team members is boosted.


True cooperation doesn't just happen; make sure everyone involved understands the goal and is committed to working toward it. It will become automatic if you keep working on it whenever possible.

blogs.blogTitle: Why High-touch skills are becoming more important in the recruiting industry
blogs.blogSummary: In the future, there will be more challenging positions to fill, thus tomorrow's recruiter will need to be able to handle them. This is the starting point...
blogs.blogDescp:
While recruiters may play a strategic role in any business, they frequently just care about finding a qualified candidate at a fair price in a timely manner. However, in my opinion, this kind of recruiting is a bureaucratic task that can be automated with the help of an applicant tracking system (ATS), a chatbot, and a robot. Conversely, when desirable candidates are few, a strategic recruiter is able to steadily increase expectations for them. Simple jobs should be given to robots. In the future, there will be more challenging positions to fill, thus tomorrow's recruiter will need to be able to handle them. This is the starting point. 


How to be an effective Recruiter in today's competitive job market


1. have experience doing the work. An effective recruiter should be prepared to answer the question "Tell me about the job?" from a top prospect by outlining some of the duties and responsibilities of the position and explaining why they are crucial. If they don't realize what they're missing, the individual is more likely to opt-out.


2. you should interview well. Hiring managers won't have faith in the recruiter's judgment, and the recruiter will have to present an excessive number of prospects if they can't conduct interviews professionally and accurately. The worst part is that the top performers won't see them as a consultant, but rather a gatekeeper.


3. disregard criticism. The top applicants, whether they are actively seeking work or not, will give you a litany of reasons why they aren't interested. In order to have a meaningful talk about your job, you will need to be persistent and have answers to their complaints.


4. Turn non-active job-seekers into active salespeople. Selling an amazing job with empty superlatives and cliches isn't what recruiting is about. You need to sell your employment as the greatest, even if the pay isn't, to someone who isn't actively hunting for work.


5. Create messages that are captivating. There is no magic set of abilities or "must-have" competencies that can guarantee you the best and brightest employees. The key is to create riveting, narrative-based ads like this one that prompts a response.


6. the finalists' primary sources. Those who made it to the semi-final round were likely well-regarded by the recruiting manager and would take a salary rise of up to ten percent.


7. find excellent competent recommendations. These people are gold for recruiters since they respond to your call and are excellent candidates for the position.


8. bargain for proposals that include small raises in pay. This is simple to achieve if the prospect is unemployed or desperate, but the goal is to get them to concentrate on the long-term career potential rather than the immediate financial benefits. The recruiter, hiring manager, and applicant all have more work ahead of them, but it will be well worth it in the end. 


9. Make yourself a fascinating person. Include the line "Let's talk even if this job isn't perfect" in your profile. I can get you a better position, or help you get started on a more promising career path, at one of my other companies.


10. Use analytics and a "small batch, high touch" approach to managing your talent pool. One exceptional prospect is all you need to make a successful hiring. You only need a few A-listers, a well-oiled recruitment machine, and an out-of-this-world experience for your candidates.


The main line is that talking about a prospective positive career change, rather than an unfilled position, is the best way to attract and retain top people. Don't stress about finding cheap and quick replacements for vacant roles. Slowing down can help you offer fewer prospects to hiring managers who will perceive your opening as the ideal career step, so you can focus on improving the quality of employees at your organization. Time-to-fill and cost-per-hire may be decreased and the quality of hiring increased via increased use of referrals and the cultivation of deep-talent networks. 


Recruiters need to create a high-touch, individualized approach that makes use of technology without being beholden to it if they want to continue to be successful. The quality of the individuals employed in the future, in my view, will rely on the quality of the recruiters recruited now, and getting good recruiters begins with learning and practicing the ten skills listed above.

blogs.blogTitle: 12 Questions That Every Manager Should Ask When Recruiting
blogs.blogSummary: If you are in charge of a group of individuals, I need you to answer two questions: who is responsible for recruiting new members? And who is responsible for...
blogs.blogDescp:
If you are in charge of a group of individuals, I need you to answer two questions: who is responsible for recruiting new members? And who is responsible for your team's inclusive culture?


The person you see when you look in the mirror first thing in the morning should be the answer to both of these questions.


When you're in charge of a team, it's your job to ensure that the best people join that team, that they stay engaged and continue to develop professionally, and that the best performers stay with that team. Your human resources or talent partners are there to advise and assist you at all times, both strategically and operationally.


However, in the end, it is up to you to make sure you have the right people on board to get the job done. And to enhance any team's effectiveness, an efficient recruiting effort that brings in a wide range of skills is essential (the overlap of Q1 and Q2).


Despite all of this, many managers make hires on the fly and haven't put any thought into being as inclusive as possible. They may wonder, "So, what should I be thinking about and doing?" which is a very reasonable and normal follow-up inquiry.


In order to successfully lead an inclusive recruiting process in tandem with their recruiter, here is a beginning list of questions hiring managers should ask themselves at each level of the process:


You're ready to expand your crew, so...


1. Which skills are really necessary, and which ones are mentioned automatically without question?


2. What knowledge or skills does the team now lack, and why are they needed?


3. Whoever you recruit, regardless of their history, has to demonstrate what skills, habits, and results during the first 90, 180, and 365 days on the job.


You and your recruiter are prepared to begin the hiring process...


4. Is the plan for finding candidates likely to reach and interest members of under-represented groups?


5. "What personal connections can you tap to help source a diverse set of candidates for this hiring?" These connections might be persons, organizations, or platforms.


6. Have you selected a panel of interviewers who represent a wide range of demographics and worldviews?


You're in the process of judging potential employees...


7. When interviewing potential employees, do you use the same standards and questions?


8. Do the questions you're asking aim to narrow the pool of applicants, or do they primarily serve to weed out unqualified ones?


9. If you looked at the candidates you're favoring together, would you see any themes or trends that would indicate unconscious biases?


So, you're ready to forward some of these potential hires to the next round...


10. Are you and your recruiter sure that, by the time you make an offer, you will have evaluated a varied slate of prospects at the onsite stage, given the pool of applicants and the progress made so far?


11. Have you discussed ways with your recruiter to broaden your applicant pool's demographic representation if you're not yet sure about obtaining a varied slate of candidates? 


12. Is your interview panel considering factors like those mentioned above while making decisions?


I recommend giving each of these questions careful thought as you build your team. They will ensure that your team has the range of experiences and viewpoints you need for success.

blogs.blogTitle: 6 Tips for Expanding Your Influence and Getting Your Ideas Heard
blogs.blogSummary: 6 Tips for Expanding Your Influence and Getting Your Ideas Heard: It's one thing to have ideas and let's face it, a lot of people have but if you want your ideas...
blogs.blogDescp:
My life's job as a public speaker and leadership coach is to encourage business executives to look optimistically into the future of work and use their positions of authority for the greater good.


There are tremendous openings for you to take charge and propel the company forward if you are a leader of people in your organization, which you probably are even if you don't have direct reports.


Your group is counting on you, in other words. They will not be able to overcome the next obstacles without you contributing your knowledge and insight.


It's one thing to have ideas and let's face it, a lot of people have but if you want your ideas to be heard and, hopefully, adopted, you need to put in the time and effort to establish your credibility and authority first.


Achieving more influence with these three dos and don'ts:


Dos:



Express your viewpoint


Do you want to have your ideas considered and respected? The next step is to make an offer.


Tell the company what you think of their problems and how you would approach solving them. Act with decisiveness while yet openly considering the input of others.


Create connections and a name for yourself


Being recognized as prominent outside of your organization is a great approach to getting respect and credibility inside it.


Create a name for yourself as someone who is respected in your field and by your peers, and who is sought out by others.


Bring Information Always


Get familiar with the market, your industry, and your rivals to show that you have done your research and are an expert in your field.


Put the data to work in decision-making, and always keep the bottom line in mind while offering suggestions.


Read on for three more arguments why your HR department ought to invest in people analytics right now.



Don'ts:


Avoid using rules and regulations as an excuse


One of the easiest ways to be seen as a person with little power is to attempt to compel compliance or to place barriers in someone's path by referencing regulations or laws, which is the corporate-speak equivalent of "because I said so."

Make sure everyone is on the same page on the rules' goals and ramifications, and encourage them to collaborate on finding workable solutions.


Avoid taking disagreements personally


This is the Godfather quote you've been looking for to use in the workplace: "It's not personal, Sonny. It's entirely professional.


To get at the greatest ideas and solutions, healthy conflict may be beneficial.

Maintain your half of the bargain by giving thoughtful responses to criticisms of your ideas or proposals and by offering explanations and clarifications where necessary.


Sit around waiting to be given orders


Can you relate to the feeling that "Nobody asked me!" If that's the case, you've got an issue, and they don't.


Maintain a state of vigilance and make an effort to learn about the business's potential and threats; then, figure out how you and your team can best assist the company in meeting its long-term goals. Don't sit on your hands if you want to be heard.


Looking to expand your sphere of influence?


Focus on developing connections and sway with important leaders in your organization, industry, and profession today if you want to be the one leading the way to success for your team in the future.


You can get your thoughts heard more easily if you have more influence.
blogs.pageTitle: 6 Tips for Expanding Your Influence and Getting Your Ideas Heard
blogs.pageKeywords: 6 Tips for Expanding Your Influence and Getting Your Ideas Heard
blogs.pageDescp: 6 Tips for Expanding Your Influence and Getting Your Ideas Heard

blogs.blogTitle: Signs that you're about to make a poor hiring choice
blogs.blogSummary: Indicators galore throughout the whole recruiting process that should raise red flags if you're worried about a problematic hire. Here are a few of the more...
blogs.blogDescp:
The financial impact of recruiting mistakes on your business may be significant. I urge my customers to add the percentage of first-year turnover to the proportion of employees they wouldn't rehire to get an accurate estimate of this expense. The percentage indicated there are a lot of bad hires at your organization. Then, I have them multiply the BHR by the sum of the new recruits' payroll contributions to get an estimate of the financial impact of poor recruiting choices.


If you're employing anywhere from a few hundred to over a thousand individuals, you'll see from the example and the graph below that this may be a very large amount indeed.


For illustration purposes, let's say that last year you spent $1,000,000 on payroll and lost $200,000 due to employee turnover and employees you wouldn't rehire. The beneficial effect more capable individuals would have had is irrelevant, as is the negative effect the terrible hires had.


Fortunately, there are indicators galore throughout the whole recruiting process that should raise red flags if you're worried about a problematic hire. Here are a few of the more glaring ones:


1. There is disagreement among the recruiters on what the position really requires


This is the most common reason for hiring mistakes, yet it may be easily avoided. First, the interviewers need to agree on a common response to the question, "What must the person in the role do during the first year in order to be considered a successful hire?" Key performance objectives (KPOs) or objectives and key results (OKRs) are used to describe the most important overarching goals and supporting work.


2. Applicants aren't being specific enough in their applications


Request a justification from the applicant before making a formal offer. An effective answer would include information on the nature of the job (the KPOs), the management style of the hiring manager, and the opportunities for professional development that exist within the firm or industry. Inattention to these aspects of the job may be indicative of a lack of interest in the work itself, which may lead to problems with both success and job satisfaction.


3. interviewers' arguments for hiring or not hiring are based on insufficient facts or don't pertain to the position at all


Dates, metrics, and specifics regarding the candidate's prior performance in roles most similar to the requisite KPOs and OKRs specified in the job description are, in my view, solid proof. Without this data, particularly whether hiring on the basis of emotional bias or general abilities, it is impossible to reliably anticipate an employee's success.  


4. The most qualified applicants usually back out early in the hiring process or turn down the offer


Recruiters and hiring managers must reach a consensus on the top candidates before making any calls. A strong measure of a company's ability to attract the best for the proper long-term career reasons is the percentage of top prospects who consent to be interviewed, complete the rigorous sequence of interviews and evaluations, and accept an offer.


In conclusion


To illustrate this complete "How to Avoid Bad Hiring Decisions" procedure, use Amazon's skill bar-raising effort as a proxy. Its software gives a third-party interviewer the power to overrule a hiring manager who makes a judgment based on expediency rather than the candidate's long-term happiness, productivity, and potential. However, rather than finding it out at the conclusion of the selection process, it is preferable to establish what I call a Win-Win Hiring approach from the start.


The 1 year anniversary of the candidate's start date is used as the success date in my Win-Win Hiring procedure. If this method of selection is used, both the hiring manager and the new employee will feel certain that they made the proper choice in accepting the position a year later. This may sound obvious, but it may be a game-changing concept that leads to much better hires and significant cost savings.

blogs.blogTitle: Incorporating Diversity and Inclusion Into Your Business: 50+ Strategies
blogs.blogSummary: Companies all across the globe are examining their diversity and inclusion (D&I) policies and initiatives in the wake of recent demonstrations against police...
blogs.blogDescp:
Diversity and Inclusion


Companies all across the globe are examining their diversity and inclusion (D&I) policies and initiatives in the wake of recent demonstrations against police brutality and systematic racism. Although difficult, this task is essential in making the workplace a more welcoming and inclusive environment for all employees.


First and foremost, I urge you to not get immobilized by this situation, as someone who built a team that became notable for its diverse workforce. There is a lot to accomplish, but the most important thing is to get going. Building a diverse and inclusive workforce and culture takes time, and it will be more difficult if you wait. 


Below you'll find over 50 suggestions for fostering a more welcoming and diverse working environment. These are intended to have an immediate effect and may be implemented in a variety of contexts and resource configurations.


Effective D&I will always call for a mix of risk-taking and attention to detail. However, the little successes and efforts are seldom highlighted in the media. Rather than seeing diversity and inclusion as "all or nothing," here is a list of little projects that may be undertaken to help create momentum.


Okay, so here we go:

1. Create gender-neutral phrasing for all job postings. You may use Text.io, an excellent tool for this, to audit all of your job descriptions to find instances of the pronouns "he/his/him" being used as the default and change them to gender-neutral pronouns like "they." If you're concerned that phrases like "aggressive" or "dominant" could discourage female applicants, use this free software and do a search.


2. To reduce the possibility of bias in the resume assessment process, blind screenings should be used. Studies have revealed that applications with feminine names are scored lower than those with masculine names, even if the applicants are equally competent, and that persons with stereotypically "ethnic" names have to send out more resumes before they receive a callback.


3. Remove the excuse of a lack of "culture fit" as an excuse to not hire someone. Unconscious bias is usually at play when interviewers seek to reject prospects based on "culture fit" or a "gut feeling." In order to identify implicit biases and have honest talks about them, ask your interviewees for a more detailed explanation. Never resort to public shaming or punishment; such actions often have the opposite of the desired effect.


4. Insist on hearing from a wide variety of references. Encourage your staff to go farther afield than just their social circle of three closest friends, who may or may not come from the same background. Stress that diversity is something that has to be actively pursued and that all workers may contribute by introducing you to exceptional individuals they know who may not match the "traditional" description. In the end, it will only help the team out more. Pinterest's blog post on how it improved things by changing how it handled referrals is here.


5. Create job descriptions that focus on outcomes. Men are more likely to apply for a job if they satisfy just 60% of the prerequisites, whereas women wait until they meet all of the requirements before applying. Objectives for the first month, six months, and year on the job are more useful than a laundry list of prerequisite abilities that may exclude minority individuals. 


6. Create a "Guys jar." The Bay Area company npm uses a "Guys jar" to discourage the use of unnecessary gendered jargon in the workplace. Someone at npm will put money in the jar every time they use the wrong gender pronoun. When they save up $50, they give it to a good cause.


7. Spend money on a well-defined interview process and training. Structured interviews lead to higher-quality recruits because they help decrease prejudice and "gut-feeling" hiring. This is not to mean that you must follow a precise script in interviews; applicants frequently give key insights when talks flow organically. If you ask each applicant the same set of questions or ones that are similar, you'll have a uniform "data set" from which to make more objective judgments. Interviewers may learn and refine your procedure more quickly when they follow a set plan.


8. Be careful to interview people from underrepresented groups as well. However, don't put too much on their plate. It's not fair to your one female engineer's performance (or sanity) if she has to sit on every single interview panel.


9. Establish a culture of diversity and inclusion from the beginning of an employee's career. Make sure new hires understand the importance your firm places on diversity and inclusion, your definition of it, and the measures you're doing to promote a sense of community. New employees may have questions about the steps your organization is doing to increase diversity; be ready to address those concerns. 


10. Ensure you have sufficient supplies in the restroom. If you can afford alcohol for the workplace, you can afford to keep tampons in the restrooms, too.


11. Alex can help you monitor team chats for sexist language. Alex is a free and open-source program that may be used in any text editor, such as Google Chrome or the Slack messaging app. Harmful language will be flagged and users will be politely reminded of alternative wording.


12. Make sure your company's internal messaging system has a dedicated D&I channel. Create a D&I-focused Slack channel for staff to discuss relevant topics and distribute relevant content. This may facilitate open dialogue on diversity and inclusion in the workplace. 


13. Show your support for marginalized communities by participating in Black History Month, Juneteenth, and Pride activities. 


14. Begin a rota for who does the dishes. Have you ever observed that women seem to take on more "office chores" than men do? To counter this, designate two employees daily as "dish duty" to ensure that everyone pitches in and the workplace is kept tidy.


15. Employees that go above and above should be publicly acknowledged for their efforts. Your contributions to your firm go beyond meeting sales quotas and getting things out the door; they also include making the organization a pleasant place to work.


16. Provide variable work schedules. Give your staff the flexibility to choose their own schedules to show that you have faith in their ability to get the job done. Because of things like taking kids to and from school, some people may not be able to stick to a traditional 9 to 5. Some workers may decide to go elsewhere for work if they are not given enough leeway to adapt to changing circumstances. 


17. Get some flashcards and test your wits! It might be challenging to know what to say to raise D&I awareness amongst your colleagues. Knowledge cards were developed by the Society of Women Engineers to spark conversation and introspection about diversity and inclusion.  


18. In order to assist employees see their own prejudices, you might have them take the Implicit Association Test. It is typically a crucial first step toward more in-depth D&I dialogues when people admit that they, too, are biased.


19. Take a look at how hot it is at your workplace. Most buildings are set at a temperature that is suitable for males. We can't ignore the fact that some of your staff members may need to wear sweaters and coats all day long just to stay warm in the office.


20. Verify the lobby's selection of magazines and newspapers. Instead of providing publications that are obviously aimed towards one gender or the other, consider providing periodicals that are relevant to your sector. GQ isn't something your lobby should have unless you work in the fashion industry.


21. Create gender-neutral stall labels. To make it easier for transgender and gender nonconforming workers to use the restroom that best corresponds with their gender identification, hang signs reading "For those who identify as" above the gender signifier on each door. 


22. Put up a sign detailing your company's dedication to diversity and inclusion (D&I) and how workers may help foster an inclusive environment. Look at these posters The Guardian put up throughout the workplace to spark conversation around its 10 D&I pillars as an example.


23. Create a mother's room so that nursing mothers may pump in peace. It's a decent thing to do, and depending on where your organization is situated, it may also be the law. Winnie gives us the lowdown on free or cheap lactation spaces.


24. Plan daytime outings to foster team camaraderie. When all the fun starts at 5, it might be difficult for working parents and caregivers, as well as those with alternative schedules, to take part.


25. Obtain your goods from a store that caters to women, or one that doesn't strictly adhere to the gender binary. Assuming unisex works for everyone is a sneaky method to exclude people.


26. The diversity of your workforce may be celebrated and conversation can flow freely with a potluck including delicacies from across the world. 


27. Review the visual design of your job posting website with new eyes. Which age groups and sexes predominate in your pictures? In your biographies as leaders? A lack of diversity on the company's job website may send the wrong message to prospective employees.


28. To that end, we need to use modern means. Emerging "D&I tech" offers a wealth of opportunities to address problems. Check out Interviewing.io (for objective technical hiring), Compaas (for equitable pay), and Allie (for an inclusive Slackbot) as a starting point.


29. Create a blog. Most of the top people in any field want for a welcoming environment where they can give their all. So that potential applicants may get a real feel for what it's like to work for your organization, and have existing workers from underrepresented groups write about their time there. 


30. If your firm currently hosts regular presentations on technical issues or on leadership, consider inviting a guest speaker from the D&I community to speak instead. Please reserve a few timeslots for D&I.


31. Distribute meeting agendas and topics of discussion in advance to ensure everyone has a chance to speak. Some individuals like discussing novel ideas right away, while others (like introverts) need time to mull them through before speaking. Immediate issue presentation reduces the likelihood of input from the second group.


32. In order to have everyone's opinion heard, use the Round Robin method next time you have a meeting. Having the option to pass ensures that no one person's voice is drowned out and that everyone has a chance to contribute.


33. Highlight the lapses in silence. Women are far more likely to be stopped in meetings than males are, according to studies, and this may make them feel as if their ideas aren't being taken seriously. You can get folks to stop doing it by making them more conscious of how often they are interrupted. 


34. Create a policy that allows for parental leave. The financial model that Optimizely utilized to fight for their expanded parental leave policy from 6 to 17 weeks may serve as a useful template for other companies. A more welcoming and competitive hiring environment may be achieved in part via policies that assist parents and caregivers.


35. Aim for a payment plan that leaves little room for discussion. Research shows that males are more likely to negotiate than women and that women may face negative consequences if they attempt to bargain. If it's impossible to avoid compromise, then maybe each position can operate within tight ranges. To ensure that all workers and applicants are paid equally, several companies, like Glitch, have made their compensation ranges public.


36. Keep open office hours. Hold regular office hours and encourage feedback on D&I issues if your HR/People department has the resources to do so.


37. Fund a gathering. Gather other businesses to compare notes on D&I initiatives, both successful and unsuccessful. The availability of video conferencing capabilities has made it possible for teams to have meetings even while they are physically separated. 


38. Sponsoring groups. Offer to be a sponsor for groups that are engaged in activities that you like or which you would want to participate in. Sponsorship informs workers that your organization puts its money where its mouth is and is devoted to supporting good change, even if you can't contribute in any other manner. 


39. Collaborate with local groups and charities. Giving to causes that share your values earns you brownie points. Do you manufacture edible goods? Get in touch with local food banks and shelters and offer to donate to their fundraising efforts. Ed-tech? Your workers may contribute their expertise by tutoring or volunteering with one of the many organizations that need help. 


40. Think about D&I while discussing your performance. Keep D&I in mind by discussing efforts, progress, and expectations with hiring managers throughout performance meetings, even if you aren't linking D&I directly to individual objectives. 


41. Employees may benefit from Learning and Development programs that help them hone transferable skills like as communication and empathy. An emotionally savvy workforce is a welcoming one.


42. To better understand one other's work and communication styles, consider having all employees complete a test like the Insights Discovery Working Styles Inventory. Learning about who we are may help us learn about and appreciate the differences in others. 


43. Allow for variable vacation time. Facilitate worker agency by letting them choose their own break times. You're showing that you trust their discretion by allowing them to take time off whenever they need it.


44. Make sure mental health days are included in your sick leave policy. Allowing workers to take mental health days indicates that you value their emotional well-being. Reduce employee fatigue and maintain productivity with this simple policy adjustment. 


45. Decorate your workplace again. Put up signs, pictures, and other mementos to remind staff of the principles you want them to internalize. Employees at Slack's San Francisco headquarters, for instance, are reminded to "Work hard and go home" through a sign on the wall. This helps to prevent a culture in which workers feel they must put in excessive hours, which may be particularly difficult for parents.


46. Workplace ergonomics should be funded. Because of this, it's important to create a positive work atmosphere by accommodating employees' unique requirements.


47. Look at diverse insurance plans. The lives of minority workers may be greatly improved if you provide inclusive benefits like as paid adoption leave and transition-related care for transgender employees. 


48. Run a book discussion group. There is a wealth of excellent material available that might serve as a springboard for thought-provoking debate. Having a book club may help coworkers feel more comfortable talking to one another and building relationships, while also exposing them to new ideas and viewpoints.


49. Throw a movie party. Another option that doesn't need reading a whole book but still leads to deep discussion is a movie night. There's a wide variety of films to choose from, including those directed by women and people of color.


50. Prompt your top officials to join the discussion. For diversity and inclusion initiatives to be successful, top-level support is essential. Encourage your executives to take part by having them do things like forward articles, tweet about the progress your organization is making, and put D&I on the agenda of company-wide meetings. Leadership silence has the unintended consequence of discouraging people from underrepresented groups from applying.


51. Banks owned by black people should be supported. In a recent move, Netflix put $100 million in banks that mostly work with people of color. Due to their longstanding undercapitalization, this very easy fix may have a significant impact.


52. Drop the racist slurs and use more neutral language. Change the use of terms with potentially racial connotations, such as "whitelist" and "blacklist" (or "master" and "slave"), in the context of the IT business.


53. Organize a scholarship program that encourages and rewards cultural and racial diversity. Providing financial support for minority professionals' conference attendance may help break down barriers and open up networking possibilities. For instance, 15 people from underrepresented groups in technology are sponsored by F5 Networks' D&I sponsorship program to attend DevSecCon. In addition to increasing brand recognition and interest, this strategy may help you attract and retain a more diverse pool of prospective employees.


54. Use this guide to tear down racism as a support system. The Dismantling Racism Works workbook was developed for a two-day workshop but is now freely accessible to everyone over the Internet. It contains a lot of information that may be put to good use.


55. Pay close attention to what your staff has to say. Spend money on things that are important to them.


56. Begin on a modest scale. Don't procrastinate because of the seemingly endless list of tasks that need to be done. The only way to make progress toward more diversity and inclusion is to put words into action.

blogs.blogTitle: 10 Crucial Interview Questions for Hiring Remote Workers
blogs.blogSummary: In the past, interviewers focused on applicants' experience, work ethic, and social skills. However, because many positions are now available only in a remote...
blogs.blogDescp:
You may add the topic of interview questions to the list of topics that have evolved during the last year. In the past, interviewers focused on applicants' experience, work ethic, and social skills. However, because many positions are now available only in a remote capacity, you'll need to update your interview questions to reflect this shift. 


Remote employment is here to remain, even after vaccinations are distributed and life returns to normalcy. One in four American workers will use remote access to the workplace in 2021, according to a poll conducted by Upwork. 

How can you tell whether an applicant would thrive as a telecommuter? We've compiled a list of 10 interview questions for remote employees, with an emphasis on the soft skills necessary for effective remote work, to assist you make the best hiring decision possible. 


1. Have you ever done any job from afar?


Many or perhaps most candidates would have said "no" to this question prior to the year 2020. Many more individuals have had the opportunity to work from home since last year when 51% of the workforce did so at the height of the pandemic restrictions. This is still a crucial issue to address, since the romanticized image of working from home (in your pajamas on the sofa) may not always match up with reality.  


2. If you have experience with remote work, what difficulties did you encounter?


This will offer you an idea of how the applicant has dealt with isolation, low connection, and slow information access, all of which are common in remote work. This question might help you figure out whether or not a candidate is prepared for the challenges of working remotely. An excellent indicator of someone's ability to find ways to cope with difficult situations is if they tell you that they attempt to meet a friend for lunch once a week or arrange a video lunch with a colleague. 


3. what is your preferred place of employment?  


Here, you're looking for clues about the prospective employee's actual place of employment. It's possible they work from home. Perhaps they choose to do business from a hotel room or a cafe instead. This may seem like a silly question, but it's important to know whether or not the applicant has access to a quiet, distraction-free work area with stable internet service, and if not, what accommodations can be made to help them succeed in the role. A coworking workplace, an ergonomic chair, and a desk are all examples. Find out specifically what they need to succeed as a remote worker.  


4. To what extent do you plan your day?


What you want to inquire about is time management and how to maintain productivity. You should find out when they start work and when they are most productive. The flexibility to choose your own schedule is a major selling point for remote employment. Freedom is great for some people, but it may be crippling for individuals who thrive with structure and a supervisor close by. Finding out whether an applicant can maintain productivity and independence despite working hours that vary from the standard 9 to 5 is thus helpful.  


5. Have you ever collaborated with a globally dispersed team? To what extent were you successful in doing so?


Information needs are easily met when all workers are present and functioning. They go into the office of a superior or colleague to inquire about anything. However, because of the different time zones in which dispersed teams operate, an employee may have to wait up to 12 hours for a response to a query. Interviewees should be questioned about how they have dealt with similar situations in the past, or how they propose to manage them going forward. Where will they look for the answers they require? This will provide you with an idea of their ingenuity and capacity for independent thought. 


6. What experience do you have with using various forms of remote communication?


Slack, Zoom, Microsoft Teams, and Google Drive are just some of the virtual technologies that scattered teams use to stay in touch and get work done. When working with a distributed team across time zones, these technologies are critical for facilitating asynchronous communication. Candidates' technological literacy and ability to implement various communication tactics may be evaluated by asking them about their experience with these tools.


7. What is the single most critical thing you can do to ensure the success of a remote work project? 


You may learn a lot about a candidate's approach to remote teamwork by asking them this question. Maybe they ask a lot of questions right from the off. Perhaps they keep their boss and other team members updated at each stage of the process to ensure that everyone is on the same page. Perhaps they are very good at setting and meeting deadlines. You may learn about their communication preferences, how they handle difficult tasks, and whether or not they will be a good team player from their responses. 


8. Describe an argument you had with a distant colleague. What did you do?


Even while this is a common interview topic for in-office positions, working remotely might provide its own unique challenges when it comes to resolving issues. A conversational aside might be misunderstood, while a hurriedly sent email could come out as rude. Investigate the candidate's potential responses. You want to see whether the applicant can take the initiative to solve problems and get back on track when things go wrong. 


9. Keeping yourself motivated when working from home is a challenge?


Working from home has its fair share of distractions. As soon as the mail carrier approaches, the dogs start barking. Having roommates or kids stop you at the precise time you get an idea that will make the project a success is a common frustration. Learn how a candidate responds when faced with these challenges. Some individuals thrive in the quiet of working from home because it allows them to immerse themselves in their job, while others require distraction-busting tools like noise-canceling headphones. Candidates should be equipped with methods they may use to maintain productivity and perform at their highest level. 


10. How do you unwind after a long day?


Since burnout is so common in the remote workforce, it's important to ask applicants how they separate work and personal life, which may be challenging when the workplace is also the kitchen. Gallup reports that throughout the epidemic, totally remote employees have had much higher rates of major burnout than in previous years. High-burnout workers are also 13% less confident in their abilities and 63% more likely to seek sick days. Find out whether they take lunch breaks and how long they are. Ask them how they unwind and whether they can leave work at the office when the day is done. Your workforce will be more efficient if they are healthy and content in their jobs.


One more thing: soft skills may end up being the determining factor


In interviews for remote jobs, the interviewer will likely focus on your interpersonal and communication abilities. You need to evaluate a candidate's experience and talents just like you would for in-office employment. You want someone with the skills to be effective while working from home. When it works well, everyone wins the employee, the business, and the community.

blogs.blogTitle: Five Proven Ways to Avoid Hiring the Wrong Person Based on a First Impression
blogs.blogSummary: This is the everyday manifestation of the primal friend vs. foe response that occurs in the interview setting. We recruit individuals who underperform because we
blogs.blogDescp:
Most recruiting blunders may be traced back to the influence of a candidate's first impression. Why? Because we are more likely to ask simple inquiries when we have an immediate positive impression of a person. When we have an immediate unfavorable reaction, we tend to probe further. Simply said, we want (often unconsciously) to back up our initial judgment.


This is the everyday manifestation of the primal friend vs. foe response that occurs in the interview setting. We recruit individuals who underperform because we prioritize their presentation above their performance. We also pass up on great performers because they have less polished presentation abilities. The following table provides a concise overview of this twofold adverse effect.


So, what's the solution? To begin, we must shift the emphasis from display to performance. The red arrow in this example is horizontal rather than descending. You won't believe how easy and crucial this adjustment is to make. The procedure is as follows:


First, Have a conversation by phone


By minimizing the influence of initial impressions, bias may be drastically decreased. The emphasis of a phone interview is understandably on previous job experience and notable achievements. Investigate the projects and teams the individual is assigned to and see whether they are expanding in scope and significance as part of the phone screen, and find out why the person moved roles and if the transition was effective. If all of the above checks up and you decide to bring this individual in for an in-person interview, your first opinion of them will likely fade.


Second, To improve neutrality in your interviews, try scripting the first few questions


Do not decide whether to hire someone during the first 30 minutes of an interview. This counteracts our natural inclination to ask our friends and family members questions designed to falsely validate their expertise while asking our enemies questions designed to falsely expose their ineptitude. Sample scripts for the first thirty to sixty minutes of an interview may be found in the appendix of The Essential Guide for Hiring and Getting Hired.


Third, while you're in the interview, use logical inversion to retrain yourself


When we meet someone for the first time, our biological instincts take over. When we encounter people we like, our friend vs. foe reaction encourages us to relax and be more open-minded, whereas the opposite occurs when we meet people we don't like. The first step in overcoming this kind of prejudice is realizing that it's happening and then doing the opposite of what comes easily. In this situation, we should be more trusting and open-minded toward individuals we don't immediately like, and more cautious of those we do. In this approach, judgments may be made about each person fairly.


Fourth, Turn around your viewpoint by a full circle


One strategy for reducing the significance of the "honeymoon effect" in hiring is to prioritize team performance above individual qualifications. Start by reviewing their work history and asking this team-related question, then go on to the more specific inquiries below:


  • Tell me about a recent, significant achievement your team made.
  • Who were the members of the team, and what did they do specifically?
  • When did the project take place, and what did you do for it? How did this function evolve as the project progressed?
  • How did you get picked for the team, if at all, and did you have any say in who else was included?
  • Was the team successful in accomplishing its goals?
  • How would you rate your impact on the team? How did you officially get this recognition?
  • Whom did you have the biggest impact on? Did you mentor any athletes? Were you coached by anyone?
  • Have you been rewarded formally for your participation on this team? If so, what kind of rewards have you received?


This sort of inquiry and fact-finding may tell you a lot about a candidate's ability to work in a team even by itself. The candidate's team projects' development rates may be seen by asking the same question for several important successes over many time periods.


The candidate's character may be gleaned a lot from this pattern. The team's rapid expansion in terms of members, responsibilities, and visibility reflects the high regard in which they are held by the company's upper management. A person's ability to engage with customers, suppliers, and executives, as well as their cross-functional and strategic viewpoint, may be confirmed by learning how and why they were picked.


Fifth, Evaluate first reactions when the interview has concluded


First impressions may be crucial to your professional success, but you should evaluate their significance when you're not emotionally invested in the outcome. Ask yourself, in an impartial manner, if your initial impression of the applicant will aid or hinder their success on the job. If you can give a candidate some time to calm down after you have an unfavorable response to them, you may find out that your first impression was unfounded. On the other hand, you may find that a candidate's initial promise isn't sustained after more investigation.


To improve impartiality and cut down on hiring blunders, try giving everyone an identical set of questions and waiting 30 to 60 minutes to provide a yes or no answer. You can immediately determine whether the prospect is a great performer and why by looking at their teamwork abilities. You may safely avoid hiring the wrong individuals and hiring the right ones by following these steps.

blogs.blogTitle: For a Powerful Online Community, Focus on Quality, Not Quantity
blogs.blogSummary: For a Powerful Online Community, Focus on Quality, Not Quantity: If you're a recruiter, a strong network is even more valuable since it helps you find qualified.
blogs.blogDescp:
The people in your network are significant resources for overcoming obstacles and advancing in your chosen field. Because of this, over 80% of working professionals say networking is critical to finding and taking advantage of new employment chances. However, more than a third of them had trouble finding common ground when getting back in touch. 


I've spent a lot of time pondering the potential of a network throughout my five years at Jobcart. If your network is made up of individuals you really know, it is authentic and useful, and each link has the potential to advance your career in some way, whether it is via a direct introduction, a referral, or some other means.


If you're a recruiter, a strong network is even more valuable since it helps you find qualified applicants for available jobs faster and more easily.


Coffee meets and other forms of in-person networking have been temporarily suspended because to the epidemic. But even in this far-flung modern world, personal connections are as vital as ever, particularly in the recruiting industry. Here are five ways to make the most of Jobcart and become more visible in your candidate search and online networking.


1. Quality should always be prioritized before quantity


The quality of the people in your network is more valuable than the quantity of those in it.

Instead of sending requests to individuals you don't know, recruiters should target people they know you can trust, such as family, friends, and current and previous coworkers. Jobcart restricts the number of invitations a user may send out each week since members have complained about receiving requests for connections from persons with whom they have never engaged. 


Recruiters may find the best possible applicants with the aid of Jobcart's other Talent Solutions solutions. New studies reveal that recruiters' success rates improve when they personalize their InMails to each prospect. The response rate to InMails that do not follow a template is around 20% greater than that of InMails that do. Similarly, response rates for individual InMails are roughly 20% higher than those for mass-sent InMails. 


2. When submitting a friend request, include a brief, handwritten remark


We've learned that the most effective method of connecting with someone is to send them a tailored note explaining why you'd want to get in touch with them. If you haven't spoken to this individual in a while, you might try reinforcing a common interest and starting a discussion by mentioning something specific about your first encounter. 


Another suggestion, if you're trying to meet new individuals, is to follow them. Then, you may start a dialogue with them on the Feed by commenting on the articles they provide. Following a conversation, you may take the next step in developing this connection by sending a connection request and a short message. 


3. Use your connections to make new ones


Everybody needs introductions to get forward in their careers; they are the key to opening doors and the lifeblood of any successful network. 


Anyone in your Jobcart network might be asked to refer you to their second-degree contacts, or individuals they know who know the person you're looking for a job with. 


In order to help your connection understand why you're asking for an introduction, we've noticed that it's helpful to provide some background on why you're interested in meeting that individual. I don't know. They may even come back with suggestions for more potential contacts, increasing the likelihood that you'll meet many candidates rather than just one. Sending them a little snippet that they may use to provide their connection with some background information is another good idea.


When someone introduces you to someone, thank them and offer to do the same for them in the future. 


One last thought: focus on quality rather than quantity


Recruiters may interact with job seekers via a variety of channels, one of which is through jobcart relationships. You may reach out to folks you don't already know by sending them InMails or requesting that they send you a note. We suggest prioritizing quality over quantity in each of these situations.


Building and maintaining professional relationships may have far-reaching benefits, from improving your skills as a recruiter to expanding your career options in talent acquisition.
blogs.pageTitle: For a Powerful Online Community, Focus on Quality, Not Quantity
blogs.pageKeywords: For a Powerful Online Community, Focus on Quality, Not Quantity
