blogs.blogTitle: 12 Most Important Interview Questions for Hiring a Recruiter
blogs.blogSummary: How can you determine whether a candidate would excel as a recruiter? To assist you in making this choice, we have compiled a list of twelve excellent interviews...
blogs.blogDescp:
Any competent recruiter will tell you that people are the most important aspect of any company and that acquiring and retaining top talent is essential to long-term success.


Therefore, it is only natural that talent acquisition managers place a premium on the individuals they employ. They would be wise to do so, since doing so may greatly benefit their company.


So, how can you determine whether a candidate would excel as a recruiter? To assist you in making this choice, we have compiled a list of twelve excellent interview questions to ask when selecting a recruiter.


1. How did you get along with your previous boss when you were being hired?


Maintaining solid connections with HR is the single most important aspect of recruiting success. And it involves the recruiter being an active participant in the hiring process rather than a passive one.

You may learn a lot about a candidate's interpersonal skills by listening to them describe their interactions with their previous hiring manager (or managers, if there were more than one).


Do these seem like authoritarian settings in which the recruiting manager makes all the decisions and the recruiter follows orders? Or, they may talk about how they both contributed equally to the recruiting process and quickly established themselves as leaders in the partnership. A candidate who will let each hiring manager operate as a dictator is not what you want. Instead, search for someone who has a firm philosophy on how to conduct that relationship.


2. What is your policy for dealing with unsuccessful job applicants?


Motivating factors for recruiters often include the number and speed with which they fill open positions. Therefore, some recruiters are so focused on moving the recruiting process forward that they neglect to provide the prospects they reject a positive experience.


Your company's employer image (and maybe consumer brand) might suffer if you hire recruiters in this manner.


The top recruiters, on the other hand, will make it very evident that they deliver excellent service to every prospect. This might include contacting all applicants, even if they don't go on to the next round, and calling unsuccessful interviewees individually. Pay attention to the recruiter's responses to see whether they demonstrate a dedication to keeping applicants informed at all times.


3. Give me two examples of how you've recently utilized data to improve your recruitment efforts.


Using data is becoming crucial in the hiring process. It's also a great method for recruiters to "swim upstream" and become more strategic partners with their hiring managers by informing them of the best places to find talent and what characteristics they should be looking for in potential candidates.


A strong applicant should be able to recall the last two times they utilized data in their recruitment efforts, such as when they consulted a Talent Pool Report from Jobcart Talent Insights. Candidates that struggle to provide concrete instances of their use of data in the recruitment process often aren't making full use of all the resources available to them.


4. Give an example of a time when you employed someone who turned out to be a poor fit for the position. 


Discuss the difficulties you encountered and the lessons you took away from them.

It's not always a home run to employ someone. Bad hiring is inevitable and may be quite costly for a company. 


Candidates who admit to making errors throughout the hiring process provide valuable insight into their character. Do they take responsibility for their mistakes or try to place the blame on someone else? What did they take away from it, and how will they use it in future situations like this? You may learn a lot about a candidate's approach to learning from their mistakes by looking at their responses to these questions. 


5. Explain the steps you're doing to develop your own brand and the significance of doing so.


The days of just posting positions online or sending InMails to eligible applicants are long gone. Recruiters need to start interacting with potential candidates online so they can find jobs more easily in the future.


The strength of a recruiter's Jobcart profile is crucial since it serves as the initial point of contact with candidates. Jobcart users that demonstrate an awareness of the value of cultivating a strong personal brand by creating and sharing content, joining communities, and engaging in the discussion are in high demand.


It's the same with other types of social media. A job candidate's lack of a professional social media presence outside Jobcart isn't a necessary cause for disqualification, but it is worth paying attention to those who do.


6. how do you rate the quality of new hires?


Given that the most brilliant brains in the field haven't settled on a definitive solution (yet), this is a quintessential "there's no right answer" issue. Defining and effectively assessing the quality of hiring is a persistent problem for personnel executives, and there is currently no silver bullet to address this issue.


However, almost everyone agrees that the quality of hiring is the most critical indicator in the recruitment process, despite the fact that no one can agree on a common definition. Therefore, a competitive job applicant should have an opinion on the topic or at least a hypothesis about the best way to evaluate it.


The depth of their thinking process and willingness to tackle the industry's toughest difficulties may be inferred from their response. Perhaps they will have found the answer.


7. Give me an example of a time when a highly qualified individual turned down a job offer. What insight did you get from this experience?


There's always the "one that got away" in the recruiting industry. The greatest recruiters, however, don't wallow in failure but instead, seek to understand why a prospect turned down an offer and how they may improve for the future.


Perhaps the applicant was initially enthusiastic about the position but later had reservations. Perhaps they didn't feel fully accepted by the culture. Perhaps they were offered something greater elsewhere. The recruiter now has the information necessary to take a more strategic approach during the offer stage.


8. What percentage of new recruits don't last?


Recruiters are typically under pressure to fill several positions fast, yet it is useless to fill a position quickly if the new worker departs within the first month. locate a recruiter that takes their time, even when time is of importance, to locate employees who will stay with the company for the long haul rather than just the short term.


A low figure here might suggest that the recruiter did not do a good job of screening prospects or that they failed to appropriately establish the candidates' expectations for the position, even if certain sectors tend to have a greater turnover than others.


9. Please explain how you would market our firm to potential employees.


You need to know whether a recruiter can set your firm apart from the competition in order to attract and retain top talent in an industry where many comparable businesses compete for the same pool of prospects. This question reveals how well they have researched your firm and also tests their ability to think creatively and critically.


You may also use this as a kind of evaluation by having the recruiter demonstrate their abilities by composing a sample outreach message. If they can distill what makes you special into an interesting narrative, you know you're dealing with a serious candidate.


10. How important is social media in your hiring process, if at all?


The best recruiters are continuously seeking new methods to attract and hire the most qualified candidates. That may include doing everything from monitoring market activity to using innovative methods of personnel selection. Getting a head start on the competition is as simple as using social media. Candidates' responses to your question on developing a personal brand on social media may have provided you some insight into their approach to using these newer technologies for both good and ill.


The candidate's own public social media profiles are one of the best ways to gain a sense of who they are as a person. Some recruiters scour them in search of candidates' hidden credentials and talents, while others use them to build relationships with candidates.  


However, there is a catch. Screening candidates' social media profiles is fraught with potential legal and ethical complications. Candidates should show that they know the difference between right and wrong.  


11. What publications do you read to keep up with the latest trends in the employment sector?


As a dynamic field, recruiting is always in need of fresh strategies and methods. Top recruiters are nimble enough to respond to shifts in the market because they keep tabs on developments in their field.


As long as a recruiter is regularly exposed to information that provokes them to consider how to improve as a recruiter, the specific periodicals, blogs, newsletters, or podcasts they consume are mostly irrelevant. In this context, an excellent follow-up interview question is what new trends in the sector have caught their attention. Both their familiarity with current events in the sector and their thoughts on fresh approaches to recruitment may be gleaned from this.


12. What do you find most satisfying about your work as a recruiter?


When a recruiter loves their work, it shows, and that makes the position more appealing to potential hires. Take note of the recruiter's motivations in answering these questions.


Perhaps they have a knack for connecting with others and taking an interest in the tales of potential hires. Or maybe they get a kick out of seeing others succeed after they've helped them obtain a job. While there is little room for error in this line of work, recruiters who aren't invested in the position because it's their dream job or because they like every aspect of it may struggle to inspire candidates.


In conclusion, if you hire a good recruiter, you'll have no trouble finding qualified workers.


Without the proper equipment, it is difficult to do anything. You may locate a recruiter who meets all your needs by using the questions we've compiled in our interview guide for recruiters. These questions cover a wide range of topics, from technical expertise to soft skills.


Finally, while searching for a recruiter, it's crucial to choose someone who is enthusiastic about working for your firm and who shows real interest in each applicant. If they have it, as well as the expertise, experience, and interest necessary to complete the work successfully, you'll be in good shape.

blogs.blogTitle: 15 Types of Jobs That Will Still Exist in 2030
blogs.blogSummary: There are a lot of professions that don't even exist yet that today's pupils will be doing in 2030. It turns out that many of these occupations will be spawned...
blogs.blogDescp:
There are a lot of professions that don't even exist yet that today's pupils will be doing in 2030. The issue on the minds of all talent acquisition teams is this: What exactly will those positions entail?


It turns out that many of these occupations will be spawned by currently developing technology, such as drones, renewable energy, autonomous vehicles, and cryptocurrency/blockchain innovations.


We consulted our crystal ball (and the internet) to come up with 15 of the most interesting (if not completely out there) careers that might arise in the near future.


1. Person Who Makes Organs


The New Zealand-based website Crimson Education speculates that scientists may develop methods to grow human organs and body parts from stem cells and other materials, some of which may not currently exist, due to the paucity of transplantable organs. Those with experience in molecular biology, tissue engineering, or biomedical engineering will be highly sought after by hiring managers.


2. Creating a trip in augmented reality


Based on ideas from films like Total Recall (memory implants of holidays) and Westworld (an amusement park run by Androids), AR trip creators will soon let clients experience whatever they can imagine. As stated in Cognizant's 21 Jobs of the Future, augmented reality journey builders will "design, write, create, calibrate, gamify, build, and — most importantly — personalize the next generation of mind-blowing stories and in-the-moment vignettes" for affluent customers. The post requires familiarity with MMORPG and a degree from an accredited film school.


3. Scheduling in the Metaverse


Ironically, some of the first employment made possible by the metaverse is in traditional retail; in early May, VR, and AR retailer Meta established a shop in California. In the future, the metaverse will also enhance the list of needed occupations provided by Jobcart. Additionally, "metaverse planner" will be a job title. According to the blog of Honeypot, a European platform for finding and hiring software talent, "[t]he Planner" will be responsible for "driving a strategic portfolio of opportunities from proof-of-concept to pilot to deployment." Planners should have an entrepreneurial mindset and background in management. And a metaverse education will be necessary for them.


4. Setup of Biofilms


Biofilms are disgusting, sticky, and difficult to deal with because they are communities of microorganisms that have adhered to damp surfaces. Eighty percent of all microbial illnesses originate in these scums of lakes and ponds. However, they are also great instruments for electricity generation, cleaning up oil spills, and treating sewage. The Canadian Scholarship Trust (CST) argues that "by coating certain surfaces in the bathroom and kitchen of homes, they will become key tools for environmentally friendly buildings," and that biofilm installers play a significant role in "retrofitting smart, energy-efficient buildings." Biofilm installers may one day place microorganisms in showers that combat bathroom mildew or, more generally, provide households with a live organism to digest waste.


5. Seismic foreseeing experts


The function of earthquake predictor has been a job of the future for at least 50 years, but many of the others on this list would have been unthinkable even a few years ago. In the 1970s, several researchers predicted that the technology to reliably detect earthquakes in advance was on the horizon. It has stayed in the same place for the last half-century: around the corner. Hopes that corporations and governments may soon begin employing forecasters have been bolstered by the rapid development of machine learning applied to seismology in recent years. Of course, you'll need some knowledge of geology and geophysics, but little magic would help, too.


6. Engineer of Ad Hoc Construction


Prosthetic limbs and experimental jet engines are now being made using 3D printing technology, which was formerly used to make novelty items like Yoda heads and keychains. Makeshift construction architects of the future will use 3D printing to build emergency shelters for victims of natural catastrophes and armed warfare (can't we manage to eradicate that by 2030?). According to CST, "3D printing will be able to print the parts needed to create small housing units, similar to trailers, in several hours or days," making it possible to swiftly assemble them for people in need. Engineers that specialize in temporary structures will often have training in both industrial design and structural engineering.


7. Auditing for algorithmic bias


From song recommendations on Spotify to potential matches on Tinder, algorithms influence many aspects of modern life. They also fuel the modern recruiting process, from the preparation of job listings for applicants to the selection of candidates via an applicant tracking system for use by recruiters. As the World Economic Forum puts it, "[G]iven the increasing statutory scrutiny on data," verification via audits will assist in guaranteeing the future workforce is also a fair workforce. Auditors specializing in algorithm bias will come from a computing or analytics background.


8. Rewilder


These revolutionary change agents have the power to convert a concrete jungle into a verdant belt. The goal of rewilders is to repair the damage done by the industrial revolution over the last two hundred years by restoring natural habitats and reintroducing native plant and animal populations. Experts in rewilding will most often come from the fields of agriculture, wildlife management, and ecology.


9. Manager of Human-Machine Collaboration


It's possible that no other job so squarely places its holder at the center of the workplace of the future as does that of a human-machine interface manager. According to Cognizant, "as a man-machine teaming manager," one must "identify tasks, processes, systems, and experiences that can be upgraded by newly available technologies and imagine new approaches, skills, interactions, and constructs." You will establish norms for how robots and humans should work together to complete a job. Requirements? Candidates with a degree in experimental psychology or neuroscience with experience in computer science, engineering, or human resources will be highly sought after by employers.


10. Expert in the field of digital currencies


The rising popularity of cryptocurrencies like Bitcoin, Litecoin, and Ethereum has given investors access to unregulated financial products with significant prospects and risks. "Digital currency advisors," as described by CST, "specialize in these currencies and show people how to manage their wealth by using the right balance of systems." As pointed out in this television commercial featuring Stephen Curry, digital currency experts are unlikely to have played professionally in the NBA.


11. Optimizer for air traffic using drones


Drones once considered a curiosity, are now being used in filmmaking, warfare, police work, package delivery, and more. They will permeate society entirely within the next decade. And someone has to keep an eye on their flight trajectories to make sure they don't cause full chaos. In the United States, governmental agencies like NASA and the Federal Aviation Administration are tackling this problem. Optimizers for drone traffic will deal with this on a regional scale.


12. Self-repairing automobile


Vehicles from Tesla, Waymo, General Motors, Hyundai, Cruise, Pony.ai, and Ford, among others, will operate without human input when they hit the market soon. The problem is that they won't fix themselves. And those who service vehicles will do so with a blend of computer savvy and the traditional joy of tinkering. Watson meets Mr. Goodwrench.


13. Manager of Intelligent Home Design


The House of Tomorrow used to be a regular attraction at World's Fairs. But the future is here today, and people can automate their houses' lights, temperatures, and security systems. According to the World Economic Forum, "the rise of smart home design managers will boom as homes are built — or retrofitted" with separate, voice-controlled entrances and soundproof home offices. These supervisors will be "home-schooled" in artificial intelligence (AI), robotics (Robotics), and residential architecture (Architecture).


14. Worker Agility in Supply Chain


Adapting quickly to changes in supply (where can I obtain components quicker, cheaper, of higher quality?) and demand (what customers are willing to pay) is becoming more important in today's interconnected, global economy. market forces (what's driving the unexpected interest in our goods in Southeast Asia?) and supply. Specifically, "[c]ompanies need people who constantly scout out new sources for materials and components and connect company supply lines on the fly to keep costs low and turnaround fast," as stated by CST. Candidates with a business degree and coursework in supply chain management and logistics would be of particular interest to employers. Veterans with SCM expertise will also be in great demand.


15. Waste management specialist; garbage architect


The United Nations estimates that global rubbish production is above 2 billion metric tons annually. We also have a serious lack of available garbage cans. Our future rubbish engineers, however, reply, "Rubbish!" to those who claim there is no escape from this problem. According to Thrillist, "Garbage designers... will be charged with coming up with clever methods to upcycle trash on a large scale, and manufacturers of everything from toys to clothes to furniture will hire them to find more efficient ways to use and reuse their byproducts." For individuals who want nothing more than to make the world a cleaner place, a foundation in materials science and industrial design will be essential.


In conclusion: The future of work has never seemed brighter

Do you remember the clumsy father of the Jetsons, George Jetson, from the space period cartoons by Hanna-Barbera? George put in his three hours at Spacely's Space Sprockets three times a week. His modern-day occupation? He was constantly switching on and off the Referential Universal Digital Indexer (and grumbling about how much work it was giving him).


The workplace of the future will be a lot more lively. Potential careers in demand include bio waste optimizers, plant therapists, memory surgeons, aquaponic fish farmers, and robotic counselors.

Is there a single promising career path? Someone to scout for quick-thinking, imaginative people to fill these positions.

blogs.blogTitle: Boost Employee Motivation and Retention with These 30 Ideas
blogs.blogSummary: Engaging work environment, encouraging managers, and development opportunities are just a few of the numerous elements that contribute to employee retention...
blogs.blogDescp:
It's more important than ever to hold on to star players at this time of Great Reshuffle. 

To begin, the annual cost of employee turnover in the United States is a staggering $1 trillion. The time it takes to find a replacement may range from half to double the employee's yearly income, and the expense of doing so can cause work to pile up.


There are, however, many other arguments in favor of preservation. Employee retention is a key factor in maintaining steady business output. Keep your most valuable information and expertise in-house. Worker morale rises and productivity soars as a result. The candidates are paying attention. When staff turnover is low, teams lack stability, motivation, and expertise. Any reasonable person would jump at the chance to work for such a corporation. 


Fair compensation, an engaging work environment, encouraging managers, and development opportunities are just a few of the numerous elements that contribute to employee retention. Still, by beginning to enhance even a handful of these, you may make your firm a place where people feel encouraged to join – and stay. We've come up with 30 effective measures to enhance retention. 



1. Consider suggestions from workers


Employees are more adamant that their opinions be valued and considered. When employees believe that company leadership values their input, productivity, and morale both increase. And that's one of the main factors in whether or not an employee stays with their present company.


2. Make sure that everyone at work feels like they have a place


To avoid further prejudice, over half of Black and Latinx workers have left their positions. Employees are more likely to do well in their employment when they have a sense of security and acceptance at work.


3. Allow employees more leeway in their schedules


The need for employees to have some control over when and when they clock in has been a consistent theme throughout the epidemic. Therefore, it is important to provide as much freedom as possible to your top staff. Employees who believe their employers provide sufficient opportunities for scheduling and other forms of personal autonomy are 2.1 times more likely to say they would suggest working for the firm to a friend. 


4. Fairly compensate your staff


Paying your workers properly will offer them plenty of motivation to remain, even if a better wage isn't always the primary reason they hunt for a new position. You should conduct a pay audit to identify and address any pay disparities, especially those that affect women and new moms. 


5. Provide one-time monetary bonuses


One-time incentives, work-from-home allowances, and assistance with student debt repayment may be offered in addition to the standard salary increase. Such perks have the potential to increase employee commitment and morale. 


6. Keep transparency about compensation


Because previously underpaid groups were disproportionately impacted by the epidemic, the issue of fair compensation for workers gained a lot of attention during the Great Reshuffle. By being open and honest about compensation, you can reduce the wage gap, improve working conditions, and boost morale. 


7. Give your workers and their families a comprehensive benefits package that includes coverage for mental health issues


Everyone has been affected by the epidemic, but you can help your staff by providing them with strong mental health benefits. A 2021 survey found that 86 percent of millennial workers were more inclined to remain with their current employer if it offered good mental health care.


8. Help working parents by providing them with access to daycare services


Women quit their jobs in droves during the pandemic because they couldn't find reliable childcare, highlighting the importance of childcare in retaining parents in the workforce. To help working parents, you may provide nursery facilities, backup care, and financial aid. 


9. Be up-front and honest about the job requirements while recruiting


During the hiring process, it is crucial to inform a candidate of any potential difficulties associated with the position or the organization. In this approach, the employee will not feel duped after being hired. They will have a higher chance of succeeding if they are prepared beforehand.


10. Make an excellent first impression with your new employee orientation


A new employee's impression of the firm is heavily influenced by the manner in which they are introduced to and welcomed into the workforce. Companies that do a good job of onboarding tend to have a positive reputation among employees and potential hires. Jobcart has a 45-day virtual onboarding checklist that might help you design a successful program. 


11. Match new hires with seasoned workers


A good mentor will make a newbie feel comfortable, provide direction, and be there to bounce ideas off of. There was a considerable difference in retention rates between those who participated in the mentorship program (72%) and those who did not engage (49%) according to a case study conducted by Sun Microsystems.


12. Conduct exit interviews with departing staff


A stay interview is a great way to find out from workers what will encourage them to remain on staff. What do they want to accomplish throughout their time working for your company? How can they do their tasks better? By catching issues early on, you may increase retention, engagement, and corporate culture with well-conducted stay interviews.


13. Promote "boomerangs" (returning customers)


A departing worker might be encouraged to reconsider their decision to leave by conducting an upbeat departure interview. Some employers are using the promise of rapid vesting in long-term compensation plans as an incentive for former workers to return. The act of welcoming back former workers will have a positive effect on the morale of the present personnel.


14. Increase Mobility Within


Employees who have the chance to advance their careers or try out various roles inside the firm are more likely to remain with the organization. According to Jobcart's findings, workers stay 41% longer at organizations with robust internal mobility programs compared to those with limited or nonexistent programs. Establishing a formalized internal recruitment program allows you to proactively approach workers who might be a good fit for new opportunities. 


15. Create career pathways for workers to follow so they can see their future with the organization and make preparations for it


More people stick around when they know what they're working towards and why it's important. Managers should meet with their staff at least once every quarter, twice a year, or once a year to discuss the workers' professional goals. 


16. Make sure there are lots of training and development chances


Workers who take the time to keep their abilities fresh are more likely to be employed and promoted. Because of this, the turnover rate is 53% lower for organizations with good ratings for staff training. According to Jobcart data, if given the chance, 94% of workers would remain with the same business for a long. 


17. Utilize the talents your staff already has


Employees who do not believe their abilities are being put to good use are 10 times more likely to hunt for a new job, according to Jobcart's 2022 Workplace Learning Report. Employees want to know that their contributions are valued, and they are open to learning new skills if necessary. 


18. Prompt transparent and efficient administration as Goal 


HR experts have heard the old adage, "People don't quit their jobs, they quit their managers," many times. Statistics gathered by Jobcart corroborate this, revealing that businesses with a reputation for "open and effective management" also have a greater level of employee loyalty. It's important to have managers that motivate their teams, thus it's worth it to invest in their education.


19. Maintain interest among workers


When workers are invested in their work, they feel valued by their employers and are more likely to be pleased with their careers overall. They have a far lower turnover rate than disengaged employees, which is five times lower.


20. Encourage a good work-life balance


Work-life balance has surpassed competitive pay and benefits as the most important factor for job applicants, according to a Jobcart poll scheduled for 2021. When employees feel overworked and underappreciated, they may consider leaving their current positions for greener pastures. Employees may achieve a better work-life balance if they are encouraged to avoid working long hours, take more time off, and disable their email notifications after business hours. 


21. Establish a system of regular check-ins with staff


Managers can't just claim to have an open-door policy anymore. Now more than ever, managers need to have ongoing, recurrent meetings with their teams to discuss issues like workload and morale. The morale of an organization's staff may be improved by encouraging open communication between employees and management. 


22. Constantly Seek Opinions


Take the temperature of the organization on a regular basis by conducting surveys and listening to employee input. Then, after gathering input, make adjustments accordingly. When workers believe their opinions matter, they are 4.6 times less likely to consider leaving their current position. 


23. Recognise and reward staff for their efforts in-house


Whether it's for completing a large project on time or celebrating five years with the same company, employees like being acknowledged for their efforts. Sixty-eight percent of respondents to an SHRM poll noted that their company's recognition program contributed to employee retention. 


24. Provide time off (sabbatical)


AARP, Intel, Clif Bar, and Patagonia are just a few of the firms that provide their workers paid time off after a certain number of years of service. This is a huge untapped resource for growing your business's reputation and customer base. For example, Clif Bar: The sabbatical is regarded as one of the most valuable benefits by employees, and the firm has a historically low turnover rate of 3%. 


25. Don't stray from your genuine calling


Employees are increasingly seeking organizations that share their beliefs and are working to improve society. For this reason, it's crucial to show - in a credible manner — that your business cares about more than just making money. Customer success stories or case studies are an excellent tool for demonstrating to staff how their efforts have benefited customers. 


26. Improve your health and wellness initiatives 


Deloitte found that 80 percent of employees see wellness as critical to the success of their company in 2021. In order to improve the health of your employees, you may want to provide them with stress management courses, online exercise classes (or reimbursement for in-person classes), financial management webinars, and healthy recipe swaps. Incorporating time for self-care into the workday has been shown to improve employee satisfaction. 


27. Share information with your staff


Being honest with workers about the state of the business and its future is especially crucial now when so much is up in the air. Workers' worries and concerns may be mitigated by consistent communication, especially during times of transition.


28. Make ties between workers 


As soon as a new hire is brought on board, efforts should be made to introduce them to the rest of the team via mentoring, group activities, and other shared learning opportunities. Motivate your staff to complete initiatives as a team. If you have a best buddy at work, you're seven times less likely to quit your job. 


29. Ample Paid Time Off


We understand that not every business is capable of doing this. One of the best benefits is telling workers, "We trust that you'll take off any time you need or want, and still get your work done." One possible difficulty with unlimited PTO is that employees may use fewer vacation days than they otherwise would if they were confined to a certain number of days per year. So, establish norms that reward and promote time off. Taking more vacation time is associated with greater professional and personal success and satisfaction. 


30. Show compassion


Almost everyone has had a tough time over the last several years, making it tough to consistently show up to work and do well. But when managers and employees alike make an effort to empathize, the atmosphere at work changes for the better. Employees who believe their contributions are valued and appreciated work harder, take more risks and support the success of their coworkers. As a result, learning retention is enhanced.

blogs.blogTitle: Top 10 Time-Management Strategies for Recruiters
blogs.blogSummary: Time-Management Strategies for Recruiters: A process for selecting which things to do first is crucial to developing a prioritization plan. While each of the...
blogs.blogDescp:
The present employment market may be a candidate's paradise, but it's causing recruiters to experience a time management nightmare due to the increase in their workload. More jobs need to be filled, more people need to be interviewed, and more requirements and expectations must be met. Due to a lack of available recruiters, talent acquisition professionals often have to take on additional responsibilities.


When your plate is too full, how do you handle the situation? Putting in more hours is not the solution. Working too many hours might have negative effects on your health and happiness outside of work. The best option is to maximize the effectiveness of your current work hours. 


This requires prioritizing which activities to immediately begin working on, and which to postpone or abandon. To complete your seemingly endless list of responsibilities, leadership expert Beate Chelette in Los Angeles recommends learning the steps involved in setting priorities. Managing one's time well is just as crucial. You will never complete the jobs that provide the greatest results if you spend too much time on those that are lower on the priority list.


Recruiters, like everyone else, may benefit from better prioritization tactics and time management approaches, even while additional time can't be added to the day.


A universal method of time management does not exist.


A process for selecting which things to do first is crucial to developing a prioritization plan. While each of the following techniques takes a somewhat unique tack, you could find at least one that works for you.


1. Limit the number of tasks on your daily list 


There's no way you can complete a list of twenty tasks in a single day.  To narrow down your options, The Personal MBA: Master the Art of Business author Josh Kaufman suggests adopting the Most Important Tasks (MIT) technique. 


Choose no more than three items from your list that you will work on exclusively today. Pick the actions that will have the most positive effect on your top priorities. John recommends asking, "What are the things that would make a huge difference if I got them done today?" to help you zero in on those must-dos. 


If finding applicants for three open jobs is your top priority, you may spend all day doing research and crafting job descriptions. You might put off filling future jobs until another time.


2. Don't feel the need to rush through life


It's easy to make the mistake of trying to fulfill every request at once when there are so many pressing needs. Dwight Eisenhower's namesake concept, the "Ikeapler Matrix," may be used to reframe your thoughts in a more productive way. 


With this method, you'll want to classify jobs in these ways: 


  • Do the most pressing and crucial things first.
  • If a job is crucial but not time-sensitive, put it on the calendar for later.
  • Delegate or outsource work that needs doing immediately but isn't mission-critical.
  • Don't bother with it if it's not an absolute must. 


Amazon's senior operations manager, Joe Capel, claims to have utilized the Eisenhower Matrix to teach every team he has ever managed. This is a "great tool that helps to put things in perspective and manage time more effectively," as Joe puts it.


3. Evaluate your work


Author Alan Lakein proposes the ABC Method as a means of ranking the relevance of various endeavors.


  • Projects having strict due dates or high organizational significance get an A. 
  • A grade of B would be given for work that is crucial in the big picture but not urgent right now. 
  • A grade of C would be given to activities that are nice to undertake but don't make much of a dent in the grand scheme of things. 


Focusing on, and completing, A-level work should take up most of your day. When A chores are finished, go on to B ones. Schedule the C duties for when you know you won't be too busy with the A and B activities. 


If making 30 InMails to prospective employees is your top priority for the day, then focus on it before moving on to anything else. 


4. Focus on what you're good at


The 80/20 rule, also known as the Pareto principle, is another method for prioritizing tasks. This principle states that, on average, just 20 percent of your efforts will produce 80 percent of your outcomes. Find out which of your actions result in the greatest financial gain, the greatest increase in customer happiness, or the most impact on the KPI that matters most to you. Start with those things, then.


Sourcing duties should frequently be prioritized if your recruitment superpower is finding exceptional applicants in places where others haven't looked. 


5. Prioritize work over personal life


Leading a company-wide initiative to replace the CEO is one such undertaking with game-changing potential. The Jobcart Learning program recommends prioritizing tasks related to these initiatives. When it seems like everything should be a top priority, how to set goals? Dorie Clark, a management professor at Duke and Columbia, advises her students to pursue projects that will have a positive influence on the company's operations or lead to career advancement if they wish to adopt a growth and impact strategy.  


You can get more done if you learn to better manage your time.  


You may make better use of your time by following these suggestions. 


6. Master your inbox 


Even the most well-planned day may be derailed by an unexpected influx of email messages. Dave Crenshaw, an author and leadership coach, suggests setting up certain periods each day to deal with email and deleting or filing away messages after reading them unless you need to respond. That implies you can't be checking your inbox at all hours of the night and day.


But what if you have thousands upon thousands of unread messages in your inbox? Dave offers this advice in his Time Management Tips course on Jobcart Learning. Discover an earlier date and declare "email bankruptcy" effective as of that time. Then, file away any messages before that date. That way, you may clear up your inbox without losing access to the messages themselves in the very improbable event that you later require them.


7. Find out what you're doing that is wasting your time


One common piece of budgeting advice is to keep a record of all the places your money is already going. In the same vein, the same words of wisdom may be applied to the passage of time. In her Jobcart Learning course, Arianna Huffington recommends that people first make a pie chart detailing how they now spend their days. Thrive, Third Edition by Arianna Huffington is all about focusing and letting go. When an app is available to do the sorting for you, you may discover that you are spending two hours every week on meaningless correspondence. By letting go of them, you'll have more time for what really matters. 


8. Make a schedule and stick to it


Three hours of focused effort on a single task might leave you feeling accomplished, but only if it doesn't divert your attention from another, equally pressing activity. Time blocking is a technique wherein you schedule blocks of time for each of your top priorities for the day. 


Asking yourself questions such, as "How long can I work before needing a break?" and "How much deep work is involved in this task?" will help you determine the optimal duration of a time block. A recruiting report, for instance, may warrant a longer time commitment than social media recruitment postings. The secret to successful time management, however, is as follows: When the timer goes off, you have to quit what you're doing and go on to the next activity.


Email and phone correspondence are two examples of time-consuming tasks that are best handled in bulk to avoid interrupting your day. For non-urgent emails, set aside 30 minutes of your time. Time should be scheduled for breaks and recharging as well.


9. Make use of a timer


The Pomodoro Technique is a well-liked time management strategy in which tasks are completed in 25-minute blocks. Each 25-minute work period is called a "pomodoro," the Italian word for tomato, after the tomato-shaped kitchen timer that software designer Francesco Cirillo was using when he invented this concept.


Put 25 minutes on the clock. Focus on a high-priority job until the bell sounds. After each Pomodoro, give yourself a five-minute pause. After four Pomodoros, you should take a lengthier pause of 15 to 30 minutes. 


Batch together any little chores that can be completed in one Pomodoro session. In addition, if you find that completing a single work takes more than four Pomodoros, consider splitting it up into subtasks so that you may still make progress on your projects.


"Setting timers has made me more productive," Phyllis Njoroge, a customer growth product manager and speaker in San Francisco, says. "If you want to take your productivity by timers game to the next level, check out the Pomodoro technique," which involves utilizing many timed intervals to complete a task.


10. Do the little things right now


Immediately return a candidate's call if they have a query that can be answered in less than a minute. The One-Minute Rule, popularised by happiness researcher and author Gretchen Rubin, is based on this idea. Don't let yourself become buried by a mountain of work because you put off doing something that will take a minute. You won't need to invest any time or mental effort planning for the completion of those chores that take less than a minute each.


Another applicant must be vetted, another position must be filled, and another phone call must be returned. You can get more done and still have time for friends and family if you only learn to prioritize and organize your time better.

blogs.blogTitle: An Inclusive Workplace: 15 Terms Related to Gender Identity
blogs.blogSummary: Gender Identity: The employment and recruiting processes may be particularly hostile environments for people with various identities. Over half of the trans...
blogs.blogDescp:
You've certainly read an article or two on the differences between male and female applicants, including how they conduct themselves throughout the job hunt, the language they use in interviews, and their approach to negotiating salary. Those pieces, however, just scratch the surface.


For the simple reason that there are not just two sexes. In terms of gender, there is no simple yes or no.


The failure to make this difference may lead to the exclusion of a huge and sometimes underappreciated segment of the workforce. roughly 0.6% of the adult population in the United States are transgender people. This amounts to roughly 1.4 million people. A recent poll of the LGBTQ+ population in the UK indicated that 13% of respondents were transgender. 2.7% of Minnesota's high school students are transgender, genderqueer, or gender fluid (I'll define these words in a little), or they don't know how they identify.


The employment and recruiting processes may be particularly hostile environments for people with various identities. Over half of the trans and nonbinary Britons surveyed in 2018 reported hiding their identity at work due to concerns about discrimination, while a study from 2015 indicated that trans people living in California are three times as likely to be jobless as the general adult population. As of right now, transgender persons in the United States have no legal recourse against discrimination in the workplace.


However, there is plenty that businesses can do to foster a more diverse and welcoming work environment for all employees, regardless of their gender. This will improve the company's talent brand and send a strong message to prospective employees from marginalised groups. Companies have generally adopted the first step of raising awareness, gaining an understanding, and striving to use the appropriate terminology when it comes to racial and ethnic diversity, but the same is not true for gender.


Therefore, here are 15 phrases connected to gender identification that each person participating in the recruiting process should know. This is by no means a full list, and as you will see, the meanings of many of the phrases are not fixed.


 1. Gender Role


The oversimplification taught in health classes that one's "true" gender is determined by one's "biological sex" is a major contributor to students' lack of clarity on the topic of gender identity. That's not how easy it is. There is no necessary relationship between sex and gender.


At birth, your genitalia serves to determine your sex. Sex isn't always as cut-and-dry as is often imagined, as we'll see when talking about intersex people (see 12).


Gender is more than just a label; it's the way you identify as a man, a woman, neither, or anything in between. A person's gender identity, then, refers to that person's innermost sense of who they are. People are happier and more productive when they can be themselves at work without worrying about being harassed or embarrassed.


2. Gender Role Performance


Gender expression refers to the outward manifestations of a person's gender identity. This may be seen in their choice of clothing, hairstyle, demeanour, and even tone of speech. Some people's expressions of male and female identities are very congruent with cultural norms, while others are more flexible or nonconformist (see 10).


If a person does not feel safe publicly disclosing their gender identity, their external look and behaviour may not be indicative of their genuine gender identity.


3. Sexual Preference


We may have a gender identity that is distinct from our sexual orientation. YouTuber Brendan Jordan puts it this way: "Sexuality is who you go to bed with, and gender identity is who you go to bed as." However, it is important to discuss here since discussions of gender identity and sexual orientation are frequently intertwined.


Transgender people may be stereotyped as homosexual because of common misconceptions about their sexual orientation that stem from their gender identification. Trans women may be attracted to other women and identify as lesbians, males and identify as straight, neither sex nor gender attracts them and identify as asexual, etc.


Avoid making assumptions about someone's sexual orientation or gender in general.


4. Queer


Many members of the LGBTQ+ (lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer, intersex, and asexual) community use the umbrella word "queer" to refer to persons who don't identify as straight or cisgender.


Queer has several meanings, similar to many of the other terms on this list. Avoid using it so there is little chance of misinterpretation; it is widely used as a slur against the LGBTQ+ population. However, many people who identify as LGBTQ+ want to reclaim the term by identifying as queer.


5. cisgender


People who identify with their natal sex are said to be "cisgender," or cis. One is considered cisgender if they are biologically female or male and self-identify as such.


6. Cishet


To be cisgender and heterosexual is to be called a cishet.


7. Non-Binary


People who do not identify as either male or female fall under the category of "nonbinary." Nonbinary people may also identify with other categories, such as genderqueer (see number 8), in addition to their nonbinary identity.


Due to the prevalence of binary pronouns in common speech, some nonbinary people, depending on where they fall on the gender spectrum, choose either "he/him" or "she/her" pronouns. Some people may prefer that you use the gender-neutral "they/them" or "ze/hir" (pronounced zed and here). These pronouns are used in the same manner as the gendered pronouns "he" and "him" are used.


It's important to make an effort when someone gives you their pronouns to use them consistently, even if it takes some getting accustomed to them. It's OK to inquire, "What are your correct pronouns?" if you find yourself unsure. If you're concerned that inquiring about someone else's pronouns would come across as intrusive, you can ease your mind by providing your own first.


8. Genderqueer


Some persons who do not strictly identify as male or female use the word "genderqueer" to describe themselves. Since it originated in queer activism in the 1990s and is strongly linked to the push for nonbinary gender identities to be recognised and legitimised, it varies from nonbinary more in connotation than in definition.


9. Transgender


People who identify as transgender do not identify with their biological gender. Some transgender people may not transition at all or may postpone transitioning for a number of reasons; others may be in the process of transitioning to their particular gender identification.


The term "transgender" is often abbreviated to "trans." However, the word "tranny" is a slur, thus your antiharassment policy should make it quite clear that your organisation does not tolerate discriminatory language.


Although there are transgender people who don't choose between male and female identities and who thus use "they/them" pronouns, the vast majority of trans people still do. Don't misgender a person by using the wrong pronouns (remember, it's OK to inquire about pronouns if you're uncertain).


10. Gender Nonconforming


Those who do not present as the "norm" for their assigned gender are said to be gender non-conforming. Being gender nonconforming does not exclude being cisgender. Some cisgender women choose to adopt more masculine features, such as shortening their hair and wearing men's clothing.


11. Genderfluid


People who identify as genderfluid experience shifts in their gender identification throughout time. They may shift their gender presentation from day to day to match their evolving sense of self. One day they may show as male, the next as female, and the day after that as neither.


Some persons who identify as gender nonconforming use the pronouns "they/them," while others prefer to be referred to using the pronouns associated with their identified gender, and still others use just one gendered pronoun. If you are uncertain, just ask, and if someone has a preference, you should strive to respect it.


12. Intersex


Another broad category, intersex people are those whose biological sex does not conform to the traditional male and female norms. This may include a person's physical characteristics, reproductive capabilities, and/or chromosomal arrangement. An intersex person may, for instance, have male-typical internal anatomy but female-typical outward genitalia.


Doctors will have to use their best judgement when deciding what sex an intersex baby is. This sex designation may not reflect the person's actual gender. However, many intersex persons still identify as male or female, therefore the words intersex and nonbinary are not synonymous.


One in one hundred newborns are not classified as either male or female, says the Intersex Society of North America. This fact has been used by many to argue that our society's binary views on sexuality and gender are too narrow.


13. Agender or Non-gendered


The agender community self-identifies as being non-binary. Nonbinary people (those who don't identify as either male or female) are also sometimes called gender-neutral people.


Asexual people may manifest in a variety of ways. Although most people don't like it, some people choose to dress in a gender-neutral way. Being agender is not the same as being androgynous, and vice versa.


14. Questing About Gender


Given the binary nature of our society, it might be difficult to learn about and accept one's own gender. That's why some people go through what's called "gender questioning," a time when they doubt their gender and experiment with other ways of expressing themselves within that identity.


15. Change in Gender


When a transgender person decides to start living their life in accordance with their gender identity rather than their natal sex, they are said to be "transitioning." Hormone replacement treatment and surgical procedures are possibilities. Changing your name officially, requesting that others use the appropriate pronouns, and wearing clothing that reflects your gender identification are all examples of possible next stages in the transition process.


This may happen at any age, and as transgender problems gain greater acceptance, more and more seniors are comfortable making the change. Financial considerations are frequently a key part of a trans person's choice to transition or not, so if your company's health insurance includes medical procedures like hormone therapy and gender reassignment surgery, be sure to let applicants and workers know.


Open your thoughts and don't worry about making blunders


After being taught that there are only two genders, hearing about the full range of gender expression might be shocking.

If you're trying to create a more welcoming environment at work, it's okay if you confuse concepts or forget the precise definition of a phrase now and then. Accepting the fact that there is always more to learn is crucial. Be humble enough to admit your mistakes, kind enough to treat everyone with respect, and receptive enough to learn about the perspectives of people whose genders differ from your own.

blogs.blogTitle: Improving Neurodiversity in the Workplace: The Role of Remote Work
blogs.blogSummary: The Role of Remote Work: How can you better accommodate neurodiverse personnel at a distance? Read on for more information- Recognize the pluses of working...
blogs.blogDescp:
The advent of remote work has opened up new doors for many individuals, including working parents and those who live in areas with fewer job options. However, employees with dyslexia, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), Tourette syndrome, autism spectrum disorder (ASD), or other neurological differences have profited greatly. 


In-person workplace cultures have always been a poor match for neurodivergent individuals. Some people may feel sensory overload due to loud noises, intense lighting, and pungent odors. Team meetings may be difficult for people with cognitive difficulties because of the need to read nonverbal clues and understand nuances in communication. People on the autism spectrum typically struggle with the stress of change and the lack of consistency that characterizes many commutes. 


Employees who identify as neurodiverse and persons with a wide variety of impairments have requested remote work as an accommodation for years, and have frequently been turned down, even when their cases went to court. As a result, many people were forced to sit out of the labor market. The vast majority of the 5.6 million individuals in the United States who have ASD are either jobless or underemployed, according to studies. While 32% of those with ASD in the UK are in the labor force, just 16% have full-time jobs. 


For businesses, this is a golden chance. Individuals with cognitive disabilities often exhibit strengths in areas such as innovation, originality, attention to detail, and the ability to maintain concentration for long periods of time.


How can you better accommodate neurodiverse personnel at a distance? Read on for more information:


Recognize the pluses of working from home


People with cognitive difficulties often do better when they are able to choose their own schedules and work conditions. If people are bothered by loud sounds or bright lights at work, they may modify their environments to make them more comfortable. If tight clothing bothers them, they can always opt for sweatpants (which, let's be real, most of us are now donning). They may avoid complex social interactions by just not engaging in them.


Employees with neurodiversity, who may find in-person meetings excruciating, benefit greatly from the accessibility of remote meetings. Meetings might be difficult for them to participate in, social signals can be confusing, and they might not get the humor or the nuances of what is being said. 


However, employees with neurodiversity may feel more at ease when meetings are done online because of the elimination of nonverbal clues. Captions, a transcript, or even a recording of a Zoom conversation may help workers who are neurodiverse and have trouble with video meetings.


Bring some of the perks of working from home into the office


The number of neurodiverse employees at EY (Ernst & Young) more than quadrupled during the pandemic, from 80 in 2020 to around 300 people presently, with many of these individuals occupying positions in cybersecurity, intelligent automation, and data analytics. Several Neuro-Diverse Centers of Excellence were established, some of which may be found in Boston and London. 


EY assisted workers in acquiring their desired resources. Many people made the decision to live completely alone. EY provided a quiet area, noise-canceling headphones, and dimmer lights for people who preferred to work in the office or with a hybrid setup. Hiren Shukla, who established EY's Neuro-Diverse Centers of Excellence, was recently quoted in a Wall Street Journal story as saying, "When you reduce the stress of the commute and the environmental stimuli, lighting, temperature, texture, sound... they are thriving." 


The skills of laser-like concentration, pinpoint accuracy, dogged determination, and pattern recognition are especially prized in the realm of cybersecurity. In the field of cybersecurity, there are now 2.7 million open positions. Hiring more neurodiverse people might help fill such positions.


Keep in mind that there is no "one size fits all”


However, former Autism Speaks vice president of services and support Valerie Paradiz warns against the assumption that people with ASD excel only in fields like information technology and finance. We, autistics, are as wide a spectrum as the rest of the population," she noted in a 2021 Forbes article. Not everyone here works in finance or can write programming. Scaling is essential in every sector, and this must be emphasized. 


In order to expand into new fields, Autism Speaks launched the Workplace Inclusion Now Initiative, which provides employees with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) with the resources they need to succeed and provides employers with the resources they need to establish inclusive workplaces.


Due to the fact that everyone is unique, those with neurodiversity have varied responses to working from home. O2, a British telecoms firm, conducted a study in which 34% of employees with neurological disorders said that working from home helped them concentrate, while 23% reported that it made them more conscious of their issues. Despite the benefits of video meetings, many people (45%) said that they had trouble focusing during them, while 44% claimed they had "Zoom fatigue," and 43% said they were overwhelmed by various types of instant messaging. 


Some employees also need the social interaction and routine of an office setting. The best way to help neurodiverse personnel succeed is to consult them about what works for them and what adjustments are necessary.


Concluding Ideas


According to studies, businesses that actively include people with disabilities enjoy 28% greater sales, twice the net income, and double the likelihood of outperforming the competition in terms of shareholder returns.


Individuals with neurological impairments not only bring a new perspective to the table but also frequently represent untapped consumer segments. However, because of the severity of their problems, they have also had to show remarkable fortitude, creativity, and flexibility. Those are admirable traits in a potential employee.

blogs.blogTitle: Training Recruiters: 6 Questions to Ask
blogs.blogSummary: Training Recruiters: 6 Questions to Ask- 1. What do the members of my team require? 2. Why are we doing this training? 3. Who exactly is this for?...
blogs.blogDescp:
Creating an effective training program for recruiters is a wonderful approach to not only improve the abilities of your recruiters but also get everyone on the same page in terms of the company's best practices.


Training is a potent and transforming tool, but it may come in all shapes and sizes, therefore it's important to consider the following questions before developing your strategy:


1. What do the members of my team require?


It's interesting to note that sometimes training backfires and does more harm than good. Don't train only to train; otherwise, you can irritate certain recruiters who are seeking to close a prospect.


Find out where your recruiters stand and where they can improve by conducting an audit. Before deciding what to teach your team, it's a good idea to get their input.


2. Why are we doing this training?


Get an accurate read on your team's performance, and use that knowledge to back up your decision to invest in training. This process will also aid in the development of learning outcomes, which may be used in post-training assessments of the team.

Consider also whether or not you have created an environment where knowledge is shared and shared is valued. Setting clear expectations is essential if training and education are not already part of the job description at your organization.


3. Who exactly is this for?


You are well aware that the positions of sales and engineering inside a firm need distinct strategies for hiring. If your business targets candidates from a wide range of companies, you'll need to tailor your content strategy accordingly. Your interview process and the recruiter's closing strategy will change depending on the team you're working with, so it's important to plan accordingly.


4. How exactly will the training work?


You may begin developing a training course's individualized material when you've determined your audience and its needs.

Focus on role-playing realistic situations if you want your staff to learn how to apply a concept or skill to the way it recruits (for example, learning how to convey your company's narrative or value proposition). If you want your recruiters to be able to recognize high technical talent profiles after training, you should have them review profiles and analyze what makes a strong prospect.


Your training's duration should reflect the complexity of the material, the number of attendees, and the amount of participation you anticipate. Give yourself plenty of time to explain, debate, and drill the material.


5. Are you establishing reasonable goals?


The first step in finding an answer to this issue is to determine precisely what it is you want the participants to accomplish, and the second is to share your expectations with them. The establishment of appropriate expectations has been deemed more crucial to training performance than the training itself or the training approach utilized.


Why do your recruiters have to boost productivity on the job? In order to define the training's goal from the workers' point of view, it is helpful to have previously had open and honest discussions with them regarding performance expectations.


Next, outline what kind of attendance and participation you anticipate from recruiters. You want them to come to the training with an open mind. After a great deal of experience with training sessions, we've learned that it's essential for participants to put down their electronic devices. Before inviting recruits to take part in a training session, we make sure they understand what is expected of them. Setting explicit expectations from the beginning will have a dramatic effect on the behavior of your participants.


6. After the course has ended, how will the trainees be assessed?


Value in training is derived from the lessons learned. Create concrete standards by which the progress of each trainee may be evaluated. In order to maximize the efficacy of the training, you should provide the recruits with tools to monitor their progress toward the desired outcomes.


You will be well on your way to developing useful recruiter training after you have addressed these critical questions.
