blogs.blogTitle: 25 Strategies Proven to Motivate Employees to Learn in the Workplace
blogs.blogSummary: How can you inspire your hurried workers to have a genuine interest in expanding their knowledge? Here are 25 ideas to try, all supported by evidence and...
blogs.blogDescp:
Engagement from students is the single most important factor in the success of any learning and development (L&D) program. That's the challenging part, unfortunately. 


How can you inspire your hurried workers to have a genuine interest in expanding their knowledge? Here are 25 ideas to try, all supported by evidence and actual implementations. 


1. Get started with the onboarding process


First impressions last, so make sure your employees have a good one throughout the onboarding process. Give new employees the support they need to feel comfortable in their new role and ready to take on new challenges. Also, make sure that a commitment to education is reflected in all aspects of the company's ethos.


2. Facilitate individual study


Self-directed learning options are favored by workers over formal training programs. 43% of Gen Z workers report having a preference for self-directed learning. This involves providing a variety of engaging learning opportunities that workers may pursue at their own speed. 


With microlearning, workers may incorporate learning into the natural rhythm of their daily job. There is a course dedicated to helping you develop a microlearning strategy on Jobcart Learning.


3. Pay attention to your skills


The abilities necessary to do a job have changed by 25% worldwide since 2015, according to a Jobcart examination of massive data. There might be as much as a 44% shift by 2025. 


59% of Gen Z professionals think their job will not exist in the same form 20 years from now. That's a lot of pressure, and it's being felt by the next generation of employees. 


Learning and development may alleviate this worry by focusing professionals' attention on the skill acquisition necessary to navigate the evolving workplace.


4. Tie your education to your future ambitions


The 2022 Workplace Learning Report found that the top three reasons why workers learn are related to their own professional development. These items are:


  1. If it helps me learn new things and advance in my area, then I'm all for it.
  2. If it's tailored to my exact needs and ambitions, then I could be interested.
  3. If it helps me move up internally, earn a promotion, or fulfill my professional objectives, I'll take it.


5. Make sure students have goals for their own growth


Assist each worker in developing a personalized strategy for professional and personal growth. An employee's strengths and potential growth areas are identified, as well as the employee's and the manager's opinions on those areas.


By integrating employees' personal development goals into their annual performance reviews, Pegasus Logistics was able to increase its learning engagement rate across the board to a perfect 100.


6. Dedicate more time to students


According to recent research, the average worker has less than an hour each week to devote to professional development. Don't simply tell your staff that learning and growth are a priority; show them that you mean it by providing them with time off specifically for that purpose.


If you work in learning and development, it's especially important to invest in your personal growth. 


7. In performance evaluations, stress the importance of learning opportunities


Few workers look forward to their annual performance reviews. They fear something that should provide them with direction and help them advance in their jobs. 


Improve morale by showing that you value your employees' development as much as you do. Managers may ask staff about their progress in training and provide guidance on how to turn obstacles into stepping stones.


8. Inspire supervisors to foster a culture of lifelong learning


Learning discussions shouldn't be held once a year and forgotten till the following year. Sixty-three percent of students said their supervisors' participation in their education would increase their motivation to study. It's important for managers to make training a regular focus of one-on-one meetings.


9. Make sure managers have coaching skills 


According to research done by Jobcart's own sales team, managers spend less than 10% of their time really educating and coaching their direct subordinates. Sales managers were given improved resources for learning and assessment in an effort to shorten this period. 


Then Jobcart decided to shift its focus to mentoring, allocating 50% of sales managers' time to the activity. As a consequence of these alterations, sales quotas were met 26% more often, salespeople performed 8% better, turnover was reduced, and employee satisfaction was raised.


10. Keep in mind the importance of internal mobility


In order to retain workers who are eager for new challenges, internal mobility has emerged as a crucial technique to provide individuals with additional opportunities to learn and develop. Companies with strong internal mobility programs have a retention rate almost double the market average, at 5.4 years.


Managers may facilitate their subordinates' upward mobility by encouraging them to take on more tasks and providing them with opportunities to do so. 


11. Facilitate everyone's access to education and ensure its usefulness


Inclusion should be kept in mind at all times while developing or enhancing a curriculum. This necessitates making the materials and tools usable by people of all ages, backgrounds, and skill levels.


12. Assist in the cross-training of managers


Managers from different departments should meet for "manager syncs" to compare notes on how they're improving learner engagement and what else they're doing well.


The L&D group at Jobcart spearheaded a discussion on sharing ideas by collecting anecdotes from throughout the company and compiling them into a presentation for management. 


13. Examine knowledge gaps 


Skill gaps may be analyzed at the group, team, and individual levels. Using Jobcart's interactive tool, users may learn more about the evolving skill sets required for various occupations and sectors. 


14. Educating the top executives should be a top concern


Econet Wireless's executives understood they needed to encourage a culture of learning in order to compete in the dynamic telecommunications market. To address this, they had its chief operating officer (COO), Fayaz King, outline the company's Jobcart Learning plan and make an emotional plea to workers via video message, saying, "We need to evolve and change by upskilling ourselves for the future."


After two months of seeing King's film, 94% of workers were utilizing the learning platform, with most spending at least four hours each month interacting with its material.


15. Evaluate the results of learning


Understanding what strategies for student motivation are most successful enables you to advocate for increased funding for the further development of these strategies. 


The most useful information demonstrates how your company's training programs contribute to its ability to respond quickly to new opportunities and challenges while keeping a keen eye on the bottom line. You might begin with any combination of the following metrics: 


  • The proportion of lessons learned
  • Faster onboarding time
  • Keeping Good Employees
  • Mobility from inside
  • Money and time are conserved.
  • Advances in bridging the skills gap


16. Exhibit examples of people's achievements


Take the time to celebrate your students' progress when they begin to achieve success. This is not only an effective method of showing gratitude to your staff, but it also motivates others to invest in their own education. After all, kids may soon be the center of attention themselves. 


17. Pay attention to learners


Ask students how they would like to be surveyed or interviewed as often as possible to find out what works best for them when it comes to learning. Introduce novel instructional methods and observe their reception carefully. Examine your statistics for trends of user interaction and provide more of what is successful. 


Many businesses now use staff opinion polls to make broad changes to their company's culture. Opportunities for professional development are also seen as a key factor contributing to a happy work environment.


18. Think about combining methods 


Consider mixing multiple educational approaches and styles when you build targeted programs. You may combine in-person and online instruction, or show students a recorded lesson while also taking questions in real-time.


19. Facilitate shared educational experiences


Group learning has been shown to improve students' interest, memory, and comprehension of course content. 

Allow students who prefer to study in groups to do so whenever feasible. Alternatively, you may suggest that students who independently complete the same courses meet together to discuss and share their findings. 


20. Make learning more enjoyable by turning it into a contest


QiCard, a Chinese credit card firm, encouraged its staff to invest twice as much time in training as did its competitors of comparable size. 


QiCard accomplished this by making its educational platform more like a game. Employees that participated in the program and completed 10 or more Jobcart Learning courses were rewarded with gift cards, certificates, and other prizes. 


21. Create an online forum for educational discussions


Use a platform or online forum that encourages engagement to make it simple for workers to talk about training, ask questions, and provide suggestions. Incorporate regular mentions of this tool into business messaging to encourage employee learning. Employees that contribute more regularly might be rewarded. 


22. Make a scoreboard to display academic achievements


Make sure there is a central location (either online or a physical whiteboard in the workplace) where everyone's progress in learning can be tracked and celebrated. 


This will highlight the significance of learning on the job and inspire students to strive for advancement. 


23. Track the most successful shows and make copies of them


The longer you provide extensive learning choices, the more information you will collect on those choices' efficacy. Patterns will develop over time that will provide valuable information.


Find the courses and learning programs that your students are actively participating in. Determine the most well-liked aspects of your service and build on them for the future. 


24. Develop educational competitions centered on upcoming events


Even though HillTop Securities' most valuable learning audience, the company's associates, agreed to continuous learning on Jobcart Learning despite the HR team's limited resources (only 5% of their time was spent on L&D).


The HR department said that the success was due to effective internal marketing, especially seasonal campaigns that highlighted timely information. This not only increased interest in learning but also gave the HillTop Security staff the sense that L&D was an integral part of the company's DNA.


25. Raise the profile of soft skills


Soft skills, the intangible qualities that help us develop productive interactions with others, are worth keeping in mind while brainstorming topics that can excite your students. Effective listening, connection building, creativity, and persuasion are just a handful of the many transferable talents that may assist employees at all levels of your firm.


Soft skills are what set people apart from robots and will help individuals and businesses thrive in the rapidly evolving workplace of the future.


Final Thoughts


The to-do lists of most L&D professionals are always expanding. You're probably also contributing to tackling other major concerns, such as employee well-being or excessive turnover, as you keep on producing high-impact, quality learning experiences. 


All that stress may make anybody feel down and out. But don't forget to enjoy the process of learning for what it is: the thrill of doing something difficult and growing into your full potential. 


You should feel much better about yourself if you can assist other people in experiencing the same joy. After all, you're giving everyone in the company the tools they need to thrive.

blogs.blogTitle: The 4 Secret Keys to Success in Hybrid Work Relationships
blogs.blogSummary: Hybrid Work Relationships: Professionals in the field of learning and development (L&D) are increasingly being tasked with educating company executives on how...
blogs.blogDescp:
Relationships at work are crucial to maintaining a safe, positive, and productive atmosphere. Coworkers don't need to be as close as friends or family, but they should be able to have constructive conversations, understand each other, and come to a decision.


This is difficult enough when everyone is sitting at the same table and can pick up on each other's social signals and body language. It may be considerably more difficult in a remote or hybrid workplace.


In a nutshell: hybrids are complex. Professionals in the field of learning and development (L&D) are increasingly being tasked with educating company executives on how to adapt to the altered dynamics of the modern workplace. Jobcart polled close to 1,500 L&D experts, and 72 percent of them claimed that L&D has become a more strategic role at their company. According to the same poll, in 2022, leadership and management education would be the top two priorities for L&D departments. 


Here are some tips from my Jobcart Learning course, Managing Office Politics, on how you, as an L&D manager, may foster productive relationships with your colleagues and help the organization reach its full potential.


1. Create a social proof


Evidence of thought leadership in the industry and the company is helpful for leaders in establishing credibility and trust in the workplace. "Social proof" refers to the endorsements of credible third parties, such as peers or established organizations.


Leaders may establish their reputations in a number of different ways. That's particularly true when more and more business conferences are held in a hybrid format or entirely online. Among other things, leaders may increase their credibility in the eyes of the public by:


  • Joining or running a nonprofit board of directors or professional organization
  • Putting pen to paper for respected trade journals
  • Using Jobcart to share authoritative posts
  • Putting in requests to give talks at physical and digital conventions


Although top executives and managers should always check with their employers before speaking publicly, many businesses welcome the opportunity to hear from their employees outside of the office. 


Whether in-person, digitally, or via a hybrid of the two, leaders who have built social proof have an easier time inspiring and guiding their people.


2. Promote harmony 


Traditional methods of leadership fostering loyalty are more challenging when employees are geographically dispersed. Having a catered Taco Tuesday or a fully stocked snack cupboard might be a challenge when you have a dispersed team. 


Leaders may take the time to get to know their staff one-on-one no matter where they are located. Leaders may discover a lot about their employees, including their personalities, values, interests, and more, in only 10 or 15 minutes of video chat. 


With this knowledge, leaders may give gifts that mean more than platitudes or gift cards to Starbucks. Instead of lavishing someone with pricey presents, focus on doing nice things for them. For instance:


  • Sending one's best wishes after a victory for one's favorite team
  • Your team leader is a wine aficionado; invite them to a tasting.
  • Putting up a few dollars to a team member's cause of choice
  • Providing assistance to increase their influence and credibility


3. Encourage realness


As a result of hybridization in the workplace, we no longer have to pretend to be someone other than who we really are (our "work selves"). Because of the intimate nature of video conferencing, employees need to feel safe bringing their whole selves to the workplace, including their kids, dogs, and significant others.


This requires more from leaders than just bearing with the odd interruption from a furry friend (or a child of four years or less). It implies recognizing the value in individual differences and actively encouraging individuals to be themselves around others. This is often a difficult procedure that calls for instruction.


Leading by example is the most prudent starting point. Leaders don't have to spill the beans about their personal life, but they may set a good example by praising their own achievements and encouraging their teams to do the same.


Here, workers may feel comfortable being themselves and discussing personal achievements, interests, and passions.


4. Facilitate communication among staff


Making introductions between people who can help one other is an effective kind of networking outside of the company. Whoever introduces you to your future spouse, best vendor, or top salesman will be remembered fondly. 


The same holds true for the company's internal workings. By facilitating employee networking, leaders may boost their reputation and create a positive work environment. Since there is no water cooler or break area in a hybrid workplace, this function becomes even more important. 


Leaders that have invested in one-on-one interactions, as discussed in point #2, will be in a prime position to facilitate introductions that benefit all parties. Amir and Steve, for instance, could both work in separate departments but have a passion for fostering rescue dogs. Alternatively, Rebecca and George may both be involved with LGBT+ organizations alone but would benefit from combining their efforts.


Even if they aren't directly tied to work, these relationships will make you feel less alone and enhance your spirits.


Final Words


Leadership development is essential for hybrid relationship building. Learning and Development is not responsible for improving communication and collaboration among employees. However, it is their duty to provide the necessary training and resources for leaders to carry out their roles effectively. With these four "secrets" in tow, managers may improve communication between themselves and their staff, as well as between departments.

blogs.blogTitle: 44 ways recruiters can make a big difference even when hiring is slow
blogs.blogSummary: Your recruiters will have a wealth of anecdotes to share with candidates when recruiting starts up, and if you don't anticipate hiring to recover, your firm...
blogs.blogDescp:
Don't let your in-house recruiters be laid off. Instead, think strategically. 


The HR department is the public face of the company when it comes to recruiting top personnel. Focus your team's efforts on proactive initiatives to enhance the applicant and employee experience when recruiting slows down. Your recruiters will have a wealth of anecdotes to share with candidates when recruiting starts up, and if you don't anticipate hiring to recover, your firm has greater concerns.


Choose from among these 44 activities. (In case you're wondering, I compiled several of these theories at the outbreak's onset.)


Rather than just throwing away your recruiters, think about:


  • Transform those boring job descriptions into something that will really interest and engage potential candidates.
  • To build your employer brand, you should conduct video interviews with company executives and key workers; *Make "Day in the Life" films or write blog articles about your company's most important departments like sales, engineering, customer support, etc.
  • To reduce turnover, extensive departure interviews should be conducted.
  • To assist your staff in advance internally, you should interview and coach them.
  • Create a program outlining potential paths for advancement for staff members.
  • Make use of ATS's automated features to save time during high hiring times in the future.
  • Teach interview techniques to recruiting committees.
  • For "regular" staffing requirements, provide standardized hiring procedures and evaluation methodologies.
  • Develop a strategy for leadership transition.
  • Consider internal pay fairness in the context of external recruiting practices.
  • Develop a plan to shift salary increases from a yearly cost-of-living raise to one based on actual job performance.
  • Solicit comments from the last year's pool of candidates as a whole. Conduct research. Recruiter experiences must be enhanced.
  • Build effective DEI initiatives to attract a more diverse pool of applicants in the future. Possibilities include assessing the recruiting process for prejudice, forming partnerships with mentoring organizations, and creating a referral program that specifically seeks out individuals from underrepresented groups.
  • Prepare for the future by organizing an early career pipeline and/or an internship program.
  • Find new sourcing resources such as organizations, schools, and networking events. 
  • To meet ongoing staffing demands, you must create and fill projects in Jobcart Recruiter. Account executives, client success managers, and software developers are all positions that might benefit from these in a technology firm.
  • Reduce dropout rates by enhancing preboarding procedures (the time between an offer and the employee's start date).
  • Enhance the first week of employment by focusing on onboarding.
  • Conduct a D.E.I.-based analysis of your current employee referral program. 
  • Encourage participation from the recruiting team throughout onboarding. Create a system of automatic reminders to guarantee that everyone on your team sends out Jobcart connection requests and schedules targeted 1:1s within the first week.  
  • Make a new or updated career page! 


There are 16 new initiatives for HR recruiters


I shared the aforementioned work on my Jobcart account, which resulted in an influx of original suggestions from the general public.


  • Anticipate staffing requirements over the next 12 months.
  • Create internal job shadowing and apprenticeship opportunities for existing workers. Give them a go at some other positions inside the company.
  • Create a program where past interns are actively involved as "campus ambassadors" (excellent for corporate branding).
  • If you want to find more places where applicants from underrepresented groups may enter the workforce, you should talk to your DEI programs and employee resource groups.
  • Develop a strategy for tracking progress on these projects. Making a business case over the long term relies heavily on metrics like ROI and continuous development.
  • Assign human resources personnel to other departments. As a result, your recruiters will be better equipped to return to the field of talent acquisition with their newly acquired skills and knowledge.
  • To encourage professional growth and best practices among the talent acquisition staff, it is important to draft (or revise) a set of standard operating procedures.
  • To speed up operations, you should look at other ATS solutions and HR technology. • Design "new hire feedback surveys" at the 30-, 60-, and 90-day points (Tessana Nemenski). Leverage information for a better onboarding process.  
  • Think about applying for the position of customer success manager. Skills like project management, negotiating, attention to detail, and value-based marketing are just a few of the numerous marketable abilities possessed by recruiters. • Develop a program that rewards workers for bringing in qualified applicants (Michi Hu Pezeshki). Transform your current workforce to become expert recruiters and advocates for your company's image.
  • Make films that help aspiring job-seekers prepare for interviews. (Alex Campo) Cross-train the recruiters you employ. Assist them in expanding their knowledge to other fields (financial recruiting for IT recruiters, for example). Marissa P., develop a plan for HRBP, People Ops, TA, Compensation, and Learning and Development employees to switch roles on a regular basis.
  • To improve communication and cooperation inside the company, hold team-building exercises for all employees.
  • Formulate a compensation theory. The leadership has to be in sync. Make a deliberate choice about how competitive you want your business to be. Educate the recruiters and HR personnel on how to explain the company's values to prospective workers.


Seven additional things to think about


  • It's a good idea to make video resumes. Candidates appreciate them. Make an entry on the CV. Give prospective employees a concrete picture of what their future may hold if they join your company.
  • Employees who have left but returned "boomerang" should be interviewed. Realize why things weren't better on the other side. 
  • Make sure you have well-organized interview guidelines. Create a system for rating the effectiveness of interviews. Be objective and reasonable in your attitude during interviews.
  • Culture ambassadors should be designated as the exclusive decision-makers for issues of cultural fit and improvement. It is irrelevant how qualified a candidate is if they don't fit or add to the company's culture.
  • Create salary summaries for existing workers on an annual basis. Focus on overall compensation, including salary, bonuses, and perks like a matching 401(k). Pay is just one component of compensation.
  • Make use of key performance indicators (KPIs) and service level agreements (SLAs) to monitor your success over time. Provide monthly, quarterly, and yearly updates on this matter to the senior leadership team and the rest of the company.
  • Make sure to check your pay rate. Be sure your prices are in line with the market.

blogs.blogTitle: How to Become an Effective Virtual Recruiter in 4 Easy Steps
blogs.blogSummary: Despite its popularity, remote work has been on the increase for quite some time. Before the current epidemic ushered in the age of virtual work, many forward...
blogs.blogDescp:
Despite its popularity, remote work has been on the increase for quite some time. Before the current epidemic ushered in the age of virtual work, many forward-thinking, employee-centric businesses adopted remote work opportunities as a strategy to promote work-life balance and recruit top talent.


Remote employment is here to stay even if many firms have reopened and restarted in-person operations. In fact, Joblist found that 45% of those polled would quit if they were forced to return to full-time in-person employment.


This has led to an increased focus on building and sustaining a virtual workforce among businesses and organizations worldwide.


For hiring managers, what does this imply?


The rising popularity of working from home brings both difficulties and possibilities. In a series of emails and Skype sessions with potential prospects, how do we get to know them as people? How can we keep the corporate culture alive when no one is in the same room together? However, because geography is no longer a barrier to employment, remote work allows us to reach a wider pool of possible individuals.


Here are my top four suggestions for bringing your recruitment game online, whether you're a seasoned headhunter or just starting out.


1. Think like your potential candidates


Where do you think your digital job applicants are hanging out?


It used to be sufficient to put up a job ad or go through a stack of resumes when hiring. However, we need to use some savviness today. After all, you're looking for exceptional talent that will boost your business and make everyone's jobs easier.

Locating these people requires an examination of the environments in which they participate. You may do it on social networking sites like Jobcart, Twitter, or Reddit, or on specialized employment forums.


Even if your dream worker isn't actively seeking new employment, it's still important to find out where they "hang out" online so you can introduce yourself.


2. Take advantage of your competitors' research efforts


Your rivals have probably previously investigated the market. Take note of the channels, kinds of applicants, and language used in their job postings. Is there a strong emphasis on "work from home" or "flexible scheduling?" The way your rivals portray themselves may teach you a lot.


The point is not to imitate them but to utilize what you learn to create something unique that may appeal to prospects they may have missed.


3. Take another look at the ratings


Many familiar aspects of the traditional office setting carry over into the modern online equivalent. It is still important to evaluate a candidate's problem-solving, critical-thinking, and initiative skills, in addition to establishing a foundational set of abilities.

Although the method remains the same, setting appropriate expectations becomes a more crucial part of the review. To be successful in your role as a virtual recruiter, it is essential that you fully comprehend the hiring manager's success criteria, soft skill requirements, communication needs, etc.


Finding the perfect applicant the first time around depends on you spending the time to study the company's assessment procedure.


4. Enhance the effectiveness of online orientation 


The onboarding procedure is routinely disregarded. Many businesses wrongly assume that the onboarding process is over after the documentation has been completed, but this may leave new hires feeling disoriented and unsure of their role. For a remote team, appropriate onboarding is even more crucial.


Effective onboarding may make remote workers feel like indispensable contributors on day one. Get the word out to HR that they need to take a hard look at current onboarding practices and improve them so that all new hires can settle in and thrive in their new positions.


As recruiters, we need to adapt to the ever-shifting job market. You may increase your chances of hiring excellent employees by using the advice given above.


Only the surface has been scratched here. Are you prepared to go deeper so that you can provide your organization or clientele with greater and more impressive outcomes? Jobcart Learning offers a fascinating and detailed course on virtual recruiting.


In this tutorial, I'll show you how to become a successful online recruiter. You'll acquire the skills necessary to spot promising remote workers, create a solid pool of potential hires, and aid businesses on their way to adopting a fully remote workforce.

blogs.blogTitle: Why Avoid Pressing Interviewers For a Yes/No Answer
blogs.blogSummary: This mentality is so pervasive that some recruiting platforms have a "Hire/No Hire" choice built right into their feedback forms. There are often three outcomes...
blogs.blogDescp:
Our customers have told us that eliminating the "Hire/No Hire" question from interview feedback is one of the most difficult changes they have made as part of their transformation of the recruiting process. 


This may seem inconceivable to some people. Everyone who goes through the interview process should come out strongly for or against the proposed hire. This mentality is so pervasive that some recruiting platforms have a "Hire/No Hire" choice built right into their feedback forms.


This is a very risky practice. There are often three outcomes when asking for a "Hire/No Hire" decision after each interview:


  • Broad and shallow questions are asked in interviews. Every interviewer has the tendency to want to cover too much ground, but that's only because making a decision needs seeing the "full picture." There is no in-depth coverage of any topic, and the total amount of information presented is extremely sparse (and repetitive).
  • The interviewers' preconceived notions and prejudices come out. They anticipate having to make a decision at some point and set out to gather evidence supporting a preconceived notion. Due to time constraints, interviewers sometimes have to "fill in the blanks" with their own assumptions and speculations rather than hard data.
  • The same kind of questions are asked of every candidate. They think you're uncoordinated, at best. You risk them doubting your level of confidence in each other at worse. In addition, applicants who aren't quite as strong might practice their answers until they're perfect before the final round.


Participate in data-sharing and specialized work


Fortunately, these issues may be avoided via the use of specialized and shared information. The following course of action is what we suggest:


  1. Create a team of interviewers that specialize in different areas, allowing them to ask in-depth questions about certain aspects of the work. 
  2. Make sure your interviewers are taking thorough notes on the candidate's answers so that you can quickly share this information with others. If a note-taker would be helpful, they should do so, but the applicant should be informed beforehand.
  3. Encourage interviewees to stick to the topics they were asked about and only request ratings (on a scale of 1–4) at the end of the interview. 
  4. Instruct your raters to ditch their intuitive reactions in favor of hard data from their interviews. When prejudices occur, encourage team members to provide each other with constructive criticism.


You may collect more and better information by breaking it up into smaller interviewing groups. And top talent will flock to your streamlined, systematic approach.

blogs.blogTitle: 3 Ways that Coded Language is Costing You Applicants
blogs.blogSummary: How you talk to people is important, and every word or phrase in your job description has the potential to turn away suitable applicants. You need to learn how...
blogs.blogDescp:
Job ads are often the first time someone looking for a job interacts with your brand, and they may be your only chance to connect with a certain candidate. Especially if you want the best people to apply, you need to make a good impression.

But what if secret language is getting in the way of your plans? 


How you talk to people is important, and every word or phrase in your job description has the potential to turn away suitable applicants. You need to learn how to spot coded language so that you can fix it before it costs you, candidates.


1. Biased language shows that some groups aren't allowed to apply


Most of the time, people don't mean to use biased language in job postings. This makes it hard to avoid and all the more important to do so on purpose. 


This includes words like:


  • Gender-coded. Job descriptions that include masculine-coded words, such as "ambitious," "confident," and "independent," turn off women and make them feel as if they don't fit in the position being advertised. Men may be marginally less attracted to women-coded adjectives like reliable, interpersonal, and loyal, but this has little to no effect on their feeling of community.
  • Ageist. Job descriptions often include ageist language like "young company" or "high energy," which discourages applicants over the age of 40 from applying. Wording like "digital native" or "recent college graduate" to characterize the perfect applicant in a job advertisement is even more blatant.
  • Ableist. Job descriptions may be unwittingly ableist if they include common terms like "see," "hear," and "stand." While not everyone may be able to write, that doesn't mean they can't enter or record data in some other method.
  • Nativistic. Qualifications based on language skills might be unfair to applicants who speak many languages. Requirements, like fluency in English or German, might dissuade talented immigrants and persons of diverse races from applying. 


Write more welcoming job descriptions by familiarizing yourself and your staff with the issues of prejudiced terminology. Since this isn't always obvious, you may want to use a tool like a gender decoder, the Ableist Language Detector from the U.S. Department of Labor, Textio, or Textmetrics to help you spot and fix any instances of coded language in your postings. 

Using language that is more welcoming and affirming will attract a wider pool of qualified applicants. 


2. Common phrases used in job descriptions may provide the wrong impression about your organization


There is a severe shortage of qualified applicants. Talent acquisition teams are making adjustments to job postings in an effort to entice and engage a more diverse pool of applicants, but some of these alterations may be counterproductive. 


The standard language used in job descriptions may be misunderstood as coded language and result in fewer applications. Opportunities are meant to seem exciting, but applicants may misinterpret phrases like "fast-paced environment" as code for "high stress." Similarly, terms like "looking for self-starters" or "willing to wear many hats" may indicate a dearth of assistance or a prescription for burnout. 


Feel free to use these expressions if they accurately depict the company's culture and goals for this position. The purpose of your job ads should be to assist you find qualified candidates. In a highly competitive sales atmosphere or the emergency room, for instance, the term must manage stress properly is appropriate. By including this information in the posting, qualified applicants may weed themselves out of consideration, speeding up the hiring process and perhaps lowering staff turnover.


However, hidden meanings in your job advertisements might be to fault if applicants' first impressions of your organization are different from reality. Before posting a job opening, have an employee examine it, and conduct frequent surveys of applicants to gauge how they feel about the company as an employer. Modifying your terminology may increase the quality of your candidates and the number of people who apply.


3. Dissuading eligible people from applying for a job because of unnecessary criteria


There are job descriptions out there that read like a shopping list of requirements, many of which may not be relevant to doing the job well.


One typical qualification listed in advertisements is a certain number of years of relevant experience. Applicants are generally judged on the number of years they have worked in the field, led teams, or executed a certain task. 


The issue is that work experience is not always indicative of competence or motivation for advancement. There's always the chance that a candidate with less experience might make a better manager than someone with more years at the helm.


Additionally, a high-performing employee who is "overqualified" may be content with their current position and have no desire to advance. The use of a predetermined level of experience might mislead exceptionally qualified applicants into thinking they don't qualify for the position.


Equally common are minimum degree and grade point average criteria listed in employment ads. In part, because applicants from historically underrepresented populations are disproportionately affected by education requirements, companies are shifting their attention from degrees to skills. 


Requiring a degree might imply that "you need to have money to make money." They may also indicate that your business is not a suitable fit for people with neurological differences, who may acquire and demonstrate knowledge in ways that are not rewarded by conventional schooling.


Emphasize the role's duties, the metrics used to evaluate performance, and the required or preferred skill sets in your job advertising. When job descriptions emphasize duties rather than qualifications, they attract 14% more applicants.


Final Words


The suitable candidates should respond positively to your job advertisements, while those who aren't a good fit should be discouraged. The retention of competent applicants in the pipeline may be improved by eliminating coded language.

blogs.blogTitle: How to Keep Your Team Motivated When Facing Uncertainty
blogs.blogSummary: Given the current state of global affairs, it might be difficult to determine which way is up. According to Jobcart, director of global talent...
blogs.blogDescp:
You are not hallucinating things if you feel like you have whiplash right now. A year ago, the rate at which requisitions were stacking up was so rapid that recruiters hardly had time to have lunch, much alone catch their breath. Now that recruiting has slowed across numerous industries, more and more groups are having to lay off their team's recruiters. 


Given the current state of global affairs, it might be difficult to determine which way is up. According to Jobcart, director of global talent acquisition at Jobcart, "It can feel like you're in a washing machine cycle, where it keeps getting turned on and off without notice." 


In light of this, we recently highlighted in our Hiring study the difficulty in maintaining recruiters' attention and enthusiasm. While recruiting at Jobcart slowed early this year, Jobcart's staff reported higher levels of engagement. 


There was no chance of occurrence here. Jobcart and the rest of the leadership team put in a lot of time and energy (and will continue to put in a lot of time and effort) to make sure the rest of the team felt included and in the loop so that they could perform their duties without distraction. 


1. Plan for a variety of outcomes


It might be difficult to take a step back, relax, and prepare for a range of outcomes when your career is so dependent on responding to the vagaries of the job market. However, Jobcart's leadership group has recently done this same thing.


Predicting the future and preparing for it is a common preoccupation, according to Jobcart. We zeroed down on the top two or three potential outcomes and made rough plans for each of them. 


The crew deliberated about two potential futures: one in which Jobcart resumes its quick, massive recruiting, and the other in which hiring significantly slows down. 


The executive staff then divided into two groups, each of which considered one of the two possibilities. They made a chart of questions they planned to ask in each potential scenario, and they took notes on current trends and past hiring trends. They put their new knowledge to work by developing potential outcomes. 


Why bother? As Jobcart puts it, "What we really want is to have our scenario playbooks" so that the company can adapt to new recruiting cycles more easily.


Business benefits aside, scenario planning may have a significant impact on morale in the workplace. When there's a strategy in place, moods often improve. 


2. Maintain constant and early contact 


The recruitment leadership at Jobcart kept their team apprised of developments as they unfolded via frequent and timely updates throughout their scenario preparation efforts. Jobcart explains that when the company first saw the slowdown, "we leaned into our director-plus and manager-plus population and were open with them about everything we knew and didn't know, and we didn't shy away from the tough questions." 


They had learned from experience that people respond positively when they are informed in advance, so they made an effort to keep everyone in the loop.


Employees like supervisors who communicate often, even though it might be stressful to be bombarded with information, according to new research from the Stanford Graduate School of Business. When supervisors are less communicative, staff members often conclude that they lack empathy. Managers may improve the morale of their staff by just communicating with them more.


3. Prioritize the team's efforts 


Additionally, Jobcart and the leadership group established priority areas. At a recent offsite, they spent time doing just that, preparing strategically. According to Jobcart, "We focused on the future, what we were going to prioritize in the fiscal year 2023, and how folks could be engaged." 


They zeroed in on certain areas Jobcart was already addressing but wanted to investigate further: diversity, operational excellence, and recruiting experiences. 


Employees were receptive to Jobcart and her team's communication regarding the new priorities. More than 300 individuals have shown interest in contributing to projects, allowing the talent acquisition team to advance the pace of several initiatives more swiftly than first anticipated. They even came up with "Project Boost" to speed things up. 


The leadership group helped their team concentrate their efforts by setting clear goals. 


Final thoughts


These three tactics are not only keeping the recruitment staff enthusiastic and productive, but they are also setting the stage for future rounds of hiring. This is not a waste of time. This lays the groundwork for improved long-term planning and productivity.

blogs.blogTitle: Recruiters: What Are Your Responses to These Typical Interview Questions?
blogs.blogSummary: Respond to applicants' queries patiently and intelligently throughout the first phone interview. This might keep your applicants interested while you both...
blogs.blogDescp:
Job applicants are doing interviews with you as thoroughly as you are conducting interviews with them. Candidates want to know whether there is a good fit between your organization, the position, and the hiring manager.


Respond to applicants' queries patiently and intelligently throughout the first phone interview. This might keep your applicants interested while you both determine whether they are a suitable match. 


Listed here are some of the most often-asked questions in interviews:


“What’s your salary range for this role?”


According to Jobcart's Global Talent Trends study, potential employees place a premium on competitive pay and perks. Expect a higher volume of inquiries on your wage history and potential bonuses. The stigma associated with discussing salaries openly has diminished. Candidates now are more open to discussing pay, and many even anticipate it will be discussed openly.


If potential applicants inquire, you should be able to tell them the wage range for the position. It's recommended practice to just disclose a portion of your range, so applicants don't assume they'll be offered the maximum amount. 


Knowing the fundamentals of how your organization determines pay is also important. This can help you explain the specifics of each offer, such as where you live, how many years of experience you have, and what kind of education you have.


“What is your policy for remote work?”


According to the Global Talent Trends study, in 2022, work-life harmony and adaptability would rank second only to financial rewards among applicant priorities. Both may be fulfilled by working remotely. Candidates will be interested in learning more about your company's stance on the topic and how the hiring manager plans to implement it.


Explain if it is a purely distant, mostly remote, somewhat remote, or entirely in-person opportunity. Consider if this policy is formally established or whether it may change in the future. Candidates will appreciate your honesty in this regard since it will help them get an accurate impression of your policies before they join your team.


“What opportunities for professional development do you offer?”


The Global Talent Trends research identifies upskilling and career growth as two of the top five most important factors for job seekers. It's important to show potential employees that there is space for advancement in their careers if you want them to join your organization.


Get ready to talk about the company's training and development offerings with your employees, such as online courses, mentorship, and career counseling. Describe the growth opportunities that would be available to the prospect if they accepted a position with your company. (Career progression might also be advertised, as GitLab does for some positions.) Show your dedication to your employees' development as professionals by sharing success stories when appropriate.


“How would you characterize the ethos of the company?”


There is an abundance of employment openings, so prospective employees may be wondering what makes working for your organization so appealing. Candidates may get a feel for your organization and whether or not they would like to work thereby learning more about your culture.


Explain what your organization stands for and provide examples of how those ideals are put into practice every day. Candidates seek evidence that you live by your own standards. Companies that place a premium on cooperation, for instance, can announce their latest team-building exercises and talk about their employee appreciation program.


“Tell me about the best and worst parts about working here”


Candidates interested in learning more about your corporate culture may ask this question throughout the interview process. As an employer, every company has both advantages and disadvantages. Potential employees want to know whether they will be able to succeed at your firm despite its flaws. 


Being forthright and truthful is essential. Your response to this question is crucial in determining whether or not the applicant will be a good match for your organization. If applicants are hired after the truth has been embellished or buried, this might lead to early disengagement and attrition.


“Is this a brand-new role or a replacement?”


Prospective employees are naturally curious as to the circumstances around your open position. A new role may indicate expansion inside the firm or a reorganization of duties to better meet the demands of the staff. Any of these events might necessitate finding a replacement for a team member.


Give some background to this question so the applicant can grasp why you are looking to fill this position. A promotion, for instance, might be used to talk about or reaffirm your company's support for career growth and advancement within the ranks. 


“How does one go about applying for this position?”


Candidates would want to have an idea of what to anticipate from the recruiting process, which might vary greatly from one organization to the next. Knowing what to expect will help them decide whether your method is feasible given their resources and timeframe. 


Tell the applicants exactly what they can expect at each step of the process when it will happen, how long it will take, and who else will be involved. Be sure to verify references, do background checks, and conduct interviews. 


Then, make sure you do everything you can to stay on track. Candidates shouldn't be left waiting in suspense if they discover you need extra time before proceeding to the next stage of your procedure and fail to inform them.


“Do you have any qualms about promoting me?”


Candidates typically put in significant mental work during interviews and are anxious to hear your final thoughts and whether or not you suggest them for further consideration. 


Get all the information you need to make a recommendation by asking candidates to elaborate on their answers to your interview questions. If you don't believe an applicant has the necessary job experience, you may give them a reason why and hear them out. Say so, nevertheless, if you believe they make a good match. This might help you maintain interest in the best prospects throughout the employment process.


Candidates should be asked if they have any concerns about joining your organization. There should be no candidate drop-off due to unanswered questions or red flags at this stage.


Final thoughts


Job interview questions are tailored to each individual applicant. You might be stumped by a few of the inquiries. If that's the case, tell the applicant you'll look into it and get back to them as soon as possible with an answer. In addition to making you a better fit for the position, this method will also impress potential employers.

blogs.blogTitle: Transform The Conventional Job Interview With This One Powerful Approach
blogs.blogSummary: You could ask a lot worse questions. However, there are significant flaws with this method. It gives too much away about your motivation, which might cause...
blogs.blogDescp:
Most competent interviewers will ask applicants many questions of this type: "Tell me about a time when you [did X specific thing we care about]."


You could ask a lot worse questions. However, there are significant flaws with this method. It gives too much away about your motivation, which might cause applicants to bend the truth to match the question (we've all done it!). And most importantly, it may cause applicants to reuse the same answers and anecdotes in several interviews. 


Maria, for instance, has prepared for and is attending many job interviews. She relies on a small repertoire of anecdotes, one of which is about the massive Android rollout at her previous workplace. She mentally "tags" this narrative as an excellent illustration of (1) originality, (2) meeting a challenging deadline, (3) successful collaboration across departments, etc. 


Maria, unsurprisingly, gives five different accounts of the Android incident to five separate interviewers. The group doing the interviews is now debating whether the account is more accurate (or more/less remarkable). No one heard any of the innumerable other tales Maria had read but didn't tell because of this.


We need to know how to prevent this. We need to avoid the candidate's scripted answers and instead create a more organic conversation. Human memory works in reverse chronological order, therefore that's how we'll have to retrieve information. 

We suggest scheduling one extended interview in which you discuss the candidate's whole professional history. This "career inventory" strategy is very effective and may be used for any position, department, or organizational level in any sector.


Through inquisitiveness and asking leading questions, you should probe the applicant about his or her most significant achievements and failures in each of the main roles along their professional paths. You'll be able to collect more information in less time, and you won't have to listen to the same narrative repeatedly.


Candidates seem to like the chronological method as a pleasant byproduct. It's far less nerve-wracking than coming up with answers to obscure queries on the spot. Your attention to detail and sincere interest in learning about each candidate as a whole leave a lasting impression.


We suggest allocating at least two hours for a candidate with five to ten years of expertise, but more is preferable. Therefore, you should provide the applicant with some advanced warning. However, after you have some background using this method, you will wonder how you ever hired anybody before.

blogs.blogTitle: 10 Easy Ways to Make Your Employer Brand Stand Out
blogs.blogSummary: Employer branding is crucial in today's upside-down talent market, whether your goal is to recruit top talent or to hold on to the employees you currently have.
blogs.blogDescp:
It's been quite a roller coaster ride in the job market over the last several years. Despite the ever-changing goals of recruiting teams, one thing has remained constant: the significance of a strong employer brand. 


Employer branding is crucial in today's upside-down talent market, whether your goal is to recruit top talent or to hold on to the employees you currently have. Your employer brand is your reputation among existing and potential workers; 86% of persons surveyed said they would not apply to or stay with a firm with a terrible reputation.


Time to fill, cost per hire, and employee turnover may all be decreased with the aid of an appealing employer brand. So, how can you build a strong reputation as an employer? Here are some suggestions to think about.


1. Make the application process simpler and faster for potential candidates


The application process for a job should be easy. If you want to streamline your application process, don't have applicants go through additional hoops like uploading a résumé and manually filling out their work history. As a result, prospective employees will have a more positive view of your business and your firm as a whole. 


2. Reduce the number of words on your resume


When searching for a new job, many people visit the company's career site or Company Page on social media. An effective way to explain your company's culture and perks to prospects while still respecting their time constraints is through a succinct and straightforward career webpage. Check out Phunware, a mobile software business, and see how well they handle their job page. It's concise and straightforward, with no fluff.


3. Make a statement with your Jobcart Company Page banner 


If you want to make a good impression as an employer, you should use the top picture on your Jobcart Company Page. Make an announcement, highlight an achievement, publicize an upcoming event, or increase consumer understanding of your company by using this tool. Affirm, a financial services provider, handles this well by showcasing its recognition as one of the "100 Best Remote-First Places to Work" against the company's motto, "People come first." For so little work, that's a lot of communication. 


4. Facilitate job seekers' quests for part-time or freelance jobs


Edit your career site to briefly include remote and hybrid employment opportunities. To achieve this, create a workplace policy and upload it to your Jobcart profile. The work environment may be specified as either "onsite," "remote," or "hybrid" in this configuration. Additionally, this provides an opportunity for businesses to brag about perks like health and wellness days, quiet work time, and teamwork-friendly offices. 


5. Exhibit the values to which your organization is dedicated


Job-seekers are drawn to organizations that strive for social good. Jobcart found that 68% of job seekers prioritize finding an employer that shares their values throughout the job search process. 


The Commitments section of your Company Page is a great place to highlight your business's core principles. You may now highlight your interest in diversity and inclusion, sustainability, work-life balance, social impact, and personal and professional development in the "About" section at no additional cost. To further establish credibility, you may publish evidence in the form of reports, certificates, articles, blogs, and videos. It's a simple and quick approach to demonstrate to potential employees why they should join your team. 


6. Highlight the most important advantages


The perks your firm offers may speak volumes to potential employees, so be sure to highlight them in job descriptions and on your organization's employment site. However, it is important to zero in on the right advantages. While it's true that health insurance and retirement plans are important, it's also important to highlight the unique perks that your company offers, such as assistance with student loans, paid time off for parents, and childcare assistance. Also, be sure to highlight any networking, training, or mentoring opportunities that may be available to you.  


7. Collaborate with your staff to crowdsource material


If you want to keep your employer brand interesting, it's a good idea to provide new and exciting material on a regular basis. Take Epic, a popular software company, as an illustration: Every five years, workers are eligible for four weeks of paid sabbatical time off, and the employer will even chip in to cover travel expenses if the employee is going to a nation they have never been before. Epic has a Sabbatablog on their employment site where staff members may briefly describe their time off.


8. Improve the application process by including applicant comments 


According to a 2018 study, 35% of applicants who have a negative experience throughout the hiring process would go so far as to openly disclose their experience online. Asking for applicant input via quick, automated questionnaires at various stages in the process might help you build a stronger employer brand. You may immediately address any concerns the applicant may have and enhance their impression of your organization.


9. Even if it's automated, you should send a thank-you message to each of your applicants   


Gratitude is universally appreciated. That's why it's smart to show appreciation for those who took the time to interview you by sending them a message of thanks, even if they didn't make the cut. To save time, you may utilize pre-made templates and have the notes generated automatically. After getting such a remark, candidates will have a more favorable impression of your organization, which is great for your brand. 


10. Spend two minutes sharing what makes your business special


A press release, article, or announcement may be published or republished in just a few minutes, making it a fast and easy method to spread the word about your company as an employer. After recent announcements by CEO Brian Chesky that the company would no longer pay employees based on where they physically worked, this became the case for Airbnb. The news spread, and in the days after Brian's revelation, more than a million people checked out Airbnb's job page. 


Final thoughts


You may utilize the extra time you have today to begin building your employer brand, even if you can only do so in baby increments. One of these tasks might be completed in a little chunk of time set aside each day. Another option is to enlist the aid of your team. When everyone in a team does their part, even the smallest action may have a significant result.

blogs.blogTitle: Why You Should Stop Asking These 5 Tiresome Interview Questions
blogs.blogSummary: A candidate's impression of your organization may be irreparably damaged if they go through an interview and encounter unnecessary stress. Overly anxious or...
blogs.blogDescp:
A candidate's impression of your organization may be irreparably damaged if they undergo an interview and encounter unnecessary stress. Overly anxious or uncomfortable people may have trouble showcasing everything they could contribute to the work, which makes it tougher to assess their fit for the post.


Despite your best efforts, some of the more routine questions you ask during interviews could make even the most confident candidates feel awkward.


In a recent research by the digital marketing firm Reboot, participants wore a fitness tracker to their interviews. The participants' heart rates were monitored when they answered standard interview questions. Their heart rates practically doubled in some situations.


Find out how to acquire the answers you need from applicants without making them sweat by avoiding the most stressful interview questions and circumstances.


1. “We’re going to give you a quick test”


The mere mention of an impending examination is enough to get most people's hearts racing. According to Reboot's research, this news raises a candidate's heart rate by an average of 93%, from around 80 to 154 beats per minute.


In order to objectively compare and contrast applicants' abilities, a test may be the best option.


However, you can take steps to improve your test performance and reduce anxiety. If the interview will include a test, applicants would appreciate knowing in advance so include that information in the agenda. By telling them roughly when this evaluation will take place, such as between interviews with various people or at the end of the day, they may better prepare for it and spend less time worrying about it.


It's probably preferable to avoid time-bound exams unless it's really necessary for the position you hiring for. It's possible you'll wish to provide your exam as homework. Despite having all the necessary skills, some applicants may need more time to read and understand instructions or complete particular activities. It's OK to reassure applicants that the exam won't take too long, but it may be more useful to their evaluation if they know they can take as much time as they need to complete it. 


2. “What salary range are you looking for?”


Many people clearly fear being questioned about compensation, as seen by the hundreds of articles that provide advice on how to respond to salary-related queries in job interviews. Salary history is no longer a fair question for an employer in several states. Moreover, posting salary ranges is becoming mandatory for businesses in an increasing number of jurisdictions. Salary expectations queries are still widespread, and according to Reboot, they raise blood pressure by 66%.


Getting rid of salary history inquiries is the most effective way to promote wage equality in the workplace, according to studies. However, it may not be productive to ask the candidate what salary they are hoping to receive. This is a question that might contribute to the problem of wage disparity since most applicants base their expectations on their prior salaries.


In order to avoid awkward conversations about wage expectations, some firms, like Glitch, publish compensation ranges online so that applicants may be certain that they will be paid fairly. Meanwhile, Starbucks has achieved full pay parity by instituting a salary calculator that produces precise pay bands according to factors like experience and education. These kinds of approaches remove the pay issue from the discussion entirely, putting the onus on the interviewer rather than the applicant, and freeing up more time for the latter to evaluate the latter's qualifications. 


3. “Do you know what we do here?”


Each prospective employee should familiarize themselves with your organization before coming in for an interview. However, the study by Reboot found that just asking the question "Do you know what we do here?" increased heart rate by 63%.

The phrasing of the query may be the issue here since it risks seeming patronizing or even menacing. Even if they've done their homework, candidates who go into interviews with the feeling that their interviewers believe they're unprepared might feel anxious.


Questions like "What inspired you to apply here?" or "What is it about our mission that resonates with you?" are better alternatives. By making these little adjustments, you may learn not only how well they grasp the tasks and objectives of your organization, but also where their hobbies and passions lay.


4. “What would your last boss say about you?”


What would your former employer say about you? may seem an innocuous method of learning about applicants' prior work connections, but it has been shown to elevate heart rates by as much as 61%.


This is a rather hypothetical question, which may contribute to the worry around it. Candidates know you may examine their previous employer's references, so they may be mentally preparing to say something that squares with what their former boss is likely to say, which is easier said than done for those of us who can't read minds. It's also conceivable that they're job-hunting because they had a rocky connection with their previous supervisor for no fault of their own. However, they may feel embarrassed if they respond negatively for fear of how others will perceive them.


Asking applicants "What was the best working relationship you've had with a manager and why did it work so well?" is a comparable but less weighty inquiry. The hiring manager may acquire valuable information about the employee's values and behavioral tendencies via the employee's favorable interactions.


5. “Why are you looking to leave your current employment?”


A candidate's pulse rate increases by 51% after being asked why they want to quit their present employment.

This is perhaps another question with hidden meanings. People look for employment for a wide variety of reasons, including economic downturn-related layoffs, career-path changes, and negative work environments. Applicants have to tread a fine line between coming off as negative and seeming flaky for wanting to go or throwaway for being unemployed.


You'll get more out of this line of inquiry if you focus on the future rather than the past. Find out what they expect from their future boss, workgroup, and position. Candidates will frequently rely on both good and bad experiences when thinking about what they want, providing you with a more complete picture of their background and aspirations.


Final thoughts


Rather than coming across as an interrogation, the finest interviews have more of a conversational tone. You can get more out of every interview and speed up the process of finding the best person for the position if you put the applicants at ease and steer clear of questions that might backfire.


It is important to get comments from all interviewees, not just those who are chosen for employment, in order to have a more complete picture of how your process may be improved. whether you want to know whether applicants had a positive experience with your firm and what you may do in order to make the experience even greater for the next prospect, all you have to do is send out a brief survey and promise complete anonymity.

blogs.blogTitle: How to Network Like a Pro In 5 Steps
blogs.blogSummary: How to Network Like a Pro In 5 Steps: Both recruitment and networking require you to contact strangers, evaluate them for potential, and make awkward small...
blogs.blogDescp:
Both recruitment and networking require you to contact strangers, evaluate them for potential, and make awkward small conversations with them in a professional setting. And for recruiters, it's not just about building that connection; how you make people feel has a direct effect on your ability to hire them, the number of people who come to work for you via recommendations, and the reputation your organization has as an employer.


Fortunately, famous venture investor and skilled networker Jobcart offers some tips for making meaningful connections and a lasting impression. His networking credo is encapsulated in the maxim, "Be human to everyone you meet."


Jobcart was profiled in a viral piece on how to rapidly expand one's network of influential contacts. We have condensed the information into the following five essential networking tactics for recruiters:


1. Be genuinely curious about the applicants, and they will like you more


Ideally, even if a candidate is rejected, they leave with a favorable impression.


Jobcart suggests expressing gratitude in these ways to create a memorable experience: Put off an aura of friendliness and vitality. People are more likely to like you if they perceive that you like them.


That requires recruiters to take an authentic, human interest in each candidate they meet. If you dig deep enough, you'll discover that even the individuals you least like have redeeming qualities.


Be aware of these qualities and don't be shy about praising potential hires. You should inquire about their work experience and qualifications, but you should also show genuine interest in and genuine respect for their interests, pets, literature, and vices. (Just be wary about asking any private or inappropriate questions).


2. Actively listen; even a casual "mm-hmm" can go a long way toward making candidates feel like they're being heard


Chris believes that the key to excellent listening is demonstrating that you comprehend what the other person is saying and asking follow-up questions to get them talking.


This may be broken down into "backchanneling," which is responding enthusiastically to the other person while they are speaking (by saying things like "yeah," "mm-hmm," "totally," and "I can see that") and then asking questions that build on the knowledge they have just provided.


Little things like these may have a big impact on the success of a recruiter's efforts. It takes more time and effort, but it's definitely worth it to ask questions that go right to the point.


If a candidate says they managed a team in another location, for instance, you may inquire about their experiences leading remote teams. Referential inquiries like those may disclose user information, such as sophisticated talents or flashes of personality, in addition to improving the applicant experience.


3. Put the applicant at ease by acting modestly, particularly if you are in a position of authority or are rejecting them


"an unspoken distinction... between the Hunters and the Hunted," Jobcart adds, referring to the unequal power dynamic present in most networking situations, in which one person has more to give but also less time and interest in the other.


Generally speaking, that difference is easy to make while recruiting: You should be modest at an interview since you are the one in a position of power. Being modest might help you stand out from the crowd while competing with all-star candidates.

Jobcart argues that recognizing one's own humanity and flaws is an effective strategy to build rapport with others. When someone is in a position of authority over you and seeking your opinion, attention, or assistance, it's important to put them at ease.


It's common for job seekers to feel nervous before an interview. It's not a guarantee that they won't be a good worker, but it certainly complicates the hiring process. They may relax and be themselves if you use "humility markers" like making a brief joke about an error you made or admitting that you are wrong occasionally.


When you need to turn down a large number of applicants, you learn humility. Chris suggests making a phone call or scheduling a face-to-face meeting before politely declining: It may not be practical for a recruiter to meet with every one of the several dozen prospects they will be rejected, but a phone call is always an option. A rejection "stands out among people's interactions," Jobcart adds. They will appreciate and respect you more if you take the effort to be kind and human.


4. Tell the candidates the truth, but only if it would help them


The truth will always come out, whether you are trying to hide how difficult a job may be or the true reason you are rejecting someone.


Being completely truthful is difficult, and it's usually not a good idea. Honesty is the best policy since it helps candidates in the long run, whether by establishing reasonable expectations or providing constructive feedback for future performance. The best way to stand out, according to Jobcart, is to be completely truthful. Always base your openness on what the other person may really benefit from.


Constructive honesty will set you apart and aid the candidate in the long run, whether it's discussing an unpleasant component of the job or pointing out that they misspelled the term "dynamic" a dozen times in their résumé.


5. You may use "no need to respond" to make applicants more interested in responding to your message


You wouldn't want to be the person who sends a potential employee an InMail and then tells them not to reply. You really want to hear anything, even if it's a negative answer. It may go against your better judgment to advise potential applicants to remain silent.


This piece of advice from Jobcart is part reverse psychology and half Jedi mind trick:


"If your first line of contact with someone is, 'Can I take you to lunch?' that's a huge ask for a lot of people, as opposed to, 'Hey, just thought I'd share with you this little update on what I'm doing. There's no need to reply. The 'no need to reply' may be a very effective tactic. Giving individuals a way out makes them more inclined to take action, which goes against common sense.

Telling a prospect they don't need to react sets you apart from every other recruiter out there; it's a breath of fresh air that shows you respect their time and are a kind, considerate person.


Strangely enough, this might make the applicant more receptive to your arguments, or at least give you a cursory "thanks, but no thanks." Even better, if you send an InMail, the recipient may simply say "no" and you won't lose any points. According to Jobcart, "it's always better to get a quick 'no,'" so long as it's honest. To paraphrase: "It's better to get that 'no' upfront than to get strung along waiting for a 'yes' that never comes."


There are several steps in the hiring process beyond informal networking, such as the search for qualified candidates, pay negotiations, and formal interviews. However, there is much knowledge that is applicable in either setting. Chris's advice is ultimately about displaying greater humanity in the workplace, which is something from which we can all benefit.

blogs.blogTitle: 7 Key Elements of an Effective Hiring Process
blogs.blogSummary: Effective Hiring Process- Many methods appear to be effective. However, there are some essential parts to the process that must be present, especially in team...
blogs.blogDescp:
Assuming that we have all of the information, we can make the crucial hiring choice. How should we proceed ideally?


There is no universally applicable method of making decisions that work for every organization or position. Many methods appear to be effective. However, there are some essential parts to the process that must be present, especially in team-based organizations. Our top picks are as follows:


1. Create a centralized aggregator or set up "blind" inputs in your application tracking system.

Interviewers should not be able to see the ratings or remarks of their colleagues, since this might lead to groupthink.


2. Provide the team with a rating rubric and remind them to use it

What does a score of 3 signify on a crucial outcome or competence vs a score of 2? Make sure everyone understands the same thing so that applicants aren't given preferential treatment (or treated unfairly) depending on who is on the interview panel.


3. Be sure to exclusively ask interviewees about their area of expertise when requesting ratings and real data points (interview-based facts).

Do not put interviewers in the awkward position of having to make a hiring choice solely on a single meeting.


Before the meeting, distribute the complete set of ratings and data, including averages for each focus area and the whole.

Make sure the people you're interviewing have read it and are ready to talk about it.


5. Choose someone to act as the conversation's moderator

If you are the recruiting manager, it is advisable to choose someone who is not part of the interview panel. The facilitator's job is to keep the discourse on the topic by providing relevant facts, encouraging diverse participation, and gently but firmly pointing out instances of prejudice.


6. Arrange the contestants from highest average rating to lowest

The top applicants need your whole attention throughout the meeting, so give them that and (if applicable) an offer as quickly as possible. 


7. Take a cautious approach

If the facts do not objectively indicate a good match, and you find yourself "talking yourself into" a candidate nevertheless, be honest with yourself and others. Don't try to change the rules after the fact (by, for instance, revising the job description to account for a near-miss). far if job openings hurt, terrible hires hurt far more.


Let the hiring begin!
